title: Designing Research
one: Data never answers your question by itself — you can have perfectly good data, run a perfectly correct analysis, and still end up with a precise answer to a question you never asked.
text: Data never answers your question by itself — you can have perfectly good data, run a perfectly correct analysis, and still end up with a precise answer to a question you never asked. Causal inference is figuring out, from data, whether one thing causes another — usually when you can't run the experiment that would settle it. Things go wrong in two places: the data can be bad, or the data can be fine and your design still answers the wrong question. The second failure is the dangerous one, because nothing about it looks like a mistake. The numbers are correct. A research design is what connects your question to your analysis, so that the number you compute is the number you wanted. The rest of the series: pick a good question → describe data → identify the right analysis → learn the specific tools. You look at the world, you get a question — does this drug cure the disease, does this policy reduce crime. The tempting move is to find data that's about your topic, run something on it, and treat the output as the answer. That move fails constantly, and it fails quietly. Sometimes the data is simply bad: wrong source, unreliable, poorly measured, inconsistent, or about something adjacent to what you care about. That failure is at least visible if you look. The other failure isn't. You can have data you fully trust, do the analysis competently, and land on a real answer to a different question — and because every step was defensible, you'll believe it. The car insurance ad Plain English. Every car insurance company advertises that people who switch to them save about $500 a year. All of them. They can't all be the cheapest — so what are those ads actually measuring? Precisely. The advertised statistic is the mean saving among people who chose to switch . Switching is voluntary, so a person only switches when this company happens to quote them less than they currently pay. The sample is selected on the outcome. The number is an honest average over a group defined by having saved money. Anchor image. A gym posts a sign: "members who joined lost 15 pounds on average." It's true. It's also true at every gym on the street — because the only people who finish the year as members are the ones it was working for. The sign measures who stayed, not what the gym does. Worked example. Two questions you might think the ad answers: Question Who's in the comparison What you'd learn Which company is cheapest? Everyone , priced at every company Which company actually has the lowest rates What do switchers save? Only people who switched An average over a self selected group Same data on prices. Different design. Only the second is what the ad computed, and only the first is what you had in your head when you watched it. Where it breaks. Nothing here is a data problem or a math problem. The company plausibly has real, well measured data on its switchers, and the average is correctly calculated. The failure is entirely in the gap between the question and the design — which is exactly the failure no amount of better data or better statistics will catch. Two ways to get a wrong answer Plain English. Bad data, or good data used to answer something other than what you asked. Precisely. Failure one is a measurement and sourcing problem — the numbers don't faithfully represent the thing. Failure two is an identification problem — the numbers are faithful, but the comparison you built out of them isolates a different quantity than the one your question names. Anchor image. A telescope doesn't create stars, it decides which light reaches your eye. Bad data is a dirty lens. A bad design is a perfectly clean lens pointed at the wrong part of the sky — and you will describe what you see with total confidence. Where it breaks. You can't fix a design problem with more data. A bigger sample of switchers gives you a more precise estimate of switcher savings, and moves you no closer at all to knowing which company is cheapest. What a research design is for Plain English. A research design is the plan that makes your analysis answer your question instead of some neighbouring question. Precisely. Given the same dataset, different questions demand different analyses. The design is the reasoning that picks the analysis matching the question — and lets you say what your number means once you have it. Worked example. The series is organised around exactly this chain: dag node Q 0 0 "Question" treat node D 1 0 "Describe" node I 2 0 "Identify" node A 3 0 "Answer" out edge Q D edge D I edge I A note 1.5 0.85 "the design is what makes this arrow chain hold" You start with a research question, learn to describe variables and relationships in data, then work out which description actually identifies the answer — and the rest of the book is that last step, done properly, situation by situation. This data is about car insurance prices, and my question is about car insurance prices, so this data answers my question → Actually, topic overlap is not the same as answering. The data has to support the specific comparison your question implies. The analysis was done correctly, so the conclusion is correct → Actually, correctness of the arithmetic says nothing about whether the arithmetic was aimed at your question. If the result is misleading, someone made an error → Actually, these gaps open up without anyone being wrong at any step. Nick's read on the insurance ads is that they aren't making a mistake at all — the design does what they want. You've seen those car insurance ads — "switch to us and save five hundred dollars a year." Every company runs that ad. They obviously can't all be the cheapest, so what's going on? Here's the trick. They only surveyed the people who switched to them . And think about who does that: you only switch if this company quoted you less than you're already paying. So the group they measured is, by definition, the group that saved money. Everyone who got quoted a higher price just walked away and never showed up in th

title: Research Questions
one: You care about *why* the world works the way it does, but data can only show you *what* happened — so a research question is the bridge you build from a why to an observable what and back again, and most research fails because that bridge d
text: You care about why the world works the way it does, but data can only show you what happened — so a research question is the bridge you build from a why to an observable what and back again, and most research fails because that bridge doesn't actually hold. Research starts with a theory — a claim about why something is the way it is. Data can't see why. It only reports what. So you need a what you can observe that genuinely informs your why . The test of a good question: imagine every result you could get, and ask whether each would change your mind. If you'd explain away the inconvenient one, your question isn't testing your theory. Then check the practical filters: is it feasible , is it affordable at scale , does a setting exist where the design works? And keep it small . One study answers one narrow question, not "why are some countries rich?" You don't do research because you want a number. You do it because you have an idea about how the world fits together and you want to know if it's right. But the thing you can measure is never the idea itself — it's some observable trace of it. The gap between those two is where research goes wrong, and it goes wrong before any data is collected . Get too deep in before checking, and you finish the analysis only to realise the number you produced never had the power to change your mind about the theory in the first place. Everything here is quantitative empirical research. The advice doesn't transfer to questions that aren't answered with data. Theory is "why", data is "what" Plain English. Your theory says because . Your data says this is what we saw . Data is very good at what and very bad at why. Precisely. A theory is any claim containing an explanation — a why, a because, a mechanism. A research question names something observable that, if you saw it, would update your belief in that theory. The job is: go from the why to a what you can measure, then reason from that what back to the why. Anchor image. You want to know whether the neighbour is home. You can't see inside. You can see whether the lights are on. "Lights" isn't "home" — but if you picked the right observable and you know how lights and people relate, the lights genuinely tell you something. Pick the wrong observable and you're staring at a timer switch, learning nothing. Worked example. Theory: being exposed to lots of different cultures makes you smarter — one reason some people are more intelligent than others. The obvious what: look in data, check whether intelligence and cultural exposure move together. They do. Does that inform the why? dag node C 0 0 "Cultural exposure" treat node I 2 0 "Intelligence" out edge C I "the theory" edge I C curve "just as consistent with the data" No — because the correlation is equally consistent with smarter people choosing more cultural exposure. The what you picked can't distinguish the two, so it can't move your belief. Now change the what: randomly assign some people to cultural exposure and follow up on their intelligence later. Same two variables, same kind of relationship measured — but now the arrow from intelligence back to exposure is broken by the randomisation, so the result actually bears on the theory. Where it breaks. Note what changed. Not the theory, not the variables, not the statistic. Only the question , and with it the design. Imagine the results before you have them Plain English. Before running anything, picture each result you might get and ask honestly: would that change what I believe? Precisely. Take every plausible outcome, including the ones that contradict you, and write down your reaction to each. If a contradicting result would make you start generating excuses, then a confirming result shouldn't have moved you either. That asymmetry is the tell: the question isn't connected to the theory. Anchor image. A bet you can't lose is a bet that isn't measuring anything. If the coin comes up tails and you say "well, that one didn't count", you weren't betting. Worked example. Correlational version: you find exposure and intelligence are negatively related. Your reaction: "maybe smart people are too busy to travel", or "maybe cultural exposure was measured badly". You've explained it away. So the positive result you were hoping for wouldn't have been evidence either. Experimental version: you randomly assigned people to more cultural exposure, and they came out less intelligent. Much harder to wriggle out of. That's the point — you want a question where the data can genuinely shoot your theory down. Where it breaks. This is a check on the question , and it only works if you do it before you see results. Afterwards you'll know which outcome you got, and you can't un know it. The three practical filters Plain English. Even a question that would settle your theory is useless if you can't actually run it. Precisely. Filter The question to ask Failure Feasibility Does the data exist, and would anyone let me do this? Nobody will let you randomly assign the minimum wage across states. Scale Do I have the money, the time, the people? The answer needs every home transaction: hundreds of thousands of dollars and a year of several people's time. Design Does a setting exist where my approach applies? Your plan needs students randomly assigned to a global studies course. Did that ever happen anywhere? Anchor image. A recipe you can't source the ingredients for isn't a dinner plan, however good it would have tasted. Where it breaks. Failing a filter doesn't kill your theory — it kills that question . You go looking for a different observable what that reaches the same why. Keep it small Plain English. One study answers one small, well defined question. It does not explain the world. Precisely. Big questions — why are some countries rich, what's the best form of government — can't be resolved by a single study. The common failure is the "determinants of X" paper: thirty predictors of school dropout, none of them identified, and no w

title: Types of Variables
one: There are five kinds of variables — continuous, count, ordinal, categorical, and qualitative — and which kind you have decides which descriptions and calculations are even legal on it.
text: There are five kinds of variables — continuous, count, ordinal, categorical, and qualitative — and which kind you have decides which descriptions and calculations are even legal on it. A variable is one measurement taken repeatedly across observations: flower height, recorded once per flower. Describing a variable means telling someone what's in it without making them read every row. Five types: continuous (any value in a range), count (whole, non negative), ordinal (ordered, no meaningful spacing), categorical (unordered groups; binary is the two option case), qualitative (too complex for maths). The type isn't a label — it's a constraint. You can't average ordinal data, because there's no such thing as the average of "middle school" and "college." The book works with the first four. Qualitative data has to be coded into one of them before you can use it. You have thousands of flower heights in a spreadsheet. Nobody is going to read them. Your job is to say what that variable looks like — what's typical, how much it varies, whether there are lots of short ones and tall ones or whether they're all much the same. But you can't pick a description until you know what kind of thing you're describing. The right summary for "how tall is this flower" is not the right summary for "what's this student's major", and applying one to the other produces numbers that look fine and mean nothing. Continuous Plain English. It can be any value in a range, and there's no "next" value up. Precisely. Between any two values there's always another, given enough measurement precision. The apparent granularity is a limit of your ruler, not of the variable. Anchor image. A dimmer switch, not a light switch. There's no click stop between settings. Worked example. Flower height. It isn't that one flower is 10 inches and the next is 11 — measure precisely enough and it's 10.132765 inches and 11.12321 inches. The flowers aren't one step apart. One is just slightly taller. Count Plain English. How many of something there are. Precisely. Whole numbers only, and normally can't go below zero — you can't count something a negative number of times. Runs from 0 up to arbitrarily large. Anchor image. Eggs in a carton. There are eleven or twelve. There is no 11.4. Worked example. How many flowers are in the field today. Where it breaks. The whole number floor at zero matters when you describe or model it — a method that happily predicts −2.3 flowers is telling you something is off. Ordinal Plain English. You can put the values in order, but you can't say how far apart they are. Precisely. A ranking without a scale. You may write numbers on the categories, but those numbers don't carry distance, so arithmetic on them is meaningless. Anchor image. Finishing positions in a race. Second beat third, and third beat fourth — but that tells you nothing about whether second was a hair ahead or a lap ahead. Worked example. Education: elementary → middle school → high school → college → PhD. Everyone agrees middle school is more than elementary, and high school is more than middle school. But high school isn't "one more" than middle school and middle school isn't "0.5 more" than elementary. So what would the average of elementary, high school, and college even be? There's no answer, because there's no scale to average on. Where it breaks. The mistake is coding it 1–5 and treating those as real numbers. The order survives that coding; the spacing you just invented does not. Categorical (and binary) Plain English. Mutually exclusive groups with no order at all. Precisely. Every observation belongs to exactly one category, and no category is "more" than another. Binary is the special case with exactly two options. Anchor image. Which platform your train leaves from. Platform 4 isn't more than platform 2. They're just different places. Worked example. College majors — engineering, biology, English. Engineering isn't more major than English. Compare with education level, which does have an order: same domain, different variable type. Binary: are you left handed or not — two options, in or out. The unrestricted categorical version of the same idea might be left handed / right handed / ambidextrous / no hands. Where it breaks. Categorical and binary variables are everywhere in social science, because so many of the comparisons we care about are between groups. That makes them the ones you'll most often be tempted to hand to a method expecting numbers. Qualitative Plain English. Too rich and complex to be represented mathematically at all. Precisely. Not covered in the book. To use it quantitatively you must first code it — convert it into one of the other four types — and you're then analysing the coding, not the original. Anchor image. A song. You can record its length in seconds, its key, its genre — but none of those is the song. Worked example. The text of a newspaper article. Word count is a count variable you derived from it. Tagging it "inflation" and "military" makes categorical variables. The meaning of the article — what it actually says — is the qualitative part, and to get at that you read it. Ordinal data has numbers on it, so I can average it → Actually, the numbers are labels for an order, not measurements of a distance. The average of elementary, high school, and college doesn't exist. Ordinal and categorical are basically the same thing → Actually, ordinal has an order and categorical doesn't. Education is ordinal; college major is categorical. Same subject area, different type, different legal operations. Binary is its own separate type → Actually, binary is just a categorical variable with two options. Height looks continuous but my data only has whole inches, so it's a count → Actually, the type is about the underlying quantity, not your measurement precision. Height is continuous, coarsely measured. Before you describe any variable you have to know what kind of thing it is, because the type decides what you're even allowed

title: Summarizing Variables
one: Every variable has a **distribution** — how often each value occurs — and summarizing a variable means compressing that distribution down to a central tendency and a spread, choosing either the mean family or the percentile family depending
text: Every variable has a distribution — how often each value occurs — and summarizing a variable means compressing that distribution down to a central tendency and a spread, choosing either the mean family or the percentile family depending on how much you want outliers to count. A distribution tells you how often each possible value occurs. A fair coin: 50% heads, 50% tails. That's the whole distribution. For a categorical variable, listing the proportion in each category is the complete distribution. You're done. For a continuous variable you can't list values, so you bin them into a histogram , then shrink the bins to nothing and get a density . Even a density is a lot of information, so you compress further: a central tendency plus a spread . Two families. Mean based : mean + standard deviation. Percentile based : median + interquartile range. Percentiles resist outliers; means are easier to work with mathematically. Skew is spread that's lopsided. Taking a logarithm pulls the long tail in. You have thousands of observations and you want to tell someone what's in them. You can't hand them the dataset — nobody can hold thousands of numbers in their head. So you compress, and the whole question is how to compress without throwing away the part that mattered. Each step down this ladder loses information on purpose: full distribution → density → central tendency + spread. Knowing what you dropped at each rung is what stops the summary from lying. Distribution Plain English. How often each value comes up. Precisely. A distribution maps every possible outcome to how frequently it occurs. For a fair coin that's heads 50%, tails 50% — and that is the complete description of the random variable. Nothing is left over. Anchor image. Not the list of every coin flip you made, but the rule the flips obey. Worked example. Rather than "I flipped a thousand coins; the first was heads, the second tails, the third tails…", you say: I flipped a thousand coins and 502 came up heads. One number replaces a thousand and loses nothing you cared about. Categorical variables are easy — just list the proportions Plain English. Say how often each category occurred. That's it, you've fully described it. Precisely. For a categorical variable the sample distribution is exactly the set of category proportions. There's no further compression to do because there was no detail lost. Worked example. A dataset of colleges, by the kind of degree they award: Degree awarded Share of sample Less than two year 47.1% Two year 22.2% Four year or more 30.7% Three numbers, and you now know everything about that variable in that sample. Continuous variables: histogram, then density Plain English. You can't list every value, so group them into bins and count. Then imagine the bins getting infinitely narrow. Precisely. A histogram turns a continuous variable into an ordered categorical one by binning — incomes 0–5,000, 5,000–10,000, and so on — and counts observations per bin. Shrink the bins toward zero width and you get a density , which describes the probability of landing near a point rather than the count inside a bin. Anchor image. Sorting coins into jars by size. Wide jars, coarse picture. Narrow jars, finer picture. Infinitely many infinitely narrow jars, and the tops of the jars trace a smooth curve. Worked example. Earnings of students after graduating from those colleges. The histogram shows most students attended a college where typical post graduation earnings land between $20,000 and $40,000. The density sharpens it: the peak sits around $30,000–$35,000. A few colleges have cohorts averaging over $100,000 — visible, but the curve is low there, so there's little area beneath it. Same at the bottom around $12,000: it happens, rarely. Where it breaks. Why the value can't be read straight off a density: what you're reading is a density, not a probability. The numbers differ from probabilities even though the idea — "how likely is roughly this?" — is the same. Central tendency: mean vs median Plain English. One number standing in for the middle of the distribution. Two ways to pick it, and they answer different questions. Precisely. The mean — add all values, divide by how many — is the value you'd break even betting on. A percentile is the value with that share of the sample below it: line 100 people up shortest to tallest, and if the fifth is 4'10", then 4'10" is the 5th percentile. The median is the 50th percentile — whoever stands in the middle. Anchor image. The mean is where the seesaw balances. The median is the person standing in the middle of the queue. Move one person to the far end of the field and the seesaw tips hard; the queue barely notices. Worked example. A hundred people lined up by income, the middle one earning $30,000. Jeff Bezos walks in. The median shifts from that person to whoever was standing next to them — maybe $31,000. The mean jumps to something absurd once his income is averaged in. Where it breaks. Neither is more correct — they serve different purposes. The mean gives you the typical value you'd get back from the distribution. The median gives you the typical observation you'd run into. Percentiles resist outliers but are harder to work with mathematically, which is why means dominate in practice. Spread: standard deviation vs interquartile range Plain English. How much things scatter around the centre. Two distributions can share a centre and be nothing alike. Precisely. The mean family measure is the standard deviation : subtract the mean from each value, square the differences, average the squares (that's the variance ), then take the square root to get back to the original units. The percentile family measure is the interquartile range : the distance from the 25th to the 75th percentile. Anchor image. Two archers both average dead centre. One's arrows are in a fist sized cluster; the other's are scattered across the whole target. The average says they're identical. The spread is the entire d

title: Theoretical Distributions
one: You only ever get one sample, but you can reason about the **theoretical distribution** — how your answer would vary if you could resample the world over and over — and then use your single sample to rule out which versions of the truth are
text: You only ever get one sample, but you can reason about the theoretical distribution — how your answer would vary if you could resample the world over and over — and then use your single sample to rule out which versions of the truth are implausible. The goal of all of this is to learn about the world from your data . You have one small window; the world is the whole building. If you resampled repeatedly you'd get a different answer every time. The distribution of those answers is the sampling distribution , or theoretical distribution . You can often write it down from theory: coin flips are binomial , averages tend toward normal , counts are Poisson , and so on. Its centre contains the thing you actually want — the true average, or the true effect of X on Y. With one sample you can't pin down the truth. You can narrow the range by ruling out truths under which your data would be very unlikely. The probability of getting your result or something more extreme, assuming a given theoretical distribution, is the p value . You want to know how the world works. Inconveniently, you don't have the world. You have a dataset — one window into it. And here's the infuriating part: statistics doesn't hand you one answer. Go back and collect a different sample and you get a different number. Again, a different one. So the question can't be "what's the answer?" It has to be "given that my answer would jump around from sample to sample, what can this particular answer tell me about the truth underneath?" That reframing is what theoretical distributions are for. If you can say in advance how your answer should bounce around, then the one bounce you observed becomes evidence. The theoretical distribution is the distribution of answers, not of data points Plain English. Not "how are the values in my data spread out" but "how would my result be spread out if I redid the whole study over and over." Precisely. The theoretical (sampling) distribution describes how a statistic varies from sample to sample, given some assumed truth about the world. Its parameters — its true mean, its true standard deviation — are properties of the world, not of your dataset. Anchor image. One dart in the board tells you little. But if you know the shape of the scatter a given thrower produces, one dart tells you a lot about where they were aiming. Worked example. A fair coin, flipped twice. From theory alone — the coin is fair, and coin flips follow a binomial distribution — you can state the whole distribution before collecting anything: Outcome How often One head, one tail (either order) 50% Two heads 25% Two tails 25% That's what you'd see if you flipped two coins here, then two coins over there, then two more, and plotted all the results. Where it breaks. This is entirely conditional on your assumptions about the world. It tells you how data would be distributed if the coin were fair. Change the assumption and you get a different theoretical distribution. Different situations, different distributions Plain English. Which theoretical distribution applies depends on what kind of thing you're measuring. Precisely. A handful you'll meet: binomial for a series of yes/no trials like coin flips; normal for averages; log normal for a variable that's normal after taking logs; Poisson for counts of how often something happens; gamma , and many more. A statistics textbook has the full zoo. Anchor image. Different tools leave different tool marks. If you know the tool, you know the mark to expect. Worked example. Why averages go normal: measure the average height in this classroom, then that classroom, then another, and plot all those averages. You get something normal shaped. Describing the distribution of averages is one of the things the normal distribution does, which is why it turns up constantly — most of what we compute is some kind of average. The centre of the theoretical distribution is the thing you want Plain English. The reason to care about this distribution is that the truth you're after is sitting at the middle of it. Precisely. The central tendency of the theoretical distribution is the true population parameter. Learn about the distribution and you've learned about the parameter. Worked example. For classroom heights, the centre of the theoretical distribution is the true overall average classroom height. Same structure applies to the true effect of X on Y — which is why this machinery carries straight into causal inference. Narrowing the range of plausible truths Plain English. You can't work out which theoretical distribution is the true one. You can throw out the ones that make your data look absurd. Precisely. Consider all the candidate theoretical distributions — one for each possible truth. Your single sample can't select among them, but it can render some of them very unlikely. That's inference: not pinning down the truth, but narrowing the range you consider reasonable. Anchor image. You can't identify a stranger from a footprint. You can rule out everyone with a different shoe size. Worked example. Sampling average classroom heights for ten year olds and getting 3'7". Could the truth be 4'0"? Sure, plausibly. Could it be 6'0"? You'd have to have been extraordinarily unlucky. So you discard the theoretical distribution centred on 6'0" — not because you've proven it false, but because your data would have been wildly improbable under it. Where it breaks. Two schools of thought on how to do this formally. Bayesians treat data far from expectation as reason to update their beliefs toward it. Frequentists run a hypothesis test: if the data would be sufficiently unlikely under a candidate truth, reject it. Nick covers the frequentist route because you'll see it more often, but rates the Bayesian approach as well worth learning — he points to Andrew Gelman, and Regression and Other Stories . p values, worked all the way through Plain English. Assume a truth. Ask how often data as extreme as yours would show up if tha

title: Conditional Distributions
one: Two variables are related exactly when learning the value of one **changes the distribution** of the other — so describing a relationship is nothing more than describing how one variable's distribution shifts as you move across values of an
text: Two variables are related exactly when learning the value of one changes the distribution of the other — so describing a relationship is nothing more than describing how one variable's distribution shifts as you move across values of another. Conditioning means holding one variable at a particular value and asking what the other variable's distribution looks like there. If the conditional distribution changes as you move across values, the variables are related . If it doesn't change at all, they're independent . This works for every kind of variable: categorical distributions shift, density curves shift. Since you already know how to summarize a distribution, you get conditional means — and conditional medians, standard deviations, skews, whatever you like — for free. Conditioning on a continuous variable is awkward, because almost nobody shares an exact value. Binning is the crude fix; line fitting is the good one, next episode. None of this is causal yet. It's description only. Causal inference is about the effect of one variable on another — which means, before anything else, it's about the relationship between two variables. You can't ask which part of a relationship is causal until you can say what the relationship is. And the good news is you already have the machinery. Everything from the last three episodes — distributions, densities, means, medians, spread — carries over untouched. You just apply it one slice at a time. Conditioning Plain English. Hold one variable fixed at some value, and look at what the other variable does there . Precisely. To condition on a variable is to restrict attention to observations at a particular value of it, and examine the distribution of a second variable within that restriction. That's a conditional distribution . Anchor image. A deck of cards face down: any card is equally likely. Someone tells you it's a red card. You haven't seen the card — but the distribution over what it could be just collapsed to half of what it was. That collapse is conditioning. Worked example. The distribution of babies' sex is roughly 50/50. You're told you'll meet a stranger; your best guess about their sex is 50/50. Now someone mentions the stranger is named Sarah. Your distribution shifts hard — maybe 95% female, maybe higher. Conditional on the name being Sarah, the distribution of sex is nothing like 50/50. That shift is the entire definition. Learning about one variable told you something about another. That is what it means for two variables to be related. Independence is the case where nothing shifts Plain English. If learning one variable's value leaves the other's distribution exactly as it was, they're unrelated. Precisely. Two variables are independent when conditioning on one produces no change in the distribution of the other. Worked example. A six sided die: each face has a 1/6 chance. You roll and get a 1. What does that tell you about the next roll? Nothing. It's still 1/6 for every face. The conditional distribution equals the unconditional one, so the rolls are independent. Contrast: names and sex. "Sarah" gives you one distribution, "Chris" gives you a different one. Different conditional distributions across values means the variables are related. It works the same for every variable type Plain English. Categorical or continuous, you're doing the identical thing — look at the distribution, then look at it again inside each slice. Worked example. Vitamin E supplements. Vitamin E wasn't recommended, then was recommended by health professionals for a while, then wasn't again. That history makes it interesting: while it was being recommended, the people who took it up were plausibly the people who follow health advice in general — so vitamin E would look related to all sorts of healthy outcomes, when really it's healthy people choosing to take it. This is the setup behind a study by Emily Oster. Start with the unconditional distribution: about 15% of people take vitamin E , 85% don't. Now condition on smoking. Among smokers, the share taking vitamin E is lower than among non smokers. Two different conditional distributions — one for smokers, one for non smokers — so vitamin E use is related to smoking. Now the continuous version: instead of whether you took vitamin E, look at how much , which is continuous, and condition on whether you exercised vigorously in the last month. The density curve shifts a little left or right depending on which group you're in. Same conclusion, reached by comparing densities rather than proportions. Where it breaks. All of this is description. You've established that vitamin E use and smoking are statistically related. You have said nothing about which causes which, or whether either does. Conditional means (and everything else) Plain English. Once you have a distribution per slice, summarize each slice the way you'd summarize any distribution. Precisely. Apply any summary statistic within each conditional distribution and compare across slices. Conditional means are by far the most common — most of causal inference is comparing conditional means — but conditional medians, standard deviations, skews, and interquartile ranges are all equally available. Anchor image. You already own the measuring tape. Conditioning just means measuring each room instead of the whole house. Worked example. "Is the average amount of vitamin E people take different depending on whether they exercise vigorously?" is a comparison of two conditional means. Useful detail: for a binary variable, the proportion is the mean — so "15% take vitamin E" is already a conditional mean when you compute it within a group. Conditioning on a continuous variable Plain English. You can't hold a continuous variable at an exact value, because essentially nobody shares one. Precisely. Conditioning on, say, exactly 0.1376 grams of vitamin E gives you a slice containing maybe one person, maybe none. The crude fix is to bin the continuous variable — the same trick t

title: Line-Fitting
one: A fitted line is a compressed conditional distribution: plug in a value of X, read the height of the line, and you have the conditional mean of Y there — and the whole choice is how much shape you let the data pick versus how much you impos
text: A fitted line is a compressed conditional distribution: plug in a value of X, read the height of the line, and you have the conditional mean of Y there — and the whole choice is how much shape you let the data pick versus how much you impose in exchange for a cleaner summary. Binning X and taking means inside bins works, but it's ad hoc — the bins are arbitrary. Fit a line instead. Its height above any X is the conditional mean of Y at that X. Non parametric approaches (like a LOESS curve) shrink bins to almost nothing and roll along X, letting the data choose the shape. Maximum flexibility. Ordinary least squares instead fixes the shape in advance — usually a straight line — and finds the best one of that shape. You lose detail. You gain an intercept and a slope , easy statistical properties, and less noise chasing. The slope is the payoff: a one unit increase in X is associated with a slope sized change in Y. Correlation is just that slope with the units stripped out. Last episode ended in an awkward place. To describe how the conditional mean of Y moves across values of a continuous X, you binned X. But where do the bins go? How many? The data doesn't say, and different choices tell different stories. There's also a plain communication problem. A conditional distribution at every value of X is an enormous amount of information. Nobody can hold eighteen thousand conditional means in their head. You need something someone can actually take away. A fitted line is a lookup table for conditional means Plain English. Draw a shape through the data. Pick any X, look at the line's height there — that's your predicted average Y. Precisely. The fitted line maps each value of X to the conditional mean of Y at that value. Reading the line across its whole range shows you how the conditional distribution of Y shifts as X changes — which is exactly the relationship you set out to describe. Anchor image. A conditional distribution at every X is the full sheet music. The fitted line is the melody you can hum. Non parametric: let the data pick the shape Plain English. Take the binning idea and make the bins tiny, sliding smoothly along X instead of chopping it into blocks. Precisely. Non parametric regression computes an average (or something slightly more sophisticated) of Y within a very narrow window around each X. A LOESS curve rolls that window along the X axis, producing a smooth curve without you specifying a functional form in advance. Anchor image. A pencil rubbing dragged along the data, taking on whatever shape is underneath it. Worked example. BMI against the proportion of people taking vitamin E. Instead of "BMI between 10 and 15", narrow to 11–12, or 11.1–11.2, and smooth. The LOESS curve shows: at low BMI, the share taking vitamin E rises quickly — going from BMI 20 to 22 buys you a lot more vitamin E takers. Then it flattens — from BMI 60 to 62, barely any change. Upward sloping overall, steep at first, shallow later. That's a real summary. You didn't have to memorise thousands of conditional means; the shape carries the message. Where it breaks. It's still visual. Reading the curve off a graph is easy; reporting it is not. Ask "how much does the expectation of Y change when X goes up by one?" and there's no single number to give. Ordinary least squares: impose the shape, gain a number Plain English. Decide up front what shape the relationship has — usually a straight line — then find the best line of that shape. Precisely. Ordinary least squares (linear regression) fits a pre specified functional form rather than letting the data choose. Committing to a shape costs detail and buys a summary in two numbers: an intercept and a slope . Anchor image. A tailored suit versus a bedsheet draped over someone. The sheet follows every contour; the suit is a deliberate simplification you can describe over the phone. Worked example. The same vitamin E and BMI data, fit as a straight line: proportion taking vitamin E = 0.110 + 0.002 × BMI Intercept 0.110. At BMI = 0 — if that were somehow possible — you'd predict 11% of people take vitamin E. Slope 0.002. This is the real payoff. For every one unit increase in BMI, the conditional mean of vitamin E taking goes up by 0.002. The curve from LOESS is gone. In exchange you get a sentence anyone can repeat: a one unit increase in BMI is associated with a 0.002 increase in the proportion taking vitamin E. Where it breaks. You genuinely lost something — the steep then shallow structure is no longer visible. Whether that trade is worth it depends on whether the detail was real signal or noise. Why simplifying is often the right call Plain English. More flexibility isn't free. Past a point you start fitting the wiggles in your particular sample rather than anything real. Precisely. Two arguments for OLS beyond readability. First, it has convenient statistical properties — the sampling distribution of an OLS coefficient is easy to describe, which is harder for a LOESS curve or anything more flexible. Second, there's a trade off: enough detail to capture what's genuinely there, but not so much that your data can't support it. Highly detailed models get noisy. Anchor image. Tracing a coastline. Zoom in far enough and you're recording individual pebbles — technically more accurate, and useless for navigation. Where it breaks. Straight isn't compulsory. Linear regression can fit curved shapes too; that, along with using it to replace comparisons of means and ANOVAs (both special cases of regression), waits until Chapter 13. Slope and correlation Plain English. The sign of the slope tells you the direction. Strip the units off and you have a correlation. Precisely. A positive slope means the variables are positively related in a linear way; negative means negatively. A correlation is a rescaled regression slope — nothing more. The slope speaks in units (units of BMI, units of vitamin E); remove the units and you get a unitless measure of strength running from −1 to

title: Conditional Conditional Means a.k.a. Control Variables
one: Controlling for a variable means explaining away everything Z can account for in both X and Y, throwing that part out, and asking whether X and Y are *still* related in the leftover — the **residual** — which is how you answer "isn't that j
text: Controlling for a variable means explaining away everything Z can account for in both X and Y, throwing that part out, and asking whether X and Y are still related in the leftover — the residual — which is how you answer "isn't that just because of Z?" Any conditional mean splits an observation into an explained part and an unexplained part , the residual . The residual is the interesting bit: it's the variation that has nothing to do with the variable you conditioned on. Look for a relationship inside the residuals and you get a conditional conditional distribution — the relationship between X and Y that survives after Z is accounted for. That is exactly what "controlling for", "adjusting for", and "matching on" all mean. Mechanically: use any relationship tool to explain X and Y with Z, subtract, then look at what's left. Regression does it automatically — just add Z as a predictor, and the coefficient on X becomes the conditional conditional relationship. It's the standard reply to "isn't that just because of Z?" You find that people who wear shorts are much more likely to eat ice cream. You announce a causal effect: make people wear shorts and they'll eat more ice cream. Someone immediately says: no, it's just that both happen on hot days. They might be right. You need a way to check — a way to strip out everything temperature can explain about shorts wearing and ice cream eating, and see whether anything survives. That's what this episode builds, and it's the last piece of description before causal inference proper. Explained and unexplained: the residual Plain English. A conditional mean tells you what to expect . The residual is how far off the actual value is. Precisely. Given a conditional mean of Y at some value of X, each observation splits into the explained part (the conditional mean) and the residual (actual minus expected). The residual is the variation in Y that the conditioning variable cannot account for. Anchor image. Weighing a loaded truck and subtracting the truck's known empty weight. What's left is the cargo — and the cargo is the only part you were ever curious about. Worked example. Nick is a college professor, and he drinks coffee. Suppose professors average 2.3 cups a day . He actually drinks 3 . Explained: 2.3 cups. That's what knowing "professor" buys you. Residual: 0.7 cups. The gap between expected and actual. That 0.7 sounds like failure — you can't predict individuals from a group mean. But notice what it is : the part of his coffee drinking that has nothing to do with being a professor. Some other reason drives it. That leftover is the useful object. Conditional conditional: look for the relationship inside the residual Plain English. Take out what Z explains, then ask whether X and Y are still related in what's left. Precisely. A conditional conditional distribution is the conditional distribution of Y given X, computed within the variation remaining after conditioning on Z. If a relationship persists there, Z alone doesn't account for it. Anchor image. Someone claims the room is only bright because the lamp is on. Turn the lamp off. Still bright? Then something else is contributing. Worked example. Claim: brown haired people drink more coffee. Objection: brown haired people are more likely to be professors, and professors drink a lot of coffee. So go to the residual. Discard the 2.3 cups that "professor" explains and work only inside the 0.7. Within that leftover, is hair colour still related to coffee drinking? Yes → there's something about brown hair beyond the professor story. No → the objector was right; being a professor was the whole thing. That's all "controlling for" ever meant Plain English. Controlling for, adjusting for, matching on — same idea, different words. Precisely. Each names the operation of conditioning on a variable and then examining the relationship that remains within that already conditioned variation. Worked example. Shorts and ice cream. You observe they go together. The objection is that both happen on hot days. dag node T 1 0 "Hot day" node S 0 1 "Shorts" treat node I 2 1 "Ice cream" out edge T S edge T I edge S I "still there after controlling for T?" note 1 1.85 "control for temperature, then look at what's left" Split by hot days versus not — or use all the values of temperature — see what temperature explains about each of shorts and ice cream, and take the residuals. If shorts and ice cream are still related in the leftover, temperature isn't the whole story and there may be something causal. If the relationship vanishes, the objector was right. Where it breaks. "May be something causal" is the honest ceiling here. Surviving a control for temperature doesn't establish causation — it eliminates one alternative explanation. There are always others, which is what the identification chapters are for. How to actually do it Plain English. Explain what you can with Z, subtract it out of both variables, then look at the relationship in what's left. Or just add Z to your regression. Precisely. The manual version uses any relationship tool you already have — line fitting, means within bins. Explain X with Z and take the residual; explain Y with Z and take the residual; then look at the relationship between those two residuals. The easy version: instead of regressing Y on X, regress Y on X and Z . The coefficient on X is now the conditional conditional relationship — regression performs the subtraction for you. Anchor image. Two hills with a valley between them. Flatten both hills by the same amount and whatever separation remains was never about the hills. Worked example — the geometry. Picture Y, X, and Z. Z is positively related to X and positively related to Y, so part of the X–Y relationship is really Z operating through both. Flatten the Y–Z relationship. Flatten the X–Z relationship. Now Z is flat against both leftovers — it can't be driving anything — and whatever X–Y relationship survives is the part not due to Z. Work

title: Data Generating Processes
one: Behind your data sits a **data generating process** — the set of laws and regularities that made the data what it is, operating whether or not you know about them — and identification is the work of getting from what you observed back to th
text: Behind your data sits a data generating process — the set of laws and regularities that made the data what it is, operating whether or not you know about them — and identification is the work of getting from what you observed back to the part of that process you actually asked about. Identification is the link between the analysis you ran and the question you wanted answered. "These two variables are correlated, therefore one causes the other" is an identification error, not a statistics error. The data generating process (DGP) is the set of laws and relationships determining why you observed this data and not some other data. Empirical research means using observations to reverse engineer that process — the way planetary paths were used to work out physical laws nobody could see directly. The same observations are usually consistent with many different processes. Isolating yours means dealing with the others. And you must know the surrounding context, because data will lie to you if you don't. A lamp that isn't plugged in makes its own pull string look useless. You've now got both halves. Episodes 1–2 covered research questions; episodes 3–8 covered doing calculations on data. Identification is where they meet: making sure the calculation you did answers the question you asked, and not some neighbouring question. The classic failure is correlation and causation. You can analyse two variables, follow every statistical step correctly, and be entirely justified in concluding they're correlated. Take one more step to "therefore one causes the other" and you've made an error — but not in the statistics. The statistics were fine. The data generating process Plain English. The rules the world is running that produced your data. Precisely. The DGP is the set of laws and relationships determining why the data looks the way it does — why you observed these particular values rather than others. It exists and operates whether or not you know about it, and every research question is, underneath, a question about some piece of it. Anchor image. You can't see the machine, only what comes out of it. Your job is to work out the gears from the output. Worked example. Gravity. For a long time people watched planets and could see the paths they traced across the night sky. Nobody could see the physical laws — but those laws were running the whole time, producing exactly those paths. What scientists managed to do was work backwards from the observed paths to the underlying laws. That's the template for all empirical research. Observations in, process out. Many processes produce the same observations Plain English. The data you're looking at is consistent with several different stories, and the data alone can't tell you which one you're in. Precisely. Any given set of observations is typically compatible with multiple data generating processes. Identification is the work of isolating just the explanation your question is about. Anchor image. Wet pavement. Rain? A street cleaner? A burst pipe? The pavement looks the same in every case. Worked example. A pen. Two observations, both things you can genuinely measure: 1. Nick is holding the pen, and it's up here. 2. He lets go, and it's down there. Two variables: whether he's holding it, and where it is. Now — you cannot see gravity. It's part of the laws that make the world look the way it does, and it is not in your data. So suppose you don't know about gravity yet, and you're trying to infer the process from these two observations. Take that data to a room of sceptics and you'll get: Maybe there's a big earth down there pulling on it — gravity. Maybe pens just really love the ground. Maybe he's secretly throwing it, adding momentum on release. Maybe there's a wind you can't see, pushing it down. Every one of those fits the data perfectly. And the point isn't that the sceptics are right — it's that your two observations, on their own, don't discriminate between them. Where it breaks. The thing you most want to measure is frequently the thing you cannot observe. Gravity isn't a column in the dataset. Neither is ability, or health consciousness, or demand. Data lies when you don't know the context Plain English. If you don't understand the rest of the machine, a perfectly accurate observation can point you in exactly the wrong direction. Precisely. Interpreting data requires knowing enough of the surrounding DGP to recognise which conditions have to hold for a relationship to show up at all. Miss one, and a real effect can look like nothing. Anchor image. Turning the tap and getting no water, then concluding the tap is broken. The mains are off. Worked example. Nick wants to know how a lamp works — what's the process making the light on or off? He collects data: pull the little string. The light does not go on. Not knowing how lamps work, you'd sit there scratching your head, and you might reasonably conclude that the string does nothing. The actual reason is that the lamp isn't plugged in. The data was accurate. The string genuinely didn't turn on the light. And the conclusion it pushed you toward — "this string is irrelevant" — is dead wrong, because you didn't know that lamps need electricity before you can learn what the string does. Where it breaks. No statistical method rescues you here. This is a knowledge of context problem, and the fix is learning your setting properly before trusting what the data appears to say. Correlation to causation is a statistical mistake → Actually, it's an identification mistake. The finding of correlation can be entirely correct; the error is in what you claim it answers. If my data is accurate, my conclusion is at least pointed the right way → Actually, the unplugged lamp gives you completely accurate data supporting a completely wrong conclusion. The data generating process is something I build, like a model → Actually, it's out there running whether or not you know about it. Your model is an attempt to describe it. With eno

title: Alternative Explanations
one: You can never see causation in data — only that two variables are related — so identification works by **listing every other reason** they might be related and killing those reasons one at a time, until what survives can only be the effect
text: You can never see causation in data — only that two variables are related — so identification works by listing every other reason they might be related and killing those reasons one at a time, until what survives can only be the effect you're after. Causality here means: if you reached in and changed X, would Y change as a result? Data doesn't show you that. It shows you conditional distributions — that Y's average differs across values of X. So the working question becomes: what are all the reasons these two could be related? Then isolate the one you want. Randomization is the cleanest tool, because assignment by coin flip can't be caused by anything else in the system. Conditioning on a variable is another, and later episodes add many more. You've identified the effect when every alternative explanation is either ruled out or removed, and a relationship still remains. The alternatives don't disprove your effect. They just have to be cleared away before you can see it. The DGP is out there, and any set of observations is consistent with lots of different ones. That's a problem, because the thing you can actually see — that X and Y move together — is exactly the thing that every one of those processes predicts. So identification can't work by staring harder at the relationship. It has to work by elimination. Enumerate the other stories, dispose of them, and see what's left standing. What data shows you versus what you want Plain English. You want to know whether changing X changes Y. What you observe is that X and Y tend to go together. Precisely. The causal question is interventional: reach in, change the value of X, does Y change as a result? What data gives you is a conditional distribution — at different values of X you get different conditional means of Y. That, by itself, doesn't say why the relationship exists. Anchor image. Two people always leaving a building at the same time. You can see the co timing. You cannot see whether one is waiting for the other. The list of alternatives Plain English. Before you claim an effect, write down every other reason the relationship could show up. Precisely. Enumerating alternative explanations converts a vague worry into a checklist. Each entry is a data generating process that would produce your observed relationship without the causal effect you care about — and each one tells you something specific your analysis has to handle. Worked example. The classic econometrics question: does more schooling raise your earnings? In the data, people with more education do earn more on average. Reasons that could be true other than education causing earnings: People who were going to be better workers anyway choose to get more education. People who live in richer areas tend to get more education and earn more because of where they are. People with a natural aptitude for a highly paid field — you'd have made a good engineer regardless — think "engineering school sounds great" and go, so the schooling tags along with the aptitude. dag node A 1 0 "Ability / area / aptitude" unobs node E 0 1 "Education" treat node W 2 1 "Earnings" out edge A E edge A W edge E W "the effect you want" note 1 1.85 "each alternative is another arrow you must deal with" Where it breaks. Crucially, none of this means education doesn't raise earnings. The effect may be entirely real. It just means it's buried among other things, and you have to dig through them to pull out the part you want. Randomization removes alternatives by construction Plain English. If a coin decided who got the treatment, then nothing about the people could have decided it. Precisely. Explicit randomization severs the link between treatment assignment and everything else. Whatever else varies across people cannot have influenced who got treated, because a random device did. That's the entire purpose of randomizing. Anchor image. Dealing cards from a shuffled deck rather than letting players pick. Nobody can claim the good hands went to the people who wanted them. Worked example. Suppose you could randomly assign how much education people get. Now revisit the alternatives: could people who'd have earned more anyway have chosen more education? No — that never happened in your data. The only reason anyone got more education is that you assigned it, and you assigned it randomly, which has nothing to do with what their earnings would have been. The alternative is gone, not adjusted for. Where it breaks. Randomization is one route, not the only one. Conditioning on a variable — the conditional conditional distributions of Ep 8 — closes off alternatives too, and much of the rest of the series is other methods for doing the same job when you can't randomize. Worked all the way through: the lamp Plain English. Take a relationship you're sure about, and see how many alternatives still need killing. Worked example. Does pulling the string cause the lamp to turn on? Collect data: pull, light on. Pull, off. Pull, on again. Strong relationship. Identification means not taking that at face value. So: Alternative 1 — the pulls are timed. Maybe Nick isn't causing anything; maybe he's timing his pulls to moments the light was going to change anyway, using some other knowledge about the lamp. That would produce this exact data with no causal effect at all. How to kill it: hand the decision to a coin. Heads, pull; tails, don't. Flip — heads, pull, light changes. Flip again — heads, pull, light changes. Now the timing wasn't Nick's choice, it was the coin's, and the coin has no knowledge of the lamp. The relationship survives, which is evidence the string is doing the work. Alternative 2 — wrong mechanism. Maybe the string controls all the electricity in the house. The string genuinely causes the lamp to change, but by cutting power to everything, which is a different process than the one you claimed. How to kill it: pull the string and check whether other things in the house switch on and off at the same time. They don't. Alt

title: Alcohol and Mortality
one: "One drink a day is good for you" comes from comparing one-drink-a-day people to non-drinkers — but non-drinkers include people too sick to drink and recovering alcoholics, and once you remove them the benefit disappears, which is identific
text: "One drink a day is good for you" comes from comparing one drink a day people to non drinkers — but non drinkers include people too sick to drink and recovering alcoholics, and once you remove them the benefit disappears, which is identification happening in public on a real question. When many studies on one topic reach wildly different conclusions, it can mean they're not answering the same question — the analyses differ even though the intent doesn't. The finding: people who have one drink a day rate healthier than people who have zero. The identification problem: who has zero drinks a day? People too sick to drink, and ex alcoholics whose past heavy drinking still affects them. Wood et al. drop those people and re ask. The apparent benefit of one drink a day goes away . They also adjust for background factors — sex, race, age — since those drive both drinking and mortality. A placebo test shows they still haven't got everything: the same method implies drinking more causes you to become a man. There is always another alternative explanation. The question is whether you've cleared enough of them. You've seen the headlines. A glass of wine a day is good for you. No, it's bad for you. No, alcohol is fine. No, alcohol is the worst thing imaginable and one sip will kill you. Sometimes — not always — a pile of contradictory studies on the same topic is a signal that the analyses aren't answering the intended question. They come out different because they're really asking different questions, even when everyone meant to ask the same one. This episode takes one such question and walks the identification through: here's the naive comparison, here's what's wrong with it, here's what fixing it does, and here's how you can tell you still haven't fixed everything. The claim and the comparison behind it Plain English. People who have one drink a day look healthier than people who have none. Therefore one drink a day is good for you. Precisely. In the data, health ratings of people having one drink a day are higher than those of people having zero — measured by cardiovascular health, fitness, how long they live. The tempting conclusion is a positive causal effect of one drink a day on health. The identification question: does a comparison of zero drinkers to one drink a day people actually answer does drinking one drink a day rather than zero make you healthier? Probably not. Who are the zero drinkers? Plain English. People with zero drinks a day are not just "the same people, minus the drink." Precisely. The zero drinks group systematically contains people who are different for reasons that themselves affect health: Too sick to drink. Their health rating is low, and their poor health is what causes them not to drink. The arrow runs backwards from the one you assumed. Ex alcoholics. Someone who drank very heavily for a long period will still be carrying the health consequences, even having stopped completely. Anchor image. Comparing people who take no medication to people who take a little, and concluding a little medication makes you well. The people on zero include both the perfectly healthy and the too far gone. dag node H 1 0 "Poor health" bad node D 0 1 "Zero drinks" treat node M 2 1 "Mortality" out edge H D "too sick to drink" edge H M note 1 1.75 "the zero drinks group is selected on ill health" Where it breaks. The comparison isn't meaningless — it might be an interesting result in its own right. It simply doesn't answer the question that was asked. Remove the alternative, re ask the question Plain English. Take those people out of the zero group and run it again. Precisely. Wood et al. attempt to identify the effect by removing that alternative explanation: from the zero drinks a day group, drop the people who are too sick to drink, the ex alcoholics, and similar cases. Then ask again whether more drinking is associated with better health at low levels. Worked example. Their result, plotted as drinks per week against a hazard ratio — higher meaning less healthy, more likely to die sooner: For the first few drinks per week : not much effect. The apparent benefit of light drinking is gone. Past roughly 100 grams of alcohol per week : the hazard ratio starts climbing. That's one alternative explanation accounted for, and it took the original finding with it. The relationship people were reading as "light drinking is good for you" did not identify the question of interest. One down, many to go Plain English. Drinking correlates with a lot of things, and most of them also correlate with dying. Precisely. The decision to drink is related to a whole range of background factors that are themselves related to mortality. Men tend to drink more and tend to die sooner, largely for reasons other than drinking. The same goes for class, race, location, and region. So the study adjusts for a number of these — racial background among them — so that if one of those is driving the remaining relationship, it's been conditioned away. Worked example. After all that, the shape holds: little relationship at low levels of alcohol, then a fairly rapid climb in the negative health effects past a threshold. Where it breaks. They can't account for everything. There will always be some factor they didn't measure or didn't control for — short of an extremely careful job of removing every alternative explanation, which is very hard to do. Given what they controlled for, you could argue things remain. The placebo test Plain English. Run your method on a relationship you know should be zero. If it isn't zero, your method is picking up something it shouldn't. Precisely. A placebo test examines a relationship that must be zero if all your assumptions hold. A non zero result means something has been left out of the analysis. Anchor image. Weighing an empty scale. If it reads 200 grams, you don't have a heavy nothing — you have a broken scale, and every measurement you took with it is suspect. Worked example. Same graph, same

title: Causality
one: X causes Y if **reaching in and changing X would change the probability distribution of Y** — which means causality is about intervention, not about two things happening together, and it's a claim about distributions rather than about what
text: X causes Y if reaching in and changing X would change the probability distribution of Y — which means causality is about intervention, not about two things happening together, and it's a claim about distributions rather than about what actually happened. Causal diagrams , developed by Judea Pearl, are a way of writing a data generating process down. Before drawing one you need to be precise about what an arrow means. The working definition: intervene on X, and the distribution of Y changes as a result. This is not the same as a conditional relationship. Names and sex are related; renaming someone doesn't change their sex. "A cause" is not "the only cause". Opening your hand causes the pen to drop, alongside gravity and there being nothing underneath. It's about distributions , not events. A wet floor nobody slips on still caused slipping — it raised the probability. The tell in language is direction . "X causes Y" ≠ "Y causes X", but "X is related to Y" = "Y is related to X". You've got identification as a goal and the data generating process as the thing you're trying to recover. What you don't have is a way to write a DGP down so you can work with it. Causal diagrams are that notation. They're lists of variables with arrows between them, and each arrow asserts that one variable causes another. So before drawing a single one, you'd better be able to say exactly what you're asserting when you draw an arrow. Worth noting up front: the definition of causality varies by field. A physicist means something different by it and would object to what follows. This is the definition that works when you're inferring causal relationships from statistical data. Causality is intervention Plain English. If you could reach in and change X, would Y change as a result? Precisely. X causes Y when intervening to change the value of X changes the probability distribution of Y. The intervention is the whole point — it's what distinguishes causality from any amount of things merely co occurring. Anchor image. A light switch versus a rooster. Both reliably precede the arrival of light. Flip one and the light comes on; silence the other and the sun still rises. Worked example. From Ep 6: the proportion of people who are women is much higher among people named Susan than among people named Chris. That's a genuine conditional relationship — learning the name shifts the distribution of sex. Now intervene. Convince someone named Susan to change their name to Denise. Does their probability of being a man or a woman shift? No. You changed the name and nothing happened to the other distribution. So: related, yes. Causal, no. Where it breaks. The two variables really are related, and that relationship is real and useful — it's just not the thing an arrow asserts. A cause, not the cause Plain English. Saying X causes Y doesn't claim X is the only thing that matters. Precisely. A variable can have many causes at once. Being one of them is enough to draw the arrow. Anchor image. A recipe. Flour caused the cake. So did the eggs, the oven, and someone turning it on. Worked example. Nick holds a pen, opens his hand, and the pen drops. Two variables: whether the hand is open or closed, and where the pen is. Intervene on the hand and the pen's position changes — causal. But opening the hand wasn't the whole story. There was also nothing underneath to stop it, and the earth was down there pulling it. Many factors caused the drop. That doesn't make the hand not a cause; it makes it a partial cause , and that's what the arrow claims. Distributions, not events Plain English. Something can cause an outcome that never actually occurred. Precisely. The definition is about changing the distribution of a variable, not about producing a particular realisation. Raise the probability of Y and you've caused Y, in this sense, whether or not Y happened. Anchor image. Loading extra faces of a die with sixes. You caused the sixes even on a roll that came up two. Worked example. A lawyer thinks about causality in terms of things that actually happened. There's a wet spot in front of Nick's store. You walk past and think, someone could really slip on that. Nobody does. You can't sue — no event occurred that he could have caused. From a causal inference point of view, though: making the floor wet changed the distribution of people falling. It raised the probability. So he is a cause of slipping even though nobody slipped. Second worked example. You give someone a scholarship they'd never have had, so they go to college. They graduate and then do exactly what they would have done anyway. Nothing observable changed. But sending them to college changed the distribution of occupations available to them — it raised the probability they'd become a lawyer, even though they didn't. So the scholarship caused their occupation, even though they ended up in the same place. Where it breaks. The reverse case too: causes don't have to work every time. Flipping a light switch causes the lamp to come on. Sometimes the bulb is burned out or the lamp isn't plugged in and nothing happens. We'd still say switches cause lamps to light — it changes the distribution, even when it doesn't change the outcome you observe. Direction is the tell in language Plain English. Causal words point one way. Non causal words read the same in both directions. Precisely. To say two variables are related without implying causality: associated, correlated, related, tend to occur together, tend not to occur together, go together. To assert causality: X causes Y, X affects Y, the effect of X on Y, X increases or decreases Y, X changes Y, X leads to Y, X determines Y. Anchor image. "Next to" versus "pushed". Two things can be next to each other in either order. Only one of them did the pushing. Worked example. "X causes Y" and "Y causes X" are different statements. "X is related to Y" and "Y is related to X" are the same statement. That asymmetry is the signature of a causal claim. Where it

title: Causal Diagrams
one: A causal diagram is nothing more than a list of the variables relevant to your question plus arrows showing which causes which — and its value is that once it's drawn you can *see* the pathways connecting your two variables, sort them into
text: A causal diagram is nothing more than a list of the variables relevant to your question plus arrows showing which causes which — and its value is that once it's drawn you can see the pathways connecting your two variables, sort them into the ones you want and the ones you don't, and go get rid of the rest. A causal diagram is a graph: variables as nodes, arrows meaning "this causes that". Literally that's all it is. The variable is hair color , not brown hair . Brown is one of its values. Arrows come from theory , not from the data. One variable can cause many; one variable can be caused by many. Both are fine. Include variables whether or not you can measure them — unmeasured causes still cause things. Grey them out, or use a placeholder like U1 . The payoff: a diagram shows you pathways . Some answer your question, some don't. Identification is keeping the first kind and killing the second. You know you need the data generating process, and you know what an arrow means. Now you need to actually write one down. Once it's on paper something useful happens that couldn't happen before: you stop seeing "X and Y are related" as one undifferentiated fact and start seeing the specific routes by which they're related. Some of those routes are your research question. Some are the alternative explanations from Ep 10. Now they're distinguishable objects you can point at. What a causal diagram is Plain English. Dots for variables, arrows for causes. Done. Precisely. A causal diagram is a set of variables relevant to what you're studying, plus arrows linking them, each arrow meaning that one variable causes the other in the Ep 12 sense. There's plenty of complexity hidden underneath — what counts as a variable, how to analyse the diagram, how to know you've got the right one — but the object itself is that simple. Variables, precisely Plain English. A node is the thing being measured , not one of its answers. Precisely. A variable is a measurement taken across observations, which takes different values for different observations. The node is the measurement. Anchor image. The node is the question on the form. The values are the boxes you could tick. Worked example. Take a thousand people and record hair colour: brown, blonde, red, black. Hair color is the variable. Brown hair is not a variable — it's one value the variable can take. Getting this backwards will wreck a diagram. For "does hair colour affect labour market performance?", the relevant variables might be hair colour, earnings, intelligence, get up and go, network connections, parents' social standing. Earnings takes values like 30,000, 40,000, 100,000. Building one up: the cake Plain English. Think about your setting, list what varies, ask what determines what. Worked example. You have cake in the office kitchen. Brad comes in and offers to flip a coin for it. Step 1 — two variables. The coin flip (heads or tails) and who has the cake (Brad or you). The flip determines who gets the cake, so: dag node C 0 0 "Coin flip" treat node K 1.4 0 "Cake" out edge C K That's a complete causal diagram. Step 2 — one cause, two effects. Say the winner gets the cake but the loser keeps the coin. Now the flip determines your money too. One variable causing two things is fine: dag node C 0 0.5 "Coin flip" treat node K 1.5 0 "Cake" out node M 1.5 1 "Money" edge C K edge C M Step 3 — one effect, two causes. Your friend Terry is sometimes in the room. If Terry is there, they see the flip, and if you lost your coin they feel bad and give you one. So money now has two causes — also fine: dag node C 0 0.5 "Coin flip" treat node K 1.5 0 "Cake" out node M 1.5 1 "Money" node T 0 1.5 "Terry in room" node D 1.5 2 "Terry's mood" unobs edge C K edge C M edge T M edge D T note 1.5 2.7 "dashed = can't be measured, still on the diagram" The rule that generates arrows: anything that changes the distribution of a variable should be drawn as a cause of it. The coin flip changes the distribution of your money, therefore coin flip → money. Unmeasured variables go on the diagram anyway Plain English. Draw the causes that are really there, not the ones you happen to have data for. Precisely. The diagram represents the underlying laws producing the data. Whether you can measure a variable is irrelevant to whether it belongs. Mark unmeasurable ones — grey them out — so a reader knows, but include them. Anchor image.
