A map that only shows roads you've personally driven is not a map. Worked example. Whether Terry is in the room might depend on Terry's mood that day. You can't measure Terry's mood. It goes on the diagram anyway, greyed out, because it genuinely causes Terry's presence. Sometimes you know an unmeasured cause exists but can't name it. Use a placeholder — U1 — for "some unknown factor causing both of these": dag node U 1 0 "U1" unobs node S 0 1 "Shorts" treat node I 2 1 "Ice cream" out edge U S edge U I note 1 1.75 "U1 makes shorts and ice cream related with no arrow between them" Whatever U1 is — maybe temperature — when it's high or low it induces a relationship between shorts and ice cream, even though there's no arrow directly between them. Where it breaks. This matters enormously for identification. The alternative explanations from Ep 10 show up whether or not you can measure them. Leave them off the diagram and you won't see the problem; you'll just get the wrong answer. The payoff: pathways Plain English. A diagram lets you trace the specific routes connecting your two variables, and decide which ones you actually asked about. Worked example. Does police presence reduce crime? A simplified diagram — you could reasonably object that poverty is missing: dag node L 0 0 "Lagged crime" node P 0 1 "Police per capita" treat node E 1.3 1.7 "Expected crime payout" node R 2.6 1 "Crime" out edge L P edge L R edge P R edge P E edge E R Now read the routes from police to crime: Pathway What it means Want it? Police → Crime Police directly stop a crime in progress Yes Police

title: Drawing Causal Diagrams
one: Drawing a causal diagram is two steps — list the variables that explain why you'd see your data, then use prior knowledge to decide which causes which — and the variables you must not miss are the ones that cause **both** your treatment and
text: Drawing a causal diagram is two steps — list the variables that explain why you'd see your data, then use prior knowledge to decide which causes which — and the variables you must not miss are the ones that cause both your treatment and your outcome, plus anything at all that causes the treatment. Nobody hands you a diagram. You have to draw your own. Step 1: list the variables. Anything that helps explain why you'd see the data you see — measured or not. Step 2: draw the arrows. This is where prior knowledge and prior research come in. You cannot draw a diagram while refusing to assume anything. Highest priority: variables causing both treatment and outcome. Second: anything causing the treatment , because how well you understand why people got treated determines how well you can do causal inference at all. Don't include everything. In social science the list of faintly relevant things is infinite. Trivial causes can be left off. Variables cause each other too, not just the treatment and outcome. Your first attempt will be an absolute mess . That's normal, and simplifying it is the next episode. You know what a causal diagram is and what an arrow means. But being handed one is not a thing that happens — if you want to use this to identify an effect, you have to be able to build one from your own understanding of a setting. The honest framing: you're trying to write down what you think the data generating process looks like. You observed some relationship between a treatment and an outcome. What set of underlying laws would produce that relationship, in that direction, at that strength? Step 1: list the variables Plain English. Write down everything that helps explain why the data looks the way it does. Precisely. Enumerate the measurements or concepts that plausibly bear on your data generating process. Some will be measured variables you can get hold of. Others will be abstract concepts you can't measure — include them anyway. Worked example. Nick's own study: does taking online courses affect your chances of staying in college? Maybe it changes how much you learn, or your affiliation with the college. Two variables to start with — the ones the question names: Treatment: was the class online or face to face? Outcome: did you stay in school or drop out? Then ask what determines each of those: Variable Why it's there Preferences about education Someone drawn to online courses takes more of them. Unmeasurable — include it anyway. Demographics — age, race, gender Related to preferences, and to dropout rates Socioeconomic status Same Schedule freedom Determines what kind of class you can take Available time / work hours Work all day and face to face classes are impossible — they run during the day Internet access No connection, no online course The two categories you must not miss Plain English. Anything causing both treatment and outcome. And anything causing the treatment. Precisely. Variables that cause both are the ones that will produce a relationship between treatment and outcome that isn't your effect. Separately, and more broadly, anything that causes the treatment matters enormously, because a great deal of causal inference comes down to how well you understand why different people got different levels of treatment . Anchor image. Before you can say the medicine worked, you have to know who got it and why. If you don't know how the pills were handed out, nothing you observe afterwards is interpretable. Worked example. Work hours, in the online courses study. Someone working a lot: is more likely to want an online course, so it doesn't clash with the job, and is more likely to drop out — it's too much time on top of work, or they got a promotion and no longer need the degree. Related to both. That one absolutely has to be on the diagram. Where it breaks. The difficulty in causal inference is usually that you don't fully understand why some people got treated and others didn't. The less you know about that, the more back door trouble you invite in — which is exactly what the next chapter is about. Don't put the whole world on it Plain English. You could keep going until you'd drawn all of society. Don't. Precisely. Something being technically a cause doesn't earn it a place. If a variable has only a very minor effect or relationship, you can generally leave it off. In the social sciences the list of things that might be slightly relevant is effectively infinite, and trying to include all of it is a trap. Worked example. Whether you live near a quiet café might affect whether you take an online course — somewhere pleasant to work. For a handful of people, that's genuinely true. It's not a major determinant for most. Leave it off. Where it breaks. Judgement call, and you can get it wrong in both directions. Ep 15 gives you a way to check. Step 2: draw the arrows Plain English. Go pair by pair. Does this cause that? Why do you think so? Which direction? Precisely. This is where you lean on prior knowledge — things you've learned from previous research, or things you simply know about how the world works. It's essentially impossible to draw a causal diagram while refusing to make assumptions or stand on any existing knowledge. You can't learn more without staking something you already believe. Worked example — the reasoning, one arrow at a time. Almost everything → dropout. Demographic background characteristics all relate to dropping out; rates differ across those groups. Not necessarily causing dropout, but related somehow, which you might shorthand as an arrow. Internet access → online classes, but not dropout. Bad internet and you won't take an online course. Little direct effect on whether you drop out. Variables cause each other, too. Socioeconomic status → preferences: how you were raised shaped your feelings about online courses. Socioeconomic status → internet access: grow up with money and a decent connection is probably available. That last chain is a theory Nick holds about t

title: Simplifying Causal Diagrams
one: Four moves turn an unusable diagram into a working one — drop the **unimportant**, bucket the **redundant**, remove **mediators** you're not studying, and cut the **irrelevant** — and the skill being trained is figuring out what you're allo
text: Four moves turn an unusable diagram into a working one — drop the unimportant , bucket the redundant , remove mediators you're not studying, and cut the irrelevant — and the skill being trained is figuring out what you're allowed to ignore, because refusing to ignore anything means never learning anything. The world is complex and your diagram should reflect it — but a diagram with everything on it is a diagram you can't use. Unimportance: drop trivial causes. Statistics models the world, it doesn't reproduce it, and every assumption you make is at least a little false. Redundancy: variables whose arrows go to and from the same places play the same role. Bucket them — race, gender, SES → demographics . Mediators: the variable explaining why X causes Y. If you're not studying the mechanism, remove it and draw X → Y directly. Irrelevance: a variable that isn't on any pathway between treatment and outcome can go, even though it's genuinely part of the DGP. Don't oversimplify. Placebo tests catch you: if the simplified diagram implies a correlation is zero and you know it isn't, you cut too much. You ended the last episode with a mess, and there's an obvious objection to cleaning it up: the world is complex, so shouldn't the diagram be? Yes, genuinely — and you have to be careful not to simplify into something that no longer represents reality. But there's a counter argument. Sometimes the diagram is a mess because you're being hard on yourself, admitting every conceivable form of complexity. The simplifications below are the kind that help you see the setting and design a study — they clarify rather than cheat. Some of them don't reduce the complexity at all; they just put it in a labelled bucket so you can see what the actual problem is. 1 · Unimportance Plain English. Trivial causes come off. Precisely. There are infinitely many things related in some obscure way to your treatment or outcome. If something is unimportant — relevant occasionally, a tiny bit — you can drop it without much worry, even though it technically causes something on your diagram. Anchor image. A map of a city that marks every drain cover is not a better map. Where it breaks. The justification matters: statistics is about modelling the world, not perfectly representing it. Almost every assumption you make is at least slightly false already. There will always be some gap between your model and a reality too complex to capture — so a little less is often a little more, if losing an unimportant distraction lets you see the important structure. 2 · Redundancy Plain English. If several variables do the same job on your diagram, treat them as one. Precisely. If a set of variables all have their arrows going into and out of the same places, then from the point of view of your research question they occupy the same role. Bucket them into a single node. Anchor image. Four people carrying one end of the same sofa. For the purpose of moving the sofa, they're one force. Worked example. In the online courses diagram: race, gender, and socioeconomic status all cause taking an online course, all cause dropping out, and don't do much else. Three variables collapse into one: dag node D 0 0 "demographics" node O 1.5 0 "Online course" treat node S 3 0 "Stay in school" out edge D O edge D S edge O S note 1.5 0.85 "race + gender + SES, bucketed" Where it breaks. Remember the bucket is a bucket. When you actually go to use the diagram, demographics is still three separate things and you'll need to handle it as such. This is a presentational simplification for thinking about identification. Note this doesn't cheat — it doesn't pretend the world is simple. It puts the problem variables in one place so you can see what the problem is. 3 · Remove mediators you aren't studying Plain English. A mediator is the reason X causes Y. If you're not studying the reason, cut it out and connect X to Y directly. Precisely. A mediator sits between two variables and explains the mechanism. Whether it stays depends entirely on your research question, not on the world. Anchor image. A recipe that says "bake at 180°C" rather than describing protein denaturation. Both are true; only one is what you're cooking from. Worked example — keep it or cut it. Price of cigarettes → lung cancer. Raise the price and fewer people smoke, so cancer rates fall: dag node P 0 0 "Cigarette price" treat node N 1.4 0 "Cigarettes smoked" node C 2.8 0 "Lung cancer" out edge P N edge N C Cigarettes smoked is the mediator. If your question is the effect of smoking on lung cancer , and you're only noting that smoking is also driven by price — keep it . It's your treatment. You don't remove a mediator just because it's a mediator. If your question is the effect of price on lung cancer — cut it . Draw price → cancer directly, and hold the reason in the back of your mind without studying it. Second worked example. Why would online schooling affect staying in school? Maybe it changes how affiliated you feel with the college — never set foot on campus and you don't really think of yourself as one of the Fighting Lions, so you don't come back. Or maybe it's a difference in education quality. Interesting questions. But if you're not able to study them, or you're humbly leaving them for another paper, and all they do on your diagram is sit between treatment and outcome — pop them out. Draw online classes → stay in school. 4 · Irrelevance Plain English. If a variable isn't on any pathway between treatment and outcome, it can go. Precisely. There's more nuance here, arriving in later videos, but the basic idea holds: a variable genuinely part of the data generating process may still have nothing to do with your research question. Worked example. A programme at the college is structured so students tend to reach the end of a four year degree still needing one more class — pushing them into an extra term. That causes plenty of dropout. It is a real determinant of your outcome and it belongs in the

title: Causal Pathways
chapterTitle: Causal Paths and Closing Back Doors
one: Every way you can walk across a diagram from treatment to outcome is a separate reason those two variables are related — so list all the paths, sort them into the ones your question wants and the ones it doesn't, and identification becomes
text: Every way you can walk across a diagram from treatment to outcome is a separate reason those two variables are related — so list all the paths, sort them into the ones your question wants and the ones it doesn't, and identification becomes the concrete job of shutting down the second kind. A causal diagram's real output is why two variables are related — every path is one reason. A path is any walk along the diagram from one variable to another. You may go backwards up an arrow . You may not visit a variable twice. Front door: all arrows point away from treatment, toward outcome. Back door: at least one arrow somewhere points back at the treatment. Good path: one you want to count for your question. Bad path: one you don't. Good usually means front door — but not always. Your question decides, not the diagram. Identification = close every bad path, leave every good path open. Then the only variation left is the variation you wanted. You can draw a diagram and simplify it. What is it actually for ? This: it tells you exactly why your treatment and outcome are related. Not vaguely — specifically, as an enumerable list. You already knew a raw correlation between treatment and outcome wouldn't give you the causal effect, because other reasons exist. The diagram turns "other reasons exist" into a list of routes you can point at and deal with one by one. What a path is Plain English. Any way you can walk from one variable to another across the diagram. Precisely. A path is a sequence of connected variables leading from a starting variable to an ending one. Two rules: 1. You can walk backwards up an arrow. If the arrow runs B → treatment, "treatment to B" is still a legal step. 2. No doubling back. You can't hit the same variable twice — so treatment → B → A → treatment → B → C → outcome isn't a path, because it returns to treatment. Anchor image. A street map where some streets are one way for traffic but you're on foot. You can walk down a one way street the wrong way; you just can't loop back through a junction you've already crossed. Worked example. On a diagram with treatment, outcome, and variables A, B, C — where B causes the treatment — all of these are paths from treatment to outcome: treatment → outcome treatment → A → outcome treatment → B → C → outcome treatment → A → B → C → outcome treatment → B → A → outcome Note the third one starts by walking against the arrow from B into treatment. Perfectly legal. Where it breaks. Each path is a separate reason the two variables are related. The direct arrow is only one entry on the list. Every path tells a story Plain English. For each path you can say out loud why it would make the two variables move together. Worked example. Does drinking wine affect your lifespan? A simplified diagram: dag node U 1.5 0 "U1" unobs node N 0.8 0.8 "Income" node H 2.2 0.8 "Health" node W 0 1.8 "Wine" treat node L 3 1.8 "Lifespan" out node D 1.5 2.7 "Drugs" edge U N edge U H edge N W edge N L edge H W edge H L edge W D edge D L edge W L Income and health affect both how much wine you drink and how long you live. Wine affects drugs — maybe wine substitutes for other drugs, maybe it's a complement and drinking makes you want them — and drugs affect lifespan. U1 is unobserved and relates income to health without sitting directly between them. Now read off the reasons wine and lifespan are related: Path The story Wine → Lifespan Wine directly affects how long you live Wine → Drugs → Lifespan Wine changes how many other drugs you take, which affects lifespan Wine ← Income → Lifespan Richer people can afford more wine, and live longer for unrelated reasons Wine ← Health → Lifespan Healthier people feel able to drink; if you're really sick you stay off alcohol — and better health means a longer life Wine ← Income ← U1 → Health → Lifespan Richer people drink more wine; income is related to background health; health affects lifespan Every one of those would produce a correlation between wine and lifespan. Only some are the effect of wine. Front doors and back doors Plain English. Front doors run forward from treatment. Back doors have an arrow pointing back at it. Precisely. A front door is a path where all arrows point away from the treatment and toward the outcome. A back door is a path with at least one arrow somewhere along it pointing back at the treatment. Anchor image. Front doors are the treatment's own influence flowing outward. Back doors are something upstream pushing the treatment around, and that something has its own route to the outcome. Worked example. In the wine diagram: Front doors: Wine → Lifespan, and Wine → Drugs → Lifespan. Back doors: the three paths that begin by walking backwards into wine, via income or health. Where it breaks. A back door means something other than random variation is driving your treatment. That's precisely why back doors are usually the alternative explanations you have to deal with. Good and bad paths — your question decides Plain English. A good path is one you want to count. That's a fact about your research question, not about the diagram. Precisely. Good paths are the ones that should count toward your answer; bad paths are everything else. Often good = all front doors. Not always. Anchor image. Front door versus back door is architecture. Good versus bad is which door you meant when you asked how people get in. Worked example — the same diagram, two different questions. Question A: what's the effect of wine on lifespan, all in? Then both front doors are good. If wine extends your life because it means you take fewer drugs — sure, that counts as an effect of wine. Question B: what's the effect of wine on lifespan, not counting its effect on drug taking? Now the only good path is Wine → Lifespan. Wine → Drugs → Lifespan is a front door that is a bad path , and you'd want it shut. Meanwhile Wine ← Income → Lifespan is bad under either question. Rich people drinking more wine and living longer for other reasons is not an

title: Closing Causal Pathways, and Collider Variables
chapterTitle: Causal Paths and Closing Back Doors
one: A path is **open** when every variable on it varies and **closed** when even one doesn't — so you close bad paths by removing variation, either by controlling for a variable or by picking a sample that doesn't vary in it; except that **coll
text: A path is open when every variable on it varies and closed when even one doesn't — so you close bad paths by removing variation, either by controlling for a variable or by picking a sample that doesn't vary in it; except that colliders invert the rule, sitting on paths that are already closed until you control for them and blow them open. Open path: every variable along it is allowed to vary in your sample. It can induce a relationship. Closed path: at least one variable on it has no variation. It can't do anything. Two ways to close: condition/control for a variable, or select a sample with no variation in it. Kill one variable and every path it sits on closes at once. Don't close good paths. Controlling for something your treatment caused is post treatment bias . A collider is a variable on a path with both arrows pointing at it . That path is pre closed — free of charge. Control for a collider and the path opens. You can do this by accident, just by choosing a sample. Collider is a property of a variable on a particular path , not a global label. You've got your paths sorted into good and bad. Identification means closing the bad ones — but nothing so far says what "closing" physically is or how to do it. And there's a trap waiting. The instinct that follows from "close bad paths" is control for everything you can . Two things on this page make that instinct actively dangerous. Open and closed Plain English. A path works only if everything along it is free to vary. Freeze any one variable and the whole path stops working. Precisely. A path is open when all variables on it are allowed to vary in your sample. It's closed when at least one variable on it has no variation. A closed path can no longer induce a relationship between the variables at its ends. Anchor image. A row of dominoes. Every one has to be free to fall. Glue any single domino down and nothing past it moves — the whole line is dead, no matter how many are still standing. Worked example. Wine ← Health → Lifespan. In ordinary data there are people who drink more and less, people healthier and less healthy, people who live longer and shorter. Variation everywhere, so the path is open — it's a live reason wine and lifespan correlate. Now suppose you either statistically adjust for health, or pick a sample where everyone is naturally healthy. Either way there's no variation in health left. You can no longer say "naturally healthy people drink more and live longer" — in your data there are no more and less healthy people. The path is closed . Where it breaks — and this is the useful part: killing health's variation closes every path health sits on, all at once. Wine ← Income ← U1 → Health → Lifespan shuts down too. The two ways to close a path Plain English. Adjust it away statistically, or choose data that doesn't have it. Precisely. 1. Conditioning / controlling. Measure the variable and adjust for it, making the data behave as though it doesn't vary. Take out the relationship between income and wine, and between income and lifespan, and income no longer influences either. 2. Sample selection. Choose a dataset with no variation in it — only healthy people, say. Where it breaks. Both work. Only the first is deliberate every time; the second can happen to you without your noticing, which is the collider trap below. Worked all the way through: wine and lifespan Plain English. Six paths. Two you want, four you don't. Two control variables close all four. Worked example. Walk out every path from wine to lifespan and you get six. Front doors (good) — leave open: 1. Wine → Lifespan 2. Wine → Drugs → Lifespan Back doors (bad) — must close: 3. Wine ← Income → Lifespan 4. Wine ← Income ← U1 → Health → Lifespan 5. Wine ← Health → Lifespan 6. Wine ← Health ← U1 → Income → Lifespan Control for income. Income sits on paths 3, 4, and 6 — all three close at once. What's left open: the two front doors, plus path 5. Control for health as well. Path 5 has only health on it, so health is the only thing that could close it — there's nothing else to control for. Now paths 3–6 are all shut. What remains: exactly the two front doors. The effect is identified. Don't control for everything Plain English. Drugs is obviously related to all this. Control for it and you destroy part of your answer. Precisely. Controlling for drugs would close Wine → Drugs → Lifespan — a front door you wanted open. You'd be removing part of the real effect. Controlling for something that is affected by your treatment is post treatment bias . Anchor image. Measuring how much a tap fills a bucket while clamping the pipe halfway along. The tap works fine; you disabled the route you were trying to measure. Where it breaks. The instinct "it's related, so I should control for it" is exactly wrong here. There are things you must not control for, and the diagram is what tells you which. Colliders Plain English. A variable that two things point into . Any path through it is already closed — until you control for it, at which point it opens. Precisely. A collider is a variable on a path where both arrows on that path point toward it . A path containing a collider is pre closed — it can't induce a relationship, and you don't have to do anything about it. But controlling for the collider opens it back up , and it becomes a problem. Critically: being a collider is a property of a variable on a particular path , not a fixed label. The same variable can be a collider on one path and not on another. Anchor image. A door that's locked by default. Leave it alone and nobody comes through. The only way to create a problem is to walk over and unlock it. Worked example. Does tutoring in high school raise intelligence? Say you even randomized the tutoring, so you'd think you're fine. dag node T 0 0 "HS tutoring" treat node I 2.4 0 "Intelligence" out node C 1.2 1.1 "Goes to college" bad edge T C edge I C note 1.2 1.9 "both arrows point at college collider path pre closed" Tutoring helps you get

title: Testing Causal Diagrams, and Placebo Tests
chapterTitle: Causal Paths and Closing Back Doors
one: All of this assumes your diagram is right — so test it: pick any two variables, close every path between them, and the relationship in the data should be **zero**; if it isn't, your diagram is missing something.
text: All of this assumes your diagram is right — so test it: pick any two variables, close every path between them, and the relationship in the data should be zero ; if it isn't, your diagram is missing something. Closing bad paths identifies your effect only if the diagram is correct . It's a set of assumptions, and modelling a complex world means you left something out. A placebo test takes your assumptions and derives a relationship that should be zero . A non zero result means you've missed something. Simple version: your ad this month can't have raised sales last month. If your design says it did, the design is broken. Every diagram is full of placebo tests. Pick any two variables, list every path, close them all — the observed relationship should be zero. Colliders do a lot of work here , because between non treatment variables many paths are pre closed and need no action. Failing isn't fatal. The goal was never 100% correct assumptions — it's assumptions close enough not to mess you up too much. Everything so far has one enormous condition attached. You list the paths, sort them, close the bad ones — and you've identified your effect if the diagram is a reasonable representation of the actual data generating process . That's hard. The world is very complex, you're modelling it, and you are definitely leaving out detail. So the natural question is whether there's any way to check — and thankfully there is, at least for finding out whether you got it particularly wrong. What a placebo test is Plain English. Use your own assumptions to predict something that must be zero. Then go look. Precisely. A placebo test takes the assumptions you've made — and writing down a causal diagram is a set of assumptions — and derives from them a relationship or effect that should be zero if those assumptions hold. Run your actual research design on it. A non zero answer means an assumption is wrong: something is missing, some control unaccounted for. Anchor image. Calibrating a scale by weighing nothing. If it doesn't read zero, every number it has ever given you is suspect. Worked example. Does a television advertisement increase your sales? You design a study — you'll control for a certain set of variables, and you believe that identifies the effect. Now check your own reasoning. If the assumptions hold, this month's advertising should improve next month's sales. It should have no effect whatsoever on last month's sales — that's not possible. So run the identical design, same controls, on last month's sales. If this month's advertising appears to have raised last month's sales, that effect should have been zero and isn't. You've missed something. Back to the drawing board. Every diagram is packed with placebo tests Plain English. Take any two variables at all, shut every route between them, and they should be unrelated. Precisely. Pick any two variables on the diagram — not just treatment and outcome. Write out every path between them. Close all of them. The relationship between those two variables in your data should now be zero, because paths aren't only causal effects — they're any reason two variables could be related. Remove all of them and nothing is left. If they're still related, there's a path you left off the diagram, or a variable you should have been conditioning on. Which means you don't yet know what to control for to identify your real effect. Anchor image. Seal every door and window in a room and the air should stop moving. A draught means a gap you haven't found. Worked example: income and drugs Plain English. In the wine diagram, pick two variables that aren't the treatment or the outcome and test the implication. Worked example. Take income and drugs from the Ep 16 wine diagram. dag node U 1.5 0 "U1" unobs node N 0.8 0.8 "Income" treat node H 2.2 0.8 "Health" node W 0 1.8 "Wine" node L 3 1.8 "Lifespan" bad node D 1.5 2.7 "Drugs" out edge U N edge U H edge N W edge N L edge H W edge H L edge W D edge D L edge W L note 1.5 3.5 "lifespan is a collider on every income drugs path through it" Step 1 — list the paths. Income → Wine → Drugs. Income ← U1 → Health → Wine → Drugs. Then a batch running through lifespan: Income → Lifespan ← Drugs, Income → Wine → Lifespan ← Drugs, and more. Step 2 — throw out the pre closed ones. Every path through lifespan has both arrows pointing at lifespan — income → lifespan, and drugs → lifespan. Lifespan is a collider on all of them, so they're pre closed. You don't need to worry about them, and you emphatically don't want to control for lifespan. Colliders come into play a lot once you look at pairs of variables that aren't the treatment and outcome. Step 3 — close what's left. The remaining paths both run through wine. So control for wine — look only at wine drinkers, or only at non drinkers, or condition on how much wine you drink statistically. Step 4 — the prediction. Having done that, the relationship between income and drugs should be zero . If it isn't, there's a route from income to drugs going through neither wine nor lifespan, and your diagram is incomplete. Where it breaks. Writing the paths out already tells you it's deficient. What the diagram claims is that the only reason your income relates to your drug taking is that wine affects drug taking. Obviously insufficient — there's a lot missing from the drug taking picture. You can predict this test fails before running it. What to do when you fail Plain English. Failing isn't a verdict, it's information. Sometimes you fix it, sometimes you note it and move on. Precisely. The goal in causal inference was never assumptions that are 100% correct — that's impossible. The goal is assumptions close enough to correct that they don't mess you up too much. So look at the size of what you found. If income and drugs are still related but only slightly, maybe that's acceptable: you're leaving a little out and it won't be a huge problem. Then do the reflection regardless: why would income and drugs be relat

title: Finding Front Doors
one: Instead of hunting down and closing every back door — which requires a diagram everyone agrees on *and* the ability to measure everything on it — find some source of random or random-ish **exogenous variation** driving your treatment, isola
text: Instead of hunting down and closing every back door — which requires a diagram everyone agrees on and the ability to measure everything on it — find some source of random or random ish exogenous variation driving your treatment, isolate the part of treatment it drives, and you've built yourself an experiment without running one. Closing back doors has three hard requirements: build the whole diagram, get others to agree it's right, and measure everything you need to control for. The alternative: pick out the front door directly rather than eliminating every back door. A randomized experiment does this. Randomize the treatment and no other factor can be driving it, so every back door is gone without measuring anything. Crucially, you then use only the randomized data — not everyone in the world. Can't randomize? Look for exogenous variation : something that drives treatment and has no back doors to the outcome . That's exactly the structure randomization creates. Sometimes someone else already randomized for you — the Vietnam draft lottery . You then isolate, statistically , the part of treatment driven by that variation. The last few chapters gave you a complete procedure: list the alternative explanations, draw them as paths, close the bad ones by controlling for things. It works. It's also very hard to actually pull off. Three problems, in order: 1. Constructing the entire causal diagram is difficult. 2. Even if you construct one, getting other people to agree it's the correct diagram — and therefore that you really do know the full set of back doors — is another matter. 3. Even if everyone agreed, can you measure everything you'd need to control for? Often not. Picking the right controls is easy once you have the diagram. The trouble is knowing the diagram is right and being able to measure what it demands. So it's worth having another route entirely. Grab the front door instead of closing every back door Plain English. You've got front door paths you want and back door paths you don't. Rather than shutting each unwanted one, pick out the wanted one directly. Precisely. Instead of enumerating and closing every back door from treatment to outcome, isolate the portion of the treatment–outcome relationship that runs through front doors. Anchor image. A noisy room. One approach is to find and silence every other conversation. The other is a directional microphone pointed at the one you want. Why randomized experiments work Plain English. A back door exists because something else is pushing your treatment around. Randomize the treatment and nothing else is. Precisely. A back door path exists because some factor drives both treatment and outcome, producing a relationship in the data that isn't the effect. Randomize the treatment and that factor is no longer driving it — so its route to the outcome no longer matters. All back doors close at once, and you didn't measure a thing. Worked example. Does wearing shorts make you eat more ice cream? In observed data, temperature drives both — on a hot day you wear shorts and you eat ice cream. Now randomly assign people to wear shorts. Shorts wearing is no longer determined by temperature. It doesn't matter that temperature still causes ice cream eating, because temperature has lost its grip on the treatment — at least in this data. Where it breaks — and this is easy to miss: you must then limit yourself to the randomized data . You wouldn't randomize the treatment and then analyse your entire dataset. You wouldn't randomize shorts wearing and then include both the people in your experiment and everyone outside it. You use the experiment. Experiments have their own problems, which the book doesn't dig into. But they do solve this particular identification problem cleanly. Exogenous variation: the generalisation Plain English. You don't need actual randomization. You need something that behaves like it. Precisely. Exogenous variation is variation that's essentially random with respect to your outcome. The requirement: something drives your treatment, and there are no back doors between that source and your outcome . Anchor image. A coin doesn't have to be your coin. It just has to be a coin. Worked example — why randomization satisfies this. In a randomized experiment, the only thing driving treatment is your randomization. Since you assigned it randomly, it's unrelated to the outcome in any other way. There's no back door from the coin flip to the outcome: dag node Z 0 0 "Coin flip" treat node X 1.5 0 "Treatment" node Y 3 0 "Outcome" out node U 2.25 1 "Everything else" unobs edge Z X edge X Y edge U X edge U Y note 1.5 1.8 "no back door runs from the coin flip to the outcome" Anything that mimics that structure works just as well. You don't have to have done the randomizing yourself. Someone else's randomization: the draft lottery Plain English. The world sometimes randomizes things for its own reasons, and you can use it. Worked example. A famous economics paper asks: does being in the military improve your later labour market prospects? It could go either way. Maybe the military teaches you valuable skills and makes you a more effective worker, so you earn more. Or maybe it takes you away from years you'd have spent building a civilian career, so it hurts your prospects afterwards. You can't randomize people into the military. Nobody would let you. But during the Vietnam War the United States had a draft, and the way it worked was that the order of birth dates was randomized . Born on June 2nd? That lands early or late in the draft order, decided at random, and whether you got drafted depended on where you fell. That's randomization — not done for research, but real. It pushes people into treatment, and your birth date should be largely unrelated to your later earnings. Maybe some minor things sneak in, and you can probably control for those. It doesn't have to be purely random; you just need to be able to close whatever other back doors exist. Is

title: Front Door Examples
one: Finding exogenous variation doesn't remove the requirement to close all back doors — it **moves** it, from "close every back door between treatment and outcome" to "close every back door between my source of variation and the outcome" — and
text: Finding exogenous variation doesn't remove the requirement to close all back doors — it moves it, from "close every back door between treatment and outcome" to "close every back door between my source of variation and the outcome" — and three real studies show that job getting steadily harder as the variation gets muddier. You haven't escaped the back door problem. You've relocated it — hopefully somewhere easier. Lottery winnings → bankruptcy. Among people who already bought tickets, winning big vs. small is genuinely random. Very believable. Wind direction → pollution → driving. Wind isn't random — it correlates with season and weather — but those are measurable and controllable. Grey area, still plausible. Medicaid expansion → uncompensated care. Which states expanded was a deeply political decision. Strongest assumptions of the three. The muddier the variation, the more work required to justify it as random. Whether it works is contextual . You need to know the setting to know whether back doors exist. Front doors sound like they let you run an experiment anywhere. That's a genuinely exciting idea, and it's also where people stop reading the fine print. The honest statement: you're looking for something contributing to whether you're treated — it doesn't have to be the only determinant — that has no back doors of its own. If you find something that's actually random, like a policy someone assigned by lottery, you're probably fine. Outside that, you're in a grey area, and how far you're willing to go is a judgement about context. The frustrating position this leaves you in: you can't always run an experiment. Even when you can, you may not get enough people for it to be worthwhile. You can't just control for everything, because you may not be able to measure everything, or get people to believe your diagram. And now this cool third thing — but sometimes it just isn't believable either. The requirement moved, it didn't disappear Plain English. You still have to close all the back doors. Just for a different variable. Precisely. Instead of closing every back door from treatment to outcome, you must close every back door from your source of random variation to the outcome. The hope is that this is easier — that you're more likely to actually know all those pathways and be able to measure them — or that if the source is truly random, there aren't any. Anchor image. You didn't cancel the debt. You refinanced it, betting the new terms are easier to service. Level 1 — lottery winnings and bankruptcy Plain English. Everyone in the study bought a ticket. Among ticket buyers, how much you won is genuinely random. Worked example. There's a story you hear: someone wins the lottery, spends big, and goes bankrupt because they couldn't handle the money. Does that actually happen? The problem: people choose to play the lottery, and there could easily be a back door between being the kind of person who plays and your chances of going bankrupt. The design: compare people who all played the lottery , some of whom won big pots and some small ones. Within the group of ticket holders, whether you win a lot or a little is basically random. dag node P 0 0.6 "Plays lottery" unobs node W 1.5 0.6 "Size of win" treat node B 3 0.6 "Bankruptcy" out edge P W edge W B edge P B note 1.5 1.5 "condition on playing; within players, win size is random" Note what would not work: comparing lottery winners to non players. That reopens the back door from simply having bought a ticket. Once you know somebody already chose to buy tickets, winning or not is a good source of random variation. The finding: immediately after a big win, the chance of going bankrupt drops considerably. But wait a couple of years and it rises sharply. It roughly cancels out — winning doesn't make you much more or less likely to go bankrupt overall, it pushes your bankruptcy a couple of years into the future . Where it breaks. Barely does. There shouldn't be back doors from how much you win to bankruptcy except through the money itself, which is the treatment. Very believable. Level 2 — wind direction, pollution, and driving Plain English. You can't randomize smog. But you can use which way the wind is blowing. Worked example. Does polluted air make people drive more? Plausibly yes — when it's smoggy you don't want to walk, don't want to bike, maybe don't even want the bus; you want your car with the air conditioning on. And that's a nasty cycle, because driving creates pollution. Why the obvious approaches fail: you can't randomly assign more pollution to one day than another. And comparing a polluted city to a clean one doesn't work either — maybe the polluted city is polluted because people there drive more. The design: stay within one city. In Beijing, wind direction strongly affects smog — blowing one way it pushes pollution into the city, blowing the other it carries it out. Isolate the part of pollution driven by wind direction and you have something like randomly assigned pollution. The back doors you have to worry about: Concern Why it's a back door Fix Season Wind might blow west more in spring, east more in autumn — and people drive differently by season Measure the season, control for it Weather It might rain more when the wind blows west, and rain affects driving Control for weather the same way The judgement: wind direction isn't entirely random — it's related to other things that relate to driving. The question is whether you can control for those. And plausibly it's easier to close the back doors from wind direction to driving than from pollution to driving, because pollution is also tied up with things like whether the factories were running that day. The finding: when the wind blows pollution into the city, people drive a little bit more. Which makes sense. Once season and weather are controlled for, the random assignment assumption is relatively believable — but you're leaning on an assumption in a way you weren't with the lottery.

title: Treatment Effects
one: There is no such thing as *the* effect — every individual has their own treatment effect, so any single number you estimate is an **average** of individual effects, and the whole question becomes *which* average, over *whom*.
text: There is no such thing as the effect — every individual has their own treatment effect, so any single number you estimate is an average of individual effects, and the whole question becomes which average, over whom . The title of the book is a lie. Effects vary by person, context, and time. A cervical cancer drug can't reduce Nick's risk by 1%. He doesn't have a cervix — his risk is 0% with or without. Even among people who can get cervical cancer, effects differ by age, race, background, and baseline risk. That's heterogeneous treatment effects , and it's true essentially everywhere. Two responses: estimate the full distribution of effects (personalized medicine — Chapter 21), or estimate one number and work out what it represents. Any single estimate is an average of individual effects. Over everybody? Over a group? Weighted? Average treatment effect (ATE) = the average over everybody. Often described as the one you want — often it isn't. Rule of thumb 1: full randomization in a representative sample → ATE. Every chapter so far has been about identifying "the causal effect of X on Y." Time to admit that phrase doesn't refer to anything. Effects vary. The same treatment does different things to different people, in different contexts, at different times. Which means once you've done all the identification work and produced a number, you have a second question waiting: whose effect is this? And the answer determines what you're allowed to do with it. There is no the effect Plain English. A drug doesn't have one effect. It has a different effect for every person it's given to. Precisely. Individual treatment effects vary across people. A single reported effect is some aggregation of them. Anchor image. "How long does the commute take?" There's no such thing. There's your commute, and mine, and the one at 3am. Worked example. A drug reduces the rate of cervical cancer by, say, 1% on average . Nick doesn't have a cervix. His chance of cervical cancer is 0%, and it will still be 0% after taking the drug. So the drug cannot possibly reduce his risk by 1%. His individual treatment effect is exactly zero . So already there are at least two effects: one for people who have a cervix to get cancer in, and a different one for people who don't. But why stop there? Among people who do have a cervix, why would the effect be identical? Older versus younger, different races, different backgrounds, different baseline risk levels. For one person it reduces risk by nothing, for another by 1%, another by half a percent, another by 2%. Every individual person has a different causal effect. Where it breaks. This is one of the things making social science and causal inference harder than the physical sciences — you're chasing a different effect for every person. Heterogeneous treatment effects Plain English. The name for "the treatment does different things to different people." Precisely. Heterogeneous treatment effects exist when a treatment has different effects for different individuals. There is essentially always at least a little heterogeneity in any effect, anywhere. Where it breaks. Sometimes you can ignore it — if you think it doesn't differ much across people. Sometimes you need to think hard about what it means. Two ways to deal with it Plain English. Map the whole distribution of effects, or report one number and be honest about what it is. Precisely. 1. Estimate the full heterogeneity. Work out who the zeros are, who the 1%s are, who the 2%s are, and describe the whole distribution — enabling statements like "here is this pill's effect for you ." That's what personalized medicine is after. Methods for it wait until Chapter 21. 2. Estimate a single value , then determine what that value represents. Worked example — why the second question matters. Your estimate for the cervical cancer drug comes back at 1%. What is that? If it's the average over everybody , it's probably understating how good the drug is, because it's dragged down by all the Nicks whose effect is zero — and you'd never give them the pill anyway, so who cares that their effect is zero. If it's the average among people with a cervix , that's a different and more useful number. And even then, is averaging over everyone with a cervix what you want? If it's the overall figure, you should be more impressed by 1%, because it's being weighed down by all those zeros. Working out who you're getting the effect for is essential to knowing what you can do with it. Every single estimate is an average Plain English. Whatever number comes out, it took the true individual effects and averaged them somehow. Precisely. If you produce one value, it aggregates the true individual effects — possibly over everyone, possibly over a particular group, possibly as a weighted average. So the question is always: what kind of average, and for whom? Anchor image. A photograph of a crowd taken with a long exposure. It's a real image, and it's not any one person. Worked example — individual effects you can't see. Person Untreated risk Treated risk Individual effect Nick 0% 0% 0 Second person 5% 3% −2 percentage points Third person 0.8% 0.3% −0.5 percentage points Where it breaks. You can't observe any of these directly, because you can't observe the same person both getting and not getting the treatment. But a method can still tell you that your estimate averages Nick's effect with the second person's and the third's — even though you never see any of them individually. The average treatment effect, and rule of thumb 1 Plain English. The ATE is the effect if you gave the treatment to everybody. Precisely. The average treatment effect is the simplest and most straightforward aggregate: the average effect across everybody. It discards the heterogeneity and gives you one value, and that value is often well worth knowing. Anchor image. The average temperature of a country. Genuinely informative. Also useless for deciding what to wear anywhere in particular. Rule of t

title: Conditional Average Treatment Effects
one: A **conditional average treatment effect** is the average effect *within a group* — and the group can be defined by who you are (men, college students) or by what happened to you (the treated, the untreated), which is why randomizing within
text: A conditional average treatment effect is the average effect within a group — and the group can be defined by who you are (men, college students) or by what happened to you (the treated, the untreated), which is why randomizing within a selected sample gives you that sample's effect and comparing against a control group gives you the effect on the treated . A CATE is the average treatment effect among some group, conditional on being in it. Rule of thumb 2: randomize within a selected population → the CATE for that population. The group can also be defined by treatment status: average treatment on the treated (ATT) and average treatment on the untreated (ATUT) . Rule of thumb 4: using a control group to stand in for what the treated group would have looked like untreated → ATT . A CATE can be exactly what you want (only randomize the cervical cancer drug among people with a cervix) or exactly what you don't (college students standing in for humanity). Most "individual treatment effect" methods are elaborate CATEs — the effect for your particular interlocking set of groups. Every effect is heterogeneous, and any single estimate is some average. So the practical question is: which average did my design just hand me? This episode answers it for the most common case — averages within a group — and gives you two of the four rules of thumb for reading a design and knowing what came out. The setup: individual effects you could never actually see Plain English. To follow the arithmetic you need a table nobody could ever have — each person's outcome both with and without treatment. Worked example. Four types of people. Names, but think categories rather than individuals. Person Sex Untreated outcome Treated outcome Individual effect Alfred male 1 2 1 Brianna female 1 5 4 Chizu female 2 5 3 Diego male 2 4 2 Treatment works best for Brianna (+4) and least for Alfred (+1). The average treatment effect is the average of 1, 2, 3, and 4 = 2.5 . Where it breaks. You could never have this table in real data — you can't see the same person both treated and untreated. It's here so you can check what each estimator recovers. CATE by who you are, and rule of thumb 2 Plain English. Run your experiment on only one group and you get that group's effect, not everyone's. Precisely. A conditional average treatment effect is the average treatment effect among some group — conditional on being in that group. Worked example. You run a randomized experiment but recruit only men . Brianna and Chizu are gone; you have Alfreds and Diegos. Random assignment treats some and not others, and you observe the outcomes. Average outcome among the treated : avg(2, 4) = 3 Average outcome among the untreated : avg(1, 2) = 1.5 Difference: 1.5 What does 1.5 represent? Alfred's individual effect is 1; Diego's is 2. Their average is 1.5 — exactly the number you got. You recovered the average treatment effect among men . Note this is not the ATE of 2.5. Randomizing within a group gives you the group's effect. Rule of thumb 2. Randomizing within a selected population gives you the average treatment effect for that population — a CATE. This holds whether you picked the sample and randomized inside it, or found real world randomization that only applies to a particular group and used just that group. Where it breaks. Good or bad entirely depending on context: Good: the cervical cancer drug. You'd want to randomize only among people who have a cervix. Who cares about the effect on cervical cancer among people who can't get it? Bad: the psychology studies randomizing among college students while wanting to say something about the general population. You get the effect conditional on being a college student when you wanted something broader. CATE by what happened to you: ATT and ATUT Plain English. You can also condition on whether someone actually got treated. Precisely. Two more conditional averages: Average treatment on the treated (ATT) — the average effect among people who actually got the treatment. Average treatment on the untreated (ATUT) — the average effect among people who did not get it: how effective would it have been for this group, had it been applied to them? Both matter, and which you get depends on your estimation. ATT comes up more often. Working out the ATT Plain English. Compare the treated group's outcomes to a control group standing in for what the treated group would have looked like untreated. Worked example. Same four people. This time, instead of randomizing, you simply observe that Alfred and Chizu got treated , and Brianna and Diego did not . First, check there are no back doors. Look at what their untreated outcomes would have been: Alfred and Chizu (the treated): avg(1, 2) = 1.5 Brianna and Diego (the untreated): avg(1, 2) = 1.5 Identical. So absent treatment, the two groups would have looked the same — no back doors here. That's what licenses using one group's untreated outcomes as a stand in for the other's. Now make the comparison: Treated outcomes of those actually treated (Alfred, Chizu): avg(2, 5) = 3.5 Untreated outcomes of those actually untreated (Brianna, Diego): avg(1, 2) = 1.5 Difference: 3.5 − 1.5 = 2 What is that 2? Alfred's individual effect is 1; Chizu's is 3. Their average is 2 — exactly what you got. It's the average effect among the people who actually got treated : the ATT. And notice what happened. Without ever seeing a single individual treatment effect, the one number you estimated turned out to be an average of the individual effects in the treated group. That's what "a single estimate is an average of individual effects" means in practice. Rule of thumb 4. If your estimate uses a control group as an indication of what the treated group would have looked like without treatment , you're likely getting an average treatment on the treated. Which makes sense — you're asking how this treated group differs from what you think they'd have been without treatment. (Rule 3 is skipped here because

title: Weighted Average Treatment Effects
one: Some averages count everybody but not equally — controlling for variables makes people with more leftover variation in treatment (or who are easier to match) count more, and isolating random variation in treatment makes people who **respond
text: Some averages count everybody but not equally — controlling for variables makes people with more leftover variation in treatment (or who are easier to match) count more, and isolating random variation in treatment makes people who respond to it count more, which is the local average treatment effect. A weighted average treatment effect includes everyone, but some people count more than others. You don't pick the weights. The method imposes them, for a reason. Variance weighting (regression): after controlling for Z, whoever has more treatment variation left gets more weight — because that's all there is to look at. Matching / sample selection: whoever is easier to find a match for counts more, so common types dominate. Rule of thumb 3: controlling for variables in any way generally introduces a weighted average treatment effect. Local average treatment effect (LATE): when you isolate treatment variation driven by a random source, weight is proportional to how much you respond to it . Don't respond at all, weight zero. Rule of thumb 5: random variation in treatment + using only the predicted part → LATE. Which one you want depends entirely on what you'll do with it. None is better. You've got equally weighted averages — over everyone (ATE) or over a group (CATE). There's a third shape: everybody counts, at different levels. And the important thing is that this isn't a choice you make. You don't walk in and declare that this person matters more than that one. The method hands you the weights, and it has a reason. What a weighted average treatment effect is Plain English. Everyone's in the average, but with different amounts of pull. Precisely. Instead of averaging individual effects equally, or restricting to a group, each person enters with a weight. Nick gets 0.1, this person 0.2, that person 0.3 — and it works like any weighted average. Anchor image. A shareholder vote. Everybody votes. Not everybody has the same number of shares. Variance weighting Plain English. After controlling for things, some people have no treatment variation left — and you can't learn anything from someone who doesn't vary. Precisely. Adding a control variable means predicting what you can with it, subtracting that off, and working with the residuals — exactly the Chapter 4 operation. So you're analysing the variation in X and Y that remains after Z is removed. If there's no variation left for someone, there's nothing to study about them. Someone whose treatment is well explained by the controls has little residual variation, so a regression estimator weights their effect less . Someone with more leftover variation gets weighted more , simply because there's more to look at. Anchor image. Trying to learn what a dial does by watching people who never touch it. The ones who fiddle constantly teach you everything; the ones who never move it teach you nothing — so your conclusion is really about the fiddlers. Worked example. What's the effect of which city you live in on your income? Regress income on city. But there's a back door: a really effective worker might be more likely to move to a certain kind of city, and that's also related to their income. So control for individual characteristics. Now what variation is left? Person After controlling for who they are Weight Never moves — lives in one city their whole life No variation in city left. You can't see whether city affects their outcomes at all Low Moves a lot You can clearly see their income is higher in this city and lower in that one High And here's the sting: if moving cities is enormously effective for the person who never moves, and irrelevant for the person who moves constantly, you only see the mover's effect. Where it breaks. How this shakes out mathematically is fairly complex and doesn't come across well in a video — the chapter has it. The idea is what matters: statistics can only study things that vary, so less variation means less weight. Weighting from matching and sample selection Plain English. If you find people's doppelgängers instead of adjusting statistically, the easy to match people dominate. Precisely. Rather than statistical adjustment, you pick a dataset with no variation in the control variable — find someone very similar in every characteristic and compare. People who are easier to find a match for count more. Anchor image. A dating app that only shows you people with a match. If your profile is unusual, you never appear in anyone's results — and the aggregate statistics quietly become about everyone else. Worked example. Effect of city on income again, but by matching. Take a person and find someone with the same hair colour, height, gender, race, birthplace — two doppelgängers who happen to live in different cities. Who gets a match easily? People with the most common race, hair colour, height, and so on. Their treatment effects count for more. Unusual people rarely match and contribute little. Where it breaks. So regression gives you variance weighting and matching gives you something more like representativeness weighting . Different weights, same underlying fact. Rule of thumb 3 Precisely. Whenever you control for variables in some way — adjusting for them in a regression, or picking a sample — you are in general introducing some sort of weighted average treatment effect . The exact weights depend on how you incorporated the controls. Local average treatment effect Plain English. When you use random variation to drive treatment, people who ignore that random push don't count at all. Precisely. The local average treatment effect arises when you have a variable that randomly influences treatment and you identify the effect by isolating only the variation in treatment explained by that source — the front door strategy from Chapter 9. Different people respond differently to that random variation, and weight is proportional to responsiveness . Anchor image. Measuring how much a nudge moves people by watching only the ones who can be

title: Causality with Less Modeling
one: If drawing a full causal diagram is hopeless — and that objection is a serious one — you have four escapes: list only the **joint determinants** of treatment and outcome, use **front-door methods** and skip the diagram, draw a simplified di
text: If drawing a full causal diagram is hopeless — and that objection is a serious one — you have four escapes: list only the joint determinants of treatment and outcome, use front door methods and skip the diagram, draw a simplified diagram and test it, or accept you didn't close everything and report a range instead of a point. The objection: this is all too hard, and it depends entirely on having the right diagram. Plenty of people are sceptical you can model social settings this precisely. In a sceptic's view the real diagram is treatment → outcome, plus a back door running through a thousand variables you'll never measure . Approach 1 — joint determinants. Skip the full diagram; list the variables that cause both treatment and outcome. Much easier, gets you most of the way. Approach 2 — front doors. Don't model the confounding at all; find exogenous variation instead. Approach 3 — draw it anyway, then test it. Your diagram implies certain variables are unrelated. Check with placebo tests. Approach 4 — partial identification. Assume a range of severities for what you couldn't control, get a range of estimates, report the range. This closes Part 1 of the book. Everything so far demands a lot: think carefully about your model, draw the causal diagram, find every pathway from X to Y, close them all, make sure you can actually measure everything you need — or set up a process isolating just the front doors. That's difficult, and it all rests on having a very good idea of what your causal diagram is. You don't have to look hard to find people sceptical that you can model things this precisely. Every diagram in this series came prefaced with "assume this is right for a minute," and you can probably think of things left off even the more complex ones. People will disagree about what a diagram should look like. For a lot of social science, the objection is: this is simply too complex to write down in a neat compact diagram, so why bother? In that view the honest diagram is: dag node T 0 0 "Treatment" treat node Y 2.6 0 "Outcome" out node S 1.3 1 "...all sorts of stuff..." unobs edge T Y edge S T edge S Y note 1.3 1.85 "maybe 100 variables. maybe 1,000. you can't measure them." This is not a trivial objection. Things really are complex, and you really do have to simplify to use this process at all — and you could make the case that any such simplification is too simple. So: are you out of luck? Four answers. 1 · Focus on the joint determinants Plain English. Don't draw the whole diagram. Just list what causes both the treatment and the outcome. Precisely. You may not be able to write out the full complex diagram, but you can think very carefully about which variables are likely to sit directly on a simple back door path — the variables that cause the treatment and cause the outcome. Writing down that list of likely confounders is a far easier task than a complete diagram, and honestly it gets you most of the way there. Anchor image. You can't map the whole river system. You can find the places where two streams feed the same pond. Where it breaks. You'll miss things. You won't catch collider variables , and you might get yourself into trouble with post treatment bias . But at minimum you'll be thinking carefully about what has to be controlled for, even if not in the fully proper way. This is how a great many researchers actually operate: rather than a full diagram, they ask what variables plausibly cause both the treatment and the outcome, deal with those, and flag anything they can't measure as a problem to address. 2 · Use front door methods and skip the diagram Plain English. Don't model the confounding. Find variation that doesn't have any. Precisely. Plenty of researchers reason that there's no point drawing a big causal diagram and finding all the pathways, because at the end there'll be something they can't measure or something they forgot. The way around it is to isolate front door paths rather than close back doors — which is what the Chapter 9 methods are for. Where it breaks — Nick's own position, worth having. There are researchers who think you can only get a causal effect from a randomized controlled experiment. He thinks that's a bit harsh . There are others who think you can only get one from an experiment or by isolating front doors with exogenous variation. He thinks that's a bit too strong, but closer to his own view . The honest summary: it is simply a lot easier to identify a causal effect when you have a source of exogenous variation, because it lets you ignore a great deal of stuff that's very hard to figure out otherwise. 3 · Draw a simplified diagram, then test it Plain English. Admit it's simplified, then check you didn't get it egregiously wrong. Precisely. Draw the diagram knowing it's a simplification, then use the fact that a causal diagram implies certain conditional independences — pairs of variables that should be unrelated once you've closed the paths between them. Test those with placebo tests . Anchor image. You can't prove the bridge is perfect. You can drive a truck over it. Worked example. The humble version: "I know I'm leaving something out. I don't have the full picture of all the thousands of interlocking parts that should be here. But my diagram says these variables should be unrelated — let me check whether they are." You might get lucky and pass, and conclude that although something's missing, it's probably not that important. 4 · Partial identification and sensitivity analysis Plain English. You couldn't control for everything. So assume a range of severities for what you missed, and report the range of answers that follow. Precisely. Partial identification is the broader idea, most commonly applied as sensitivity analysis . Rather than stopping when you can't close every back door, you make a range of plausible assumptions about the leftover and compute what your estimate would be under each — arriving at a range rather than a point. title: Regression and The Error Term
one: Everything your model leaves out doesn't vanish — it goes into the **error term**, and "close all the back doors" translated into regression means exactly one thing: **make your treatment unrelated to the error term**, because if it isn't,
text: Everything your model leaves out doesn't vanish — it goes into the error term , and "close all the back doors" translated into regression means exactly one thing: make your treatment unrelated to the error term , because if it isn't, OLS hands your treatment the credit for whatever is hiding in there. Residual = actual minus predicted, for a data point. OLS picks the line making the squared residuals smallest. Error term (ε) = everything in the data generating process your model left out. Different concept. The error term is not noise . It's real determinants of your outcome — parents' wealth, lottery wins — that simply aren't in your equation. Adding a control variable moves it out of the error term into the model . That's what controlling is , in regression. The translation: closing all back doors ≈ making X unrelated to the error term. If X is related to it: omitted variable bias , X is endogenous , the effect isn't identified — all the same statement. Why it biases: OLS can't see Z, but it sees X hanging around while Z does good things. So it credits X. Part 2 of the book starts here — more technical, more about applying tools rather than thinking about identification in the abstract. And it starts with regression, because regression is enormously important and enormously common in causal inference. You already met it in Chapter 4. Regression estimates a conditional relationship by picking a shape — often a straight line — and fitting the best version of that shape. You can go further than a line into a plane in 3D space by adding control variables, and that easy flexibility to add controls is a big part of why it's so popular: it lets you isolate just the part of the X–Y relationship not explained by the control. So what's left to say? This: you've been focusing on the line you fit. Now focus on what you left out . Residual vs. error — two different things Plain English. A residual is how wrong you were about one point. The error term is what your whole model ignores about the world. Precisely. Residual — the difference between your prediction and the actual value, written as actual minus prediction. Some points sit above the line (reality was higher than you predicted), some below. Any prediction method has residuals, and they arise for many reasons: things left out, sampling variation, whatever. Error term — the difference between what your model would predict if it had all the data in the world and you had the best possible version of that estimated model , and what would still be left over. It captures only one thing: what's in your model versus what's still missing from it. Anchor image. A weather forecast. The residual is that it said 20° and it was 22°. The error term is that the model never looks at the ocean. Worked example. Predicting someone's wealth from their income. Higher paying job, more wealth accumulated — probably a strong relationship. Residual: you predict wealth from income, and sometimes you're a bit high, sometimes a bit low, for any individual. Error term: if income is the only thing you use, then everything else that predicts wealth — your parents' wealth, winning the lottery — is not in your model. It's still real, it still determines the outcome, and it has to go somewhere. That somewhere is the error term. Where it breaks — this is the important bit. The error term is not just random noise . Writing the data generating process as y = β₀ + β₁x + ε makes two claims: that x is in the data generating process for y, and that there's other stuff too. If you're predicting wealth from income, it isn't that everything besides income is noise — you can name several of those things, and they're real determinants of wealth. The linguistic jump: controlling = moving things out of the error term Plain English. A control variable doesn't create information. It relocates it. Precisely. Start with y = β₀ + β₁x + ε . If z is part of the data generating process for y — if z was used in the creation of y — then z is already in that first model . You can't see it, but everything determining y is in there somewhere, and if it isn't x, it's in ε. Now add z as a predictor: y = β₀ + β₁x + β₂z + ε . You haven't discovered z. You've moved z out of the error term and into the model. Anchor image. A room where the mess is either on the shelves or under the bed. Putting something on a shelf doesn't create it — it just stops being part of the unexamined pile. Why it matters: this changes the relationship between x and the error term. If z and x are related, then in the first model x is related to the error term . In the second, you've removed the part of the error term x was related to — and maybe now x is unrelated to it. The jump. We've spent chapters saying close all the back doors to identify the effect of X on Y . In a regression context, that is very closely related to saying: Make X unrelated to the error term. Omitted variable bias, and its many names Plain English. Leave out something correlated with your treatment, and your treatment absorbs its effect. Precisely. If x is related to the error term you have omitted variable bias . You may also hear that x is endogenous . These, plus "the effect isn't causally identified" and "you haven't closed all the back doors", are all largely the same statement. The "omitted variable" part: you've omitted from your model a variable related to x. Because it's omitted it lives in the error term, so x and the error term are related, and your estimate won't reflect the actual causal effect of x on y. Anchor image. A colleague who is always standing next to whoever fixes things. You never see the fixer. You promote the colleague. Worked example — why the bias happens. You estimate the relationship between y and x, and there's a z out there. Suppose z is positively related to x and positively related to y , but you fit the first model, so z sits in the error term. Z is doing all sorts of positive things for y. OLS doesn't know z exists — you n

title: Regression and Sampling Variation
one: An OLS coefficient is **normally distributed across samples**, centred on the population parameter, with a spread — the **standard error** — driven by how well your model predicts, how much your X varies, and how many observations you have;
text: An OLS coefficient is normally distributed across samples , centred on the population parameter, with a spread — the standard error — driven by how well your model predicts, how much your X varies, and how many observations you have; and hypothesis testing uses that distribution to ask whether your starting assumption survives, which is a much narrower question than people treat it as. Set identification aside. You don't have all the universe's data, so your estimate carries sampling variation . Across samples, an OLS coefficient follows a normal distribution , centred on the population parameter . Its standard deviation is the standard error , and three things drive it: model prediction error σ (up top), the variance of X , and the sample size n (both below). All three are about information . More information, more precision. Hypothesis testing: assume a null, build the sampling distribution it implies, see how unlikely your result was. Either something unlikely happened, or the assumption was wrong. It does not tell you the probability your estimate is correct. Nick's rant: the null is arbitrary, significance depends on sample size, insignificance can be the right answer, and never change your model to chase stars. You're doing statistics, which means you don't have all of the universe's data. The estimate you got isn't a perfect representation of the truth — ideally it's a representation of the truth plus some sampling variation and noise built in. So you've run a regression and got a coefficient. What does that estimate actually mean? To answer that you need to know how it would have bounced around if you'd drawn different data. The sampling distribution of an OLS coefficient Plain English. Collect data, estimate, repeat forever, and plot all the estimates. You get a bell curve centred on the truth. Precisely. An OLS coefficient follows a normal distribution across samples — provable, though not proved here. Its mean is the population parameter : what you'd get if you did have all the data in the universe and estimated your model. So on average, OLS gives you the population parameter. Anchor image. A well calibrated archer. Individual arrows scatter; the centre of the scatter is where they aimed. Worked example. You estimate 2.5. You don't know the population parameter — maybe it's 2.5, maybe 3, maybe 2. But you can say the truth is probably not 10 . That's the whole game: using the distribution to make claims about the population parameter from your single estimate. Where it breaks.
