"On average OLS gives you the population parameter" is not the same as giving you what you want. That population parameter can itself be biased if you haven't got causal identification. This episode is only about sampling variation. The standard error and its three ingredients Plain English. The standard error is how wide the bell curve is. Three things make it narrow, and all three are versions of "you have more information." Precisely. A standard error is the standard deviation of a sampling distribution. For an OLS coefficient it has three components: Component Where What it means σ — prediction error numerator The variance of the error term, estimated by the variance of the residuals. How closely your model can predict the outcome. Variance of X denominator How much your predictor moves around. Sample size n denominator How many observations you have. σ — model precision. If your model predicts the outcome very accurately, you know a lot about what's going on, so you can state your parameter with real confidence. Small residuals → small σ → small standard error. Knowing a lot about the outcome means your coefficient won't vary much from sample to sample. Variance of X. You can say more about a variable's effect if it moves around a lot — and the intuition is straightforward. If X moves a lot and Y stays still, you know nothing is happening. But if X barely moves and Y doesn't change much either, maybe you just can't see it. It's simply hard to study things with statistics that don't vary. More movement along X makes it far easier to see whether there's movement along Y. Sample size. More observations, more information, more precision. A large sample gives a small standard error because you have a lot to build inferences from. Anchor image. Reading a sign in fog. It gets clearer if the fog thins (σ), if you can walk back and forth to see it from angles (variance of X), or if a hundred people read it and compare (n). Why statisticians care about distributions at all Plain English. Knowing how a thing varies lets you rule out claims about the truth. Worked example. You estimate an OLS coefficient of 2 . Could that have come from a distribution with population parameter 100 and standard deviation 1? Essentially never — you'd basically never see a value that low. And yet you did. So that's the wrong sampling distribution. Hypothesis testing, carefully Plain English. Assume something, work out what data you'd expect, and see whether what you got is absurd under that assumption. Precisely. The procedure: 1. Make an assumption about the world — the null hypothesis . Say the true population parameter is 100. 2. Build the sampling distribution that assumption implies. You know it's normal, you know it's centred on the assumed parameter, and you know the standard deviation — so you know exactly what the sampling distribution would be, and how likely any particular estimate is under it. 3. Compare your actual estimate to it. 4. If your outcome was very unlikely under that distribution, exactly two things could have happened: something very unlikely just occurred , or your starting assumption was wrong . Worked example. Null hypothesis: the average person is 5 feet 7 inches tall. That gives you a sampling distribution of sample averages centred on 5'7", varying a bit above and below from sample to sample. Now you draw a sample and it averages 3 feet . Under the null, that is wildly unlikely from random chance alo

title: Reading Regression Tables
one: Every cell in a regression table is the same three things — **estimate**, **standard error in parentheses**, **stars for the p-value** — and the fit statistics underneath (R², adjusted R², F) describe the model as a whole, which for causal
text: Every cell in a regression table is the same three things — estimate , standard error in parentheses , stars for the p value — and the fit statistics underneath (R², adjusted R², F) describe the model as a whole, which for causal work you should mostly ignore. A regression table is compact and lets you compare models side by side. Each column is one regression. Rows are the predictors, plus an intercept (a.k.a. constant, β₀, α). Each cell: estimate , then in parentheses a standard error — or sometimes a t statistic — then significance stars . Stars encode the p value against a default null of coefficient = 0 , and they stack . What the stars mean varies by field. Look for the table note. Below: number of observations , then model fit statistics — R² , adjusted R² , F statistic . For causal inference, R² is mostly irrelevant. A clean randomized experiment often has a small R² and a very good model. After that rant, something straightforward. A regression table is just a table with regression results on it — a very common presentation because it's compact, easy to read, and lets you show several models at once, all read the same way. The layout Plain English. Columns are models. Rows are variables. The stuff at the bottom is about the model, not any one variable. Worked example. Two regressions predicting restaurant health inspection scores : (1) (2) Intercept … … Number of locations −0.019 <br (0.000) … Year of inspection −0.065 <br (0.006) Observations 27,178 … R² 0.065 … The top names the dependent variable — what you're predicting, your y. Here it's inspection scores in both columns. The first row is the intercept , which some tables call the constant ; in an equation it's β₀ or α. Then the predictors — the independent variables, your x and your controls. Here: number of locations (for chain restaurants) and year of inspection. Column 1 uses only number of locations; column 2 includes both. Reading a single cell Plain English. Three numbers stacked: what you estimated, how precise it is, how surprising it is under a null of zero. Worked example. Take −0.019 in column 1. 1 · The estimate. The first number is the estimated coefficient. If you wrote out y = β₀ + β₁ × number of locations , this is your β₁ hat — the estimated slope on number of locations is −0.019. 2 · The parentheses. Below sits 0.000 , a standard error . Some tables instead show a t statistic — the coefficient divided by the standard error. A table note usually says which; if not, here's the heuristic: Significant coefficients with small values in parentheses → standard errors Significant coefficients with large values in parentheses → t statistics And 0.000 doesn't mean the standard error is actually zero — it can't be. It means it's smaller than the smallest value the table is willing to display. 3 · The stars. Those asterisks are significance stars , representing the p value. By default in essentially all regression tables, the null being tested is that the coefficient is zero . So: assume the slope on number of locations is zero, build a sampling distribution centred on zero with a standard error of (here) roughly zero, and ask how unlikely it would be to see an estimate of −0.019 or further away in either direction. That probability is the p value, and the stars encode it. In this table — the standard convention in economics: Stars Meaning p < 0.1 p < 0.05 p < 0.01 Other fields commonly use for 0.05, for 0.01, for 0.001. Look for the table note. Three stars therefore means p < 0.01 — significant if you'd chosen a 0.01 cutoff. And you can read it in reverse: had you picked 0.01 as your threshold, this coefficient clears it. Stars stack , because if p < 0.01 then it's also below 0.05 and 0.1. Column 2's year of inspection cell reads the same way: estimate −0.065 , standard error 0.006 , p < 0.01. Where it breaks. Depending on how the table is built, standard errors sometimes sit beside the coefficient rather than below. That's fine — just a formatting choice. Model fit statistics Plain English. Numbers at the bottom describing the whole model rather than any coefficient. Number of observations. Here, 27,178. R². The correlation between your model's predicted values and the actual outcome, squared. It represents the proportion of the outcome's variance explained by the model. With R² = 0.065: doing 1 − 0.065 = 0.935 says that if you split the outcome into the part your model predicted and the part left in the residual, 6.5% of the variation is explained and 93.5% is not . It partitions the outcome into in the model and out of the model. Where R² breaks — and it gets misused a lot. R² measures only how much variance you explained. It does not tell you how good the model is. For causal work you want to estimate one parameter very well. The concern is whether −0.019 reflects the causal effect of number of locations on inspection score. That plenty of other things drive inspection scores simply doesn't matter to you. Never change your model to raise R². The clinching example: a randomized controlled experiment has very clean, very easy causal identification, and often a small R², because a lot is going on in the outcome that the randomly assigned treatment doesn't explain. R² looks bad; the model is excellent. If your only goal is prediction , R² makes more sense — that's what it's about. Even then, there are probably better measures. Overall, in this book, you can more or less ignore R². It's a nice quick diagnostic with a sharp interpretation — the share of outcome variation inside versus outside your model — but that's all it does, and it isn't answering a question you care about. Adjusted R². R² will always go up as you add variables, because more predictors means more flexibility to fit the data — and that's true even if the variables you add are complete nonsense. Adjusted R² instead asks whether the variables you included explain more than you'd expect from adding random noise . Nick's view: he uses adjusted R² less

title: Interpreting a Regression Coefficient
one: There is one sentence — *a one-unit change in X is associated with a coefficient-sized unit change in Y* — and it handles essentially every regression coefficient you will ever meet, provided X appears in only one place in your model.
text: There is one sentence — a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y — and it handles essentially every regression coefficient you will ever meet, provided X appears in only one place in your model. The mantra: a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. Say it out loud. Fill in the blanks. Done. With controls: adjusting for all the other predictors, a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. "Associated with", not "increases". Causal language requires causal identification, which by default you don't have. If X is transformed — say you regressed on log(X) — the sentence still works. It's just about the log, so you have extra work to get back to X. It only fails when X appears more than once in the model: polynomials (X and X²) or interactions (X, Z, X×Z). When it fails, use the derivative instead. The effect becomes an expression, not a single number. This is genuinely simple, and people work far too hard to make it complicated for themselves. If you have a regression model you're unsure how to interpret, the fix is almost always to say the sentence again and slot the pieces in: what's X, what's Y, what does a one unit change mean here? The mantra Plain English. One unit up in X, coefficient many units up in Y. Precisely. If you regress Y on X and get a coefficient: A one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. Add controls and it barely changes: Adjusting for all the other predictors in the model, a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. Anchor image. A currency conversion rate. One of these buys that many of those. The coefficient is the exchange rate between X units and Y units. Where it breaks. This always works as long as X only shows up in one place in your model. Wrinkle 1 — "associated with" is doing deliberate work Plain English. The mantra is written in non causal language on purpose. Precisely. "Associated with" implies no causal relationship, and that's the correct default — you shouldn't be assuming you've causally identified anything. You may have heard the causal phrasing: a one unit change in X increases Y by the size of the coefficient. That version assumes causal identification. If you genuinely have it, you're entitled to say "increases" or "decreases". Otherwise, "associated with". Anchor image. The difference between "these two arrive together" and "this one brings the other." Wrinkle 2 — transformed variables Plain English. If you regressed on a transformation, the sentence describes the transformation, not the original variable. Precisely. Regress Y on log(X) and the mantra still applies exactly: a one unit change in the log of the predictor is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. The sentence never breaks, because you're just regressing an outcome on a predictor. Where it breaks. You probably don't care about a one unit change in the log of X — you care about X. So the sentence works, and then you have some work to do afterwards converting it into a statement about the thing you're actually interested in. That's the next episode. Wrinkle 3 — when the sentence genuinely fails Plain English. If X appears twice, you can't change one X without changing the other. Precisely. The mantra fails in one common setting: X shows up in more than one place among the predictors. Two ways that happens: Polynomials — regressing Y on X and X² Interaction terms — regressing Y on X, Z, and X×Z Why it breaks: the full mantra says holding all the control variables constant , a one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized change in Y. But you can't hold the others constant . If your model contains X and X², you cannot change X by one unit without also changing X². They move together. The fallback: the derivative Plain English. The coefficient was always a slope. When there are several, compute the slope directly. Precisely. Another interpretation of a regression coefficient: it's the derivative . If you regress Y on X, then the derivative of Y with respect to X is simply β₁. "A one unit change in X is associated with a certain sized change in Y" is describing the slope of the line. For a more complex model — Y on X and X², or anything else — do the same thing: take the derivative of Y with respect to X. You'll get a calculation, which may itself contain coefficients and variable values. Whatever that expression is, that's the relationship between X and Y: A one unit change in X is associated with a [calculation] sized unit change in Y. Anchor image. A single coefficient is a fixed exchange rate posted on the wall. A derivative is a rate that depends on how much you're changing — you have to look it up for your amount. A coefficient means X increases Y by that amount → Actually, "associated with" unless you've established causal identification. The causal phrasing is a separate claim. The mantra needs modifying for models with control variables → Actually, you just prepend "adjusting for all the other predictors". The structure is identical. If I regressed on log(X), the coefficient tells me about X → Actually, it tells you about a one unit change in log(X). Getting back to X takes extra work. The mantra breaks whenever the model gets complicated → Actually, it breaks in one specific circumstance: X appearing in more than one place. I can hold X² constant while changing X → Actually, you can't. That's precisely why the mantra fails for polynomials and interactions. Derivatives are a separate, more advanced interpretation → Actually, it's the same interpretation. A single coefficient is the derivative; multi place X just makes it an expression. People tie themselves in knots interpreting regression coefficients, and honestly it's one sentence. Here it is: A one unit change in X is associated with a coefficient sized unit change in Y. That's it. If you're staring at a model you do

title: Polynomials in Regression
one: Regression gives you the best version of the shape you chose — and the best version of a bad shape is still bad — so when a relationship curves, add powers of X to let the line bend, then accept that the effect of X is now **different at ev
text: Regression gives you the best version of the shape you chose — and the best version of a bad shape is still bad — so when a relationship curves, add powers of X to let the line bend, then accept that the effect of X is now different at every value of X and read it off with a derivative. Regression fits the best version of the shape you picked . Pick badly and you get the best version of something bad. Default OLS shape: a straight line. Plenty of relationships aren't straight. Fix by transforming variables so the transformed relationship is straight. The two big ones in causal inference: polynomials and logarithms . A polynomial adds powers of X. Linear = first order. Quadratic = X and X². Cubic = X, X², X³. Keep the lower terms. Diagnose the need by plotting residuals against X . A visible pattern is a warning sign. Stop at third order. More gets weird — don't add them willy nilly. Interpretation: there is no single effect of X , and there shouldn't be. Take the derivative and plug in a value of X. Remember what regression is: a shape fitting procedure. You supply a shape and it finds the best version of that shape for your data. Getting the best version of a shape sounds great — but you still had to pick the shape, and if the shape doesn't make sense for your data you get back a bad result. Even the best version of a bad thing is still a bad thing. By default, ordinary least squares fits a straight line. Look at data that clearly curves: fit a straight line and you'll get the best possible line, which is still a poor fit, because the relationship simply isn't well described by a straight line. Transformations Plain English. You can't bend the line, so bend the variables instead. Precisely. If the relationship between your variables isn't a straight line, you can often still get a good fit from regression by transforming the variables — so that while the original data has no straight line relationship, the transformed data does. Several transformations are popular in statistical analysis; the book covers a few, and there are more it doesn't. The two you'll meet most in causal inference and regression analysis are polynomials and logarithms . Polynomials Plain English. Add X squared to let the line curve. Add X cubed to let it curve back. Precisely. A polynomial adds additional powers of the variable to your model. Model Terms What it allows Linear — first order polynomial X A straight line. Slope never changes: increase X by one and Y always moves by β₁. Quadratic — second order X and X² One curve. Parabola shaped, though zoomed in it may not curve back on itself. Cubic — third order X, X², X³ A curve, then a curve back the other direction. Quartic, quintic, … X⁴, X⁵, … More wiggle than you want. Keep the lower order terms. A quadratic includes X and X². You don't leave the X out. Anchor image. A garden hose. Straight by default. One bend, two bends — after about three you're not shaping it, you're just tangling it. Where it breaks. You're pretty much always fine with the first three orders. Beyond that, don't get wild: too many polynomial terms makes the model behave strangely, and you shouldn't add them willy nilly. Diagnosing with residuals Plain English. Plot your residuals against X. If they make a shape, your line has the wrong shape. Precisely. With a straight line fit to curved data, the residuals sit consistently above the line at low X, dip below in the middle, then come back above at high X. That's a relationship between the residuals and X, which you don't want. Why it matters: one of the assumptions you need about the error term is that it's unrelated to X . What you're really worried about is the error term, not the residuals — but a visible pattern in the residuals is still not a good sign. Worked example. Plot the residuals from both fits of the same curved data: Straight line model: a clear pattern remains — residuals shift systematically as you move along the X axis. Quadratic model: no relationship whatsoever between residuals and X. Exactly what you want. Where it breaks. So the rule is: plot residuals against X, and if you see a pattern, that may indicate you want a polynomial term to get rid of it. Interpreting polynomial terms Plain English. You can't read one coefficient anymore — and there is no single effect of X. Precisely. Once X appears in more than one place, you must consider all the places it appears to interpret its effect. And stranger still: there is no single effect of X, and there shouldn't be. The effect of a one unit increase in X is the slope of the fitted line, and in a polynomial model the slope is changing all the time. So the effect differs at low, middle, and high values of X. How to get it: write out the equation and take the derivative. For a cubic model, the derivative of Y with respect to X is: dY/dX = β₁ + 2·β₂·X + 3·β₃·X² where β₁ is the coefficient on X, β₂ on X², and β₃ on X³. Notice X is in that expression. The whole expression is the effect of X — so to state an effect you must pick a specific value of X to evaluate it at. At X = 0 : everything else drops out, and the effect is just β₁ . At X = 1 : the effect is β₁ + 2β₂ + 3β₃ . Worked example: chain restaurants Worked example. Regress restaurant health inspection scores on the number of locations the chain has, with a quadratic term: Predictor Coefficient Number of locations −0.08 Number of locations² 0.0001 You want: comparing two chain restaurants where one has one more location than the other, what's the association with the inspection score? Write out the model and take the derivative: Score = −0.08 · locations + 0.0001 · locations² + … dScore/dLocations = −0.08 + 2 · 0.0001 · locations Now plug in whatever value of X you care about: At this many locations The effect of one more location 1 −0.08 + 2(0.0001)(1) = −0.0798 1,000 −0.08 + 2(0.0001)(1,000) = +0.12 Going from one location to two, and going from a thousand locations to a thousand and one, give completely different answ

title: Logarithms in Regression
one: Logarithms fix two problems at once — they scrunch skewed variables so billionaires don't drag your line around, and they convert additive changes into **proportional** ones — at the cost of an interpretation step, which you can do exactly
text: Logarithms fix two problems at once — they scrunch skewed variables so billionaires don't drag your line around, and they convert additive changes into proportional ones — at the cost of an interpretation step, which you can do exactly or with the approximation that log(1 + small) ≈ small . Reason 1 — skew. In social science, a few observations are many times bigger than the mass. OLS minimises squared residuals, so it chases the billionaire. Reason 2 — proportional relationships. Some relationships should multiply rather than add. Interest rates don't add dollars to your wealth, they multiply it. Logs keep the big values in the data but scrunch them , so they still count without over influencing. Logs also give proportional interpretations, because log(x) + log(y) = log(x·y) turns addition into multiplication. The mantra still works — it's just about a one unit change in the log , so you need a conversion step. Approximation: log(1 + a) ≈ a for small a. Breaks down fast — good to about 10%, useless at 50%. Logs can't take zero or negative values, and zeros are a real problem. Polynomials let a straight line curve. Logarithms solve two different problems, and they get enormous use in the social sciences because we deal with a lot of highly skewed variables. Remember the framing: it's fine to fit a straight line between some non straight things . That's what lets you model non straight relationships with a procedure that only draws straight lines. Reason 1: skew and over influential outliers Plain English. A few observations are astronomically bigger than everyone else, and OLS contorts itself to accommodate them. Precisely. A skewed variable has a mass of relatively similar observations and a few that are way different. Crucially, it isn't just that there are outliers — it's that the outliers are so many times bigger than the mass. Worked example. Wealth. A typical person has a few thousand dollars, maybe tens of thousands, maybe a hundred thousand depending on the group. Then there's someone with billions and billions. Why OLS struggles: it minimises the sum of squared residuals. Suppose it's describing the mass of people well — and then there's someone whose wealth it's off by billions on. That residual is enormous, so the model changes to fit that one person well. Anchor image. A see saw with a hundred children on one end and a cargo ship on the other. It doesn't matter how many children there are. Where it breaks — the honest nuance. Outliers aren't necessarily wrong data, and they should affect your analysis — they're part of your data. The problem is only that you don't want them to have undue, over large influence on your model. That's really what the concern about outliers is. Also worth noting: in the social sciences the skew is almost always in this direction. There aren't many variables where a lot of people are very large and a few are very, very tiny. Reason 2: relationships that should be proportional Plain English. Some things multiply rather than add, and a straight line can't express that. Worked example. Your wealth over time depends on what's in your savings and the interest or investment return on it. Regress wealth on investment return — that shouldn't be a straight line relationship. If the return rate goes up by a certain percentage, your wealth doesn't rise by a certain number of dollars ; it rises by a certain percent . If the interest rate goes up by 0.01, that should multiply your wealth by 1.01, not add a fixed amount. What logs do about both Plain English. They squash the giants without deleting them, and they turn plus into times. Precisely. Skew: logs take those very large values and keep them in the data but scrunch them down, so there's less distance between them and the mass. They're still counted; they just don't pull as hard. Proportionality: logs have a convenient proportional interpretation, because log(x) + log(y) = log(x·y) . The mechanism. A regression gives you additive changes — "a one unit increase in X is associated with a coefficient sized increase in Y." But adding 1 is the same as adding log(e¹) . And log(x) + log(e) is log(x·e) . So an additive one unit increase in the log becomes a multiplication of the original x by e . That's how a linear increase becomes a proportional one. Interpreting: the exact way Plain English. Push the coefficient back through the log algebra until you get a percentage. Precisely — two directions. Log on the left: regress log(y) on x. a one unit change in x → β₁ unit change in log(y) β₁ = log(e^β₁) [natural logs, always, in social science] log(y) + log(e^β₁) = log(y · e^β₁) So y has increased proportionally by e^β₁ . Log on the right: regress y on log(x). What's the effect of a 1% increase in x? a 1% increase means x · 1.01 log(x · 1.01) = log(x) + log(1.01) so log(x) rose by log(1.01) units therefore y rises by β₁ · log(1.01) Where it breaks. That's the accurate route: convert the linear change your regression gives you into a proportional change you can actually interpret — in either direction. Interpreting: the approximation Plain English. For small numbers, log(1.05) is basically 0.05. So percentages and log units are nearly the same thing. Precisely. log(1 + a) ≈ a when a is small: Expression Approximately log(1.01) 0.01 log(1.05) 0.05 log(1.1) 0.1 Where it breaks — quickly. This is an approximation and it degrades fast as the change grows. You would not say a 50% increase in x (a 1.5 proportional increase) is about a 0.5 increase in log(x). That no longer works. Keep the change around 0.1 (10%) or lower ; above that, do it properly. Worked example — three walkthroughs. Start from the mantra and convert one stage at a time. Regress y on log(x): 1. A one unit change in log(x) is associated with a β₁ sized change in y. 2. Scale everything down by 100 so the approximation applies: a 0.01 unit change in log(x) is associated with a 0.01·β₁ change in y. 3. A 0.01 unit change in log(x) is approximately a 1% change

title: Categorical Predictors
one: A binary predictor in a regression is just a **comparison of means**, and a categorical variable is a set of binary indicators with **one left out as the reference group** — which means every coefficient, and every significance test on it,
text: A binary predictor in a regression is just a comparison of means , and a categorical variable is a set of binary indicators with one left out as the reference group — which means every coefficient, and every significance test on it, is relative to whichever category you happened to omit. A binary predictor compares means . Regress income on treatment, get 5,000: mean income is 5,000 higher among the treated. Same as a two sample t test. Why bother with regression? Because you can add control variables . It follows from the mantra: with a binary variable the only sensible one unit change is 0 → 1. The intercept becomes the mean of the group coded zero. A categorical variable becomes one binary indicator per category — and you must leave one out . The omitted category is the reference group . Every other coefficient is a difference from it . Change the reference group and every coefficient and every significance test changes . To test the variable as a whole, use a joint F test . You've been working on the right hand side of a regression — transformations, non linear terms. Now something you'll see constantly: predictors that are categories. Lots of variables are like this. Did you get the treatment or not — you can't half get it. Which continent are you on — seven mutually exclusive options, and you can't be on two at once (barring something odd at a border). So how do you put those on the right hand side, whether as your treatment or as a control? A binary predictor compares means Plain English. The coefficient is the gap between the group averages. Precisely. Including a binary predictor gives you a comparison of means — exactly the same comparison as a two sample t test. Anchor image. A step, not a slope. There's only one place to put your foot down. Worked example. Regress income on whether you got the treatment, and the coefficient comes back 5,000 . That says the mean income is 5,000 higher for the group that got the treatment than for the group that didn't. Why use regression instead of a t test? Because you can add control variables. Then you can say: controlling for everything I need to identify my effect, the effect of the treatment on the outcome is [coefficient] — the mean of the outcome rises by that much going from 0 to 1. You also get a standard error and a significance test on the coefficient for free, letting you check whether the average outcome differs statistically between the yes group and the no group. Why it works. Return to the mantra: a one unit increase in X is associated with a coefficient sized change in Y. Now make X binary. There's only one one unit change that makes any sense at all — going from 0 (not in the group) to 1 (in the group) . So the coefficient gives the jump in the outcome you'd see, on average, going from a no to a yes. The intercept becomes the reference mean Plain English. Set the binary variable to zero and only the intercept is left — so the intercept is the mean of that group. Worked example. Regress sales on whether it's currently winter. Average sales are 10 in winter and 15 in not winter . Term Coefficient Why Intercept 15 Set winter = 0 and only the intercept remains, so it must be the not winter average Winter −5 Winter sales are 5 lower than not winter Your model should give the best prediction it can — the winter average for winter, the not winter average for not winter. That's exactly what these two numbers deliver. Where it breaks — the key idea. The coefficient on winter is relative to something : winter versus not winter. A binary coefficient is always about being relative to something else. That's the bridge to categorical variables with more than two categories. Categorical variables: one indicator per category, minus one Plain English. Split the categories into yes/no variables, then throw one away. Precisely. Take each category and turn it into its own binary indicator. For continents: are you in Asia, yes or no? In Europe, yes or no? In South America, yes or no? And so on — seven categories, seven binary indicators. Then, when you run the regression, you have to leave one out. The reason is statistical (the book has the full explanation), but you do have to. Whichever you leave out becomes your reference group . A note on vocabulary. You may have heard binary indicators called dummies . Nick avoids the term — if you're talking to someone who doesn't know econometrics and you say "I put the female dummy in the model," that may not go over well. Binary indicator is the better term. Where it breaks. People in the omitted group are still in your data. They just don't get their own coefficient — they become the intercept, and everything else is measured relative to them. Leave out Asia, and the coefficient on Europe is not "the effect of being in Europe." It's the difference in means between Europe and Asia. And South America's coefficient is South America versus Asia. And Africa's is Africa versus Asia. Everything is relative to the same reference group. Because of this, the individual coefficients don't mean much by themselves. They only mean something relative to the reference group you selected. The reference group is arbitrary — and so are your significance tests Plain English. Pick a different omitted category and every number in the table changes. Worked example. Income regressed on country, with three countries in the data: Country Average income France 30 Gambia 30.5 New Zealand 33 Reference group: France. Term Coefficient Why Intercept 30 France's average — the prediction when both other indicators are 0 Gambia 0.5 30.5 − 30 New Zealand 3 33 − 30 Now switch the reference group to Gambia. France re enters the model with its own term: Term Coefficient Why France −0.5 30 − 30.5 New Zealand 2.5 33 − 30.5 Where it breaks. All the coefficients — and therefore all the statistical significance tests — are completely arbitrary, determined by whichever reference group you happened to choose. So if you want to test something, mak

title: Interaction Terms
one: An interaction term lets the effect of X **differ across values of another variable Z** — so the effect of X is no longer β₁ but **β₁ + β₃Z**, meaning β₁ is the effect for the Z = 0 group, β₁ + β₃ is the effect for the Z = 1 group, and β₃ i
text: An interaction term lets the effect of X differ across values of another variable Z — so the effect of X is no longer β₁ but β₁ + β₃Z , meaning β₁ is the effect for the Z = 0 group, β₁ + β₃ is the effect for the Z = 1 group, and β₃ is the difference between those two effects . Polynomials let the effect of X vary across values of X . Interactions let it vary across values of Z . Most common use: treatment effects differing across groups — a conditional average treatment effect. Adding Z as a control does not do this. Without the interaction, changing Z shifts the line up and down; the slope on X never changes. Always include Z on its own alongside X×Z, or the interaction coefficient absorbs Z's effect from the error term. The effect of X is β₁ + β₃Z . You must plug in a value of Z. β₁ = effect when Z = 0. β₁ + β₃ = effect when Z = 1. β₃ = the difference between them. Don't go fishing. Interactions are noisy — a binary interaction estimates each effect on roughly half your sample. Fair warning: interaction terms are fantastic and they show up everywhere in causal inference — a lot of research designs are built around them — and they are also genuinely hard to get your head around. This is a page to bookmark and come back to when you meet one in the wild. Take it slowly, one step at a time, and you'll get the right interpretation. The idea itself is a small step from what you've already done. Polynomials and logarithms fit a curvy line, which is really saying the effect of X on Y should be different at different values of X . But what if the effect of X changes as the value of a different variable changes? That can obviously happen, and interaction terms are how you study it. The commonest version: seeing how a treatment effect differs across groups. One effect for women, another for men; one for tall people, another for short. That's a conditional average treatment effect — you're letting the effect of X on Y differ across values of another variable. The model, and what does not work Plain English. You need the product term. A plain control won't do it. Precisely. The model with an interaction: y = β₀ + β₁·x + β₂·z + β₃·(x·z) + ε You're interested in the effect of x, and you're allowing it to vary across values of z. The common misconception: that adding z as a control by itself lets the effect of x differ across values of z. It doesn't. Drop the interaction and keep only x and z: as z changes, the line moves up and down , but the slope on x stays the same . For the effect of x to differ between groups, the slope has to differ between groups — and without the interaction term that simply isn't allowed to happen. Anchor image. A control variable is an elevator: it moves the whole floor up or down. An interaction is a hinge: it changes the tilt. Always keep Z in the model Plain English. Never include X×Z without also including Z on its own. Precisely. You pretty much always want z in the model, even when you have no interest in it. The reason is omitted variable bias — back doors. Leave z out and it goes into the error term, because everything determining y that isn't in the model goes there. And the interaction term x×z is definitely related to z. So the coefficient on the interaction would be mixed up , combining the effect of the interaction and the effect of the z now sitting in the error term. Getting the effect of X: take the derivative Plain English. X appears twice, so β₁ isn't the answer. Differentiate. Precisely. Because x shows up in two places, the change in y as x changes is reflected in two parts of the model. Write out the equation and take the derivative — the first thing to do every time, especially when you're starting out: dy/dx = β₁ + β₃·z β₁ is the coefficient on x; β₃ is the coefficient on the interaction. This whole expression is the effect of x , and it's conditional on z — so you have to plug in a value of z. Everything below works if z is continuous too; it just takes an extra mental step. Assume z is binary , which is both the most common case and much easier to read. Reading the three coefficients Quantity Value Meaning Effect of x when z = 0 β₁ Plug z = 0 and the β₃·z term drops out Effect of x when z = 1 β₁ + β₃ Plug z = 1 β₃ the difference How much the effect of x changes going from z = 0 to z = 1 Why β₃ means that. Look at the derivative again — β₁ + β₃·z — and compare it to a regression on a binary indicator, y = β₀ + β₁·(binary) . There, the coefficient on the binary variable gave you the difference in means between the two groups. Same idea here, except the left hand side is now the derivative of y with respect to x rather than y itself. So β₃ is still the difference across groups — it's just the difference in the effect of x on y rather than in the mean of y. You can also see it directly: the z = 1 group's effect is β₁ + β₃, the z = 0 group's is β₁, and the difference is β₃. Which means: the coefficient on the interaction term gives you a test of whether the effect differs between the two groups. Worked example: restaurant inspections on weekends Worked example. Regress health inspection scores on the number of locations a chain has, whether it's currently the weekend, the interaction of those two, and a control for year of inspection: Term Coefficient Number of locations −0.019 Weekend 1.759 Number of locations × weekend −0.010 The effect of number of locations is the derivative: dScore/dLocations = −0.019 + (−0.010 · weekend) When Effect of one more location Not the weekend (weekend = 0) −0.019 The weekend (weekend = 1) −0.019 + (−0.010) = −0.029 Difference between them −0.010 — the interaction coefficient So the effect is 0.010 different on the weekend than during the week. And note it isn't a particularly strong effect — there's no statistical significance here, so you don't have enough evidence to reject that the effects are the same on the weekend and during the week. The warning: don't go fishing Plain English. Interactions are tempting, addictive,

title: Your Standard Errors are Wrong
one: The standard error formula isn't a definition — it's a **calculation resting on assumptions about the error term**, and when those fail (heteroskedasticity, autocorrelation, clustering) your standard errors stop measuring the sampling distr
text: The standard error formula isn't a definition — it's a calculation resting on assumptions about the error term , and when those fail (heteroskedasticity, autocorrelation, clustering) your standard errors stop measuring the sampling distribution, which is the only thing they were for. Good news first: when these assumptions fail, your coefficient is usually fine . It's the standard error that breaks. Normality of errors is the least of your worries — and it's about the error term , not your variables. The real assumption is that errors are i.i.d. : identically distributed and independent. Not identically distributed → heteroskedasticity. The variance of the error is related to X. Not independent → autocorrelation (repeated measures over time) or clustering (hierarchical data). Why it breaks the formula: σ² is meant to capture error variance, and you measure it badly if you ignore these patterns. Fix with sandwich estimators — same formula, σ² and the variance of X computed to allow for the problem. Many economists just always use heteroskedasticity robust errors. Nick: seems okay to him. Bad news: your standard errors are probably wrong and you need to fix them. This is a scourge of the applied econometrician — or anyone doing causal inference — and you will have to deal with it. Here's the issue. A standard error is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of your coefficient, and you need it because it tells you how much you actually know — how precise a claim you can make. Ep 26 gave you a formula for it. But that formula is not the definition of the standard deviation of an OLS coefficient. It's a calculation based on assumptions . If the assumptions are wrong, the calculation no longer reflects the standard deviation of the sampling distribution — which is the only reason you computed it. Reassuringly: when these assumptions fail, the main casualty is your estimate of the standard error . Your OLS coefficient will usually be fine . But you still have to get the standard errors right. Assumption 1: normally distributed errors — mostly ignorable Plain English. It's about the errors, not your variables. And even then it barely matters. Precisely. In y = β₀ + β₁x + ε , one assumption is that ε follows a normal distribution. The widespread misunderstanding: many people take this to mean your variables — your X's and Y's — must be normally distributed. They don't. The assumption is only about the error term. And even past that, it doesn't matter much. If your errors aren't normal, what breaks is that you can no longer prove the OLS coefficient is normally distributed. It might follow some other distribution — but it's usually pretty close to normal anyway, unless your errors are really, really weird, in which case you have bigger problems than non normality. Anchor image. A warning light for a fault that almost never grounds the plane. Assumption 2: i.i.d. errors — the one that matters Plain English. Every error comes from the same distribution, and knowing one tells you nothing about another. Precisely. Independent and identically distributed. Identically distributed — your error term, my error term, everyone's error term follows the same distribution. Independent — they're uncorrelated. Knowing my error term tells you nothing about that person's. Three common ways this fails. Failure 1: heteroskedasticity Plain English. The spread of the errors changes as X changes. Precisely. Heteroskedasticity is when the variance of the error term is related to your predictors X. Note the subtlety: you already assumed X is unrelated to the error term itself — but that doesn't mean its variance can't still be related to X. Anchor image. A river that's narrow and calm at the source and wide and choppy at the mouth. Same river, different behaviour along its length. Worked example. The classic "fan" shape: at low values of X there's very little spread around the line — the variance of the residuals, and so probably of the errors, is small. As you move to high values of X, things spread out; the variance grows. Where it breaks. Not all heteroskedastic data looks like a fan. That's one example; other shapes occur too. Failure 2: autocorrelation Plain English. Anything measured repeatedly over time correlates with itself nearby in time. Precisely. With time series data (one observation tracked over time) or panel data (many individuals each measured repeatedly), observations are likely correlated with their nearest periods in time. Anchor image. Today's weather is a decent guess for tomorrow's. Errors behave the same way. Worked example. Fit an OLS line to the unemployment rate over time and it looks bad immediately — partly because there's shape you haven't accounted for, but also because of autocorrelation. Pick any point on the graph: the points on either side of it are likely closer to it than other observations in the data are. If you're above the line this period, you're likely above it next period too. And being above the line is the residual — how far above you are is the residual, which is your representation of the error term. So the error terms are correlated with each other across time. Where it breaks. This shows up essentially any time you have a measure that is repeatedly measured. Failure 3: clustering Plain English. Data collected in groups has errors that come in groups. Precisely. When data is collected hierarchically , observations within a group resemble each other more than they resemble observations in other groups — and so do their residuals, and so do their errors. Anchor image. Families at a restaurant. Knowing what one person ordered tells you a lot about their table and nothing about the next one. Worked example. Students taking a test. Students at the same school probably have more similar scores than students at different schools. And within a school, students in the same classroom — sharing a teacher — are likely more similar still. Once you fit a model and look at t

title: Probit and Logit
one: When your outcome is binary, a straight line predicts probabilities above 1 and below 0 and ignores that there's less room to move near the edges — so you run the same linear **index function** through a **link function** that squashes it i
text: When your outcome is binary, a straight line predicts probabilities above 1 and below 0 and ignores that there's less room to move near the edges — so you run the same linear index function through a link function that squashes it into 0–1, and then report average marginal effects because the coefficients themselves are uninterpretable. OLS quietly assumes a continuous dependent variable. Plenty aren't — counts, bounded variables, and binary outcomes. Run OLS on a binary outcome and you get a linear probability model . Two problems. Problem 1: the line predicts probabilities like 150% or −2. Impossible. Problem 2: it says a nudge moves you 5 points whether you're at 50% or at 99.9%. There's no room at 99.9%. Fix: a generalized linear model. The index function β₀ + β₁x is exactly what OLS does; run it through a link function that maps any number into 0–1. Logit uses the logistic function; probit uses the standard normal CDF. You can't read the coefficients — they describe the index. Report average marginal effects instead. Many economists use the linear probability model anyway, and Nick thinks there's some justification. Everything so far assumed a linear relationship between predictors and outcome, with transformations like polynomials and logarithms available for odd shapes. But sometimes that still doesn't work — and the reason is the dependent variable . There's been a background assumption that your dependent variable is roughly continuous: it can take any value in a range and isn't really restricted. Basic OLS assumes exactly that. Often it's false. You might be counting how many times something happened — an integer that can't be negative. You might have a continuous variable that can't be negative, or can't be positive. And very commonly: a binary dependent variable, 0 or 1. You've used binary variables as predictors; what happens when the thing you're predicting is binary? Were you cured of the disease, yes or no. Did you get into college. Did you move house. There's a whole world of models for different dependent variable types, and this book skips almost all of it — the focus here is causal inference. But probit and logit get a look. The linear probability model, and its two problems Plain English. You can run OLS on a binary outcome. The line just does two silly things. Precisely. Running ordinary least squares with a binary dependent variable gives you a linear probability model — so called because you're treating the probability of the outcome linearly, and OLS is all about linear relationships. Picture the data: every point sits at exactly 1 or exactly 0, nothing between, because the outcome can only be binary. Now fit a straight line through it. Problem 1 — impossible predictions. The line extends beyond 0 and 1. And that line is your prediction of the outcome — with a binary outcome, OLS is trying to give you the probability that the outcome equals 1. So for certain values of X it predicts a probability of 110%, or 150%, or 3,000% if you go far enough out, or −2. None of those are probabilities. Problem 2 — no room at the edges. Suppose the outcome is whether you go to college, and based on your background characteristics I predict a 50% chance. I give you some tutoring, and that raises it to 55%. That five point jump is the slope of the line. Now take someone already at 99.999% . Give them the same tutoring. It might move the needle a little — 99.9% to 99.92%, say — but it obviously cannot add five percentage points. They were already nearly certain. Same change on the X axis, hugely different change in probability. Near the middle, where there's lots of room to move, you should see big effects. Near the edges, where the outcome is essentially guaranteed either way, there's simply no room — so the slope should get flatter . A straight line can't do that. Anchor image. A glass nearly full. Pouring the same amount in adds far less than it did when the glass was half empty — and at some point it just spills. The fix: index function plus link function Plain English. Do the normal linear thing, then squash the answer into the 0–1 range. Precisely. This is a generalized linear model . Look at the equation and you'll recognise most of it: P(y = 1) = f( β₀ + β₁·x ) The inside part — β₀ + β₁x — is the index function . It takes your predictors, multiplies them by coefficients, and adds them together linearly. That is exactly what OLS has been doing all along. The only difference between OLS and a generalized linear model is that you run the index through a link function — that's the f . Why you need one. The index can take any value: make x a very large negative number and the index is very large and negative; make it very positive and the index is very positive. So you need a function you can pass any value into and get back something on the same scale as your dependent variable — here, something between 0 and 1. The two link functions: Model Link function Logit the logistic function Probit the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution The mathematical details are in the textbook chapter. What this buys you. Both problems solved at once: 1. You cannot predict outside 0 to 1 , because the link function can't go above 1 or below 0. 2. The curve flattens near the edges and is steep near the middle, like the OLS line is. So the model says you get big movements where there's room to move and little movement when you're already near certainty. When it matters most. Be most sure to use probit or logit — rather than OLS — when you think the probability of the outcome is close to 100% or close to 0% , rather than in the middle. Why plenty of people use the linear probability model anyway Plain English. Proper modelling costs you elsewhere, and if you only want the slope, it may not be worth it. Precisely. Probit, logit, and other proper modelling of the dependent variable do solve real problems — predictions are better and the slope more accurately accounts for

title: Matching
one: Regression closes a back door by *subtracting out* the variation a confounder explains; matching closes the same back door by **picking a sample that has no variation in it** — build a control group whose confounders look just like the trea
text: Regression closes a back door by subtracting out the variation a confounder explains; matching closes the same back door by picking a sample that has no variation in it — build a control group whose confounders look just like the treated group's, and that confounder cannot possibly be driving your comparison. Closing a back door means removing all variation through the confounder. Regression does it by subtracting out predictions and using residuals. Matching does it by choosing a sample instead. No variation in the confounder → it can't confound. More precisely: make the treated and control groups have no differences in that variable. Vs. regression: matching leans less on linearity and gives more flexibility over which treatment effect average you get. But it demands far more decisions, and results are more sensitive to them. Two approaches: select a matched sample (a subset of controls that look like the treated), or weight the controls so their weighted average looks like the treated group. Running underneath everything: the bias–variance trade off . Better matches or more matches — pick one. Regression is popular in causal inference largely because it's so easy to use it to control for confounders and therefore close back doors. But step back and ask why that works — what are you actually doing when you close a back door? You're removing all variation through the variable on that back door . Take shorts and ice cream: a hot day causes both, so you'd see a positive correlation that isn't causal. Adjust for temperature and what you've done is take all the variation in ice cream eating driven by temperature, and all the variation in shorts wearing driven by temperature, and get rid of both. In regression, that means subtracting out whatever you can predict with temperature and using just the residual. But there are other ways to remove variation associated with a variable . What if you simply picked a sample where there isn't any? What matching is Plain English. Don't adjust the data. Choose data that doesn't have the problem. Precisely. Matching closes a back door — controls for something, removes a confounder — by picking a sample in which there is no variation in that variable. Or more generally, by picking a sample in which the groups you're comparing, typically a treated group against a control group, have no differences in that variable. Anchor image. Two runners and a headwind. Regression estimates the wind and subtracts it from their times. Matching just makes them run the same race. Worked example — the intuition. Take only hot days, and see whether ice cream eating is related to shorts wearing within hot days. Or only cold days. In that sample temperature can't be driving anything, because it isn't varying. Or make the groups match: your treated group has a 70 degree day and a 50 degree day, and your control group also has a 70 degree day and a 50 degree day. Temperature can't be driving any difference between those two groups — they have the exact same temperatures. They're already comparable on that basis. Worked example: the job training program Plain English. Your treated group is 80% men and your control group is 50/50. Fix that by choosing who's in the control group. Worked example. Does a job training program affect whether you get a job? Group Composition Treated (took the program) 80 men, 20 women Control (didn't) 500 men, 500 women You can't compare straight across. If the treated group turned out more likely to get a job, you couldn't tell whether that's the effect of the program or the effect of gender — men and women may have different job getting rates, and there's a big gender imbalance in one group and not the other. That's a back door. The fix: pick 80 men and 20 women out of the control group. Now the gender mix is identical in both groups, so whatever difference remains cannot be because of gender — there isn't a different gender mix anymore. Back door closed. Matching versus regression Plain English. Neither wins. They trade different things. Matching is better at Matching is worse at Not relying so heavily on linearity assumptions — OLS assumes predictors and controls enter linearly; many forms of matching don't care Requiring a lot more decision making More flexibility in estimating different kinds of treatment effect averages (Chapter 10) Results being more sensitive to the particular decisions you make Neither is better than the other — they do different things. And you can combine them in ways the chapter discusses. Approach 1: select a matched sample Plain English. Hand pick control observations that resemble your treated ones. Precisely. Choose a subset of your control observations that are like the treated observations. The job training example is exactly this — take an 80:20 ratio out of the control group. Worked example beyond binary variables. Suppose income also differs between groups. Take a treated person who earned $100,000 before needing a job and is male — find a male control who earned $100,000. Take a treated male who earned $20,000 — find a male control earning $20,000, or at least close to it. You may not get it exactly on the nose, but you can get in the range. Do that across every characteristic you can measure and you end up with two groups that are basically the same on all those control variables, and therefore comparable. If you've matched on enough variables to close all the back doors, any difference in their outcomes can be assigned to the effect of treatment. Approach 2: weighting Plain English. Keep everyone, but make some people count more. Precisely. Construct a weighted average of your control observations such that the weighted average looks like your treatment group. Worked example — what a weighted average is. Two people: one earns $100,000, the other $200,000. Plain average: (100,000 + 200,000) / 2 = $150,000 Weighted , giving the first person double weight: count them twice. (100,000 + 100,000 + 200,000) = 400,000, a

title: Five Questions About Matching
one: Matching demands five decisions — what counts as **close**, whether to **pick matches or use weights**, **how many** matches, **how fast the weights decay**, and **how bad a match is too bad** — and every one of them is the same bias–varian
text: Matching demands five decisions — what counts as close , whether to pick matches or use weights , how many matches, how fast the weights decay , and how bad a match is too bad — and every one of them is the same bias–variance trade off wearing a different hat. Q1 — What's the matching criterion? What does "similar" mean? Easy with one variable (just the difference); hard with several, which needs a distance measure . Q2 — Matches or weights? Pick the closest control observations and discard the rest, or keep many and weight them by closeness. Q3 — If matches, how many? Each extra match is a worse match, but grows the comparison group. Q4 — If weights, how do they decay? How fast, and by what kernel — triangular, Epanechnikov, others. Q5 — What's the worst acceptable match? Beyond some distance you drop the match — and possibly drop the treated observation entirely. Every one is bias versus variance. Some procedures let you skip a question or two; usually you face all five. The goal of matching is a treated group and a control group that are comparable across all the variables you match on. Every time you match on a variable, you close a back door. But how exactly do you do that? There turn out to be five decisions, and this episode walks each one. To keep it tractable it uses a single matching variable — not how you'd normally run a matching study, where you'd match on several, but much simpler to follow. The running example. Data on 30,000 credit card holders in Taiwan in 2005 : their monthly bills, and whether they repaid, for both April and September. The question is the stickiness of credit problems — is being late on your April payment related to being late in September? Role Variable Treatment Were you late with your bill in April? Outcome Were you late with your bill in September? Matching variable The size of your April bill (continuous) Why that back door: bigger April bills mean you're likely to have bigger September bills too, so big bills make you more likely to be late in both. You want to isolate how late you're willing to be rather than simply having difficulty paying. Q1 · What is the matching criterion? Plain English. You have to define what "similar" means before you can find similar people. Precisely. You want people with similar April bill sizes. But you can't require they be exactly the same — if a treated person's bill is 47,432, essentially nobody in the control group has that exact figure, and you'd match nobody at all. So for a continuous variable you must decide what it means to be close . Anchor image. "Nearby" is meaningless until you say whether you're walking or flying. Worked example. With one variable it's straightforward: take the difference. One person's bill is 43,000, another's is 43,001 — a difference of 1, which is very close. The bigger the difference, the more different they are. On the graph, treated observation row 10,305 has two nearby candidates: row 27,719 (a bit lower) and row 27,281 . All three had April bills close to 91,600 , so they're comparable — and you'd pick whichever is closest. Where it breaks. It gets much harder once you add matching variables. If two candidates are similar on one variable but less similar on another, how do you combine those? You need a proper measure of distance — which is the next episode. Q2 · Select a set of matches, or construct weights? Plain English. Keep only the best few, or keep lots and let closeness decide how much they count. Worked example — one to one matching. For treated row 10,305, pick the single closest control observation — row 27,281. Those two are treated as basically the same person except one got treated. Everything else is discarded entirely, greyed out. You could pick the two best matches, or three, but the principle is the same: pick the best, throw away the rest. Worked example — weights. Instead of in or out, count the closest ones more. Picture the control observations as circles: the biggest circles are the ones closest to the treated observation — the control bills most similar to it. As you move further away, the circles shrink; they're being counted less. Past a certain distance you stop counting them entirely — too far to be a good comparison at all. Where it breaks — the trade off. Picking only the best matches gives the lowest bias — the best possible job of closing that back door — but you may have a single control observation, which makes the control group's average very noisy. Weights let worse comparisons creep in, so possibly a slightly worse job on the back door, but you have far more observations to average over and therefore more precision. Q3 · If selecting matches, how many? Plain English. One match, or the best five? Precisely. You could take just one, but that severely limits your control group's sample size. You can take the best two, three, four, five — whatever you want. Anchor image. Going back to the well. The first bucket is the cleanest. Where it breaks. As you go back for the next best match, and the next, you are consecutively picking worse and worse matches — inviting more bias with each one. But you're also building a bigger comparison group, which makes your estimate more precise. Same trade off. Q4 · If using weights, how do they decay with distance? Plain English. Two sub decisions: how fast the weights fall off, and what shape the fall off has. Precisely — how fast. A very good match gets a lot of weight and worse matches get less, but how quickly does that drop? Fast decay → you're essentially only using the best matches. Fewer effective observations, best comparisons. Slow decay → a wide range of observations included, though with small weights. More precision, more bias. Precisely — what shape. You have to give it a function so it knows what decay to apply. That function is a kernel : something maximised at the centre, where you're as close to the treated observation as possible, and getting smaller as you move away. Kernel Shape Triangular stra

title: Distance Matching
one: With several matching variables you need to boil many separate distances down to **one**, which you do by treating observations as points in space and taking the straight line between them — usually the **Mahalanobis distance**, which first
text: With several matching variables you need to boil many separate distances down to one , which you do by treating observations as points in space and taking the straight line between them — usually the Mahalanobis distance , which first divides out the correlations so shared traits aren't counted twice — and the catch is that every dimension you add pushes everyone further apart. One matching variable: distance is trivially the difference between values. Several variables: you need to combine several distances into a single distance to pick a closest match. The trick is a spatial metaphor — distance on each axis collapses into one straight line, and it generalises past three dimensions even though the picture doesn't. Euclidean distance is literally Pythagoras. Mahalanobis distance is more common, because it divides out the correlations between variables so a shared underlying trait isn't double counted. Once you have one distance you can do anything — best match, best three, or weights. Curse of dimensionality: each variable you add is another way to be far apart, so all matches start to look bad. The goal hasn't changed: pick a group of control observations very similar to the treated group across your matching variables, so that when someone asks whether a back door is driving the difference between the groups, you can say no — you matched on it, there's no variation left in it between the groups, so the back door is closed. Distance matching does this by asking, literally, how far apart are two observations on the matching variables. Trivial with one variable. Not trivial with several. The problem: multiple distances Plain English. Two candidates, each closer on a different variable. Which one is closer overall? Worked example — one variable, easy. Match on April credit card bill size. If your bill is 6,000 and mine is 5,900, the difference is 100. To pick the best match for me, find whoever in the control group is closest to 6,000, or build weights around it. Simple. Worked example — two variables, not easy. Now match on bill size and age. Say I'm 35 with a 6,000 bill. Two candidates: Candidate Bill Age A 6,000 — identical 60 — 25 years off B 1,000 — 5,000 off 35 — identical Which is closer to me? You have one distance based on how far apart our bills are and a different distance based on how far apart our ages are — and to pick "whoever is closest", you need a way to combine multiple distances into a single distance , then take whoever has the smallest one. The spatial metaphor Plain English. Treat each observation as a point in space and measure the straight line between them. Precisely. Most methods for boiling multiple distances into one are based on a metaphor with literal distance in the real world. Anchor image. Hold up two fists. There's a distance between them left to right. There's another up and down. And one is further forward than the other. Three separate distances — and yet there is obviously one distance between your fists: the straight line. Worked example. With two matching variables, define the distance along one variable and the distance along the other. Draw a straight line between the two points on that 2D plane and you have a single distance value — and you pick matches by minimising it. With three variables you have three separate distances and still one straight line between the points. And it generalises beyond three dimensions into four, five, six — the visuals break down there, but the maths stays relatively easy. Euclidean and Mahalanobis distance Plain English. Euclidean is plain Pythagoras. Mahalanobis first removes the overlap between your variables. Precisely. Euclidean distance — literally what was just described: take this distance and that distance, apply the Pythagorean theorem, get the distance between the points. Mahalanobis distance — more common in matching. It makes a statistical adjustment that makes more sense in statistical applications: some of those distances are double counting the same thing , so it divides out the correlations between the variables. Worked example. Suppose gender is a back door you want to match on and close — but you can't actually measure gender. What you do have is how much arm hair someone has and whether they have a beard . Both are more common, or take higher values, among men than women. Use both as matching variables and you're treating them as two separate distances — but you're also counting "being a man" twice , because it shows up in both variables. Mahalanobis divides out the correlation between them. Both variables stay in as matching variables, but the shared "man" component only counts once; the double count gets subtracted out. What that leaves you. You've rescaled all the axes to take out the shared elements, so every distance is now orthogonal to the others: here's how much arm hair you have not counting the overlap with facial hair, here's how much facial hair not counting the overlap with arm hair. And once you've subtracted that out, you're back to exactly the same geometric idea — points in space, shortest line between them. What you do with it Plain English. One number per pair, and now every matching option is open. Precisely. For every treated observation you can now calculate the distance to every single control observation. Then do whatever you like: Pick the best match — the shortest distance. Pick the three best matches — the three shortest. Construct weights where the shortest distances get the biggest weights, decaying as distance grows. The key is that you now have a single measure of distance , and that's what makes any of it possible. The curse of dimensionality Plain English. Every variable you add is another way for two people to be far apart. Precisely. The curse of dimensionality afflicts methods that are very sensitive to added dimensions — and here they're literal dimensions. One matching variable, one dimension. Two variables, two dimensions. Three, four, five. As the number of

title: Propensity Score Matching
one: You don't need to close off everything a confounder does — only the part that **drives treatment** — so predict each person's **probability of being treated**, collapse all your matching variables into that single number, and weight by its
text: You don't need to close off everything a confounder does — only the part that drives treatment — so predict each person's probability of being treated , collapse all your matching variables into that single number, and weight by its inverse. The goal was never to control the variables. It was to close the back doors . Confounders do many things; only part of what they do is affect treatment. Close off that part and you've done the job. The propensity score is the probability someone gets treated, usually predicted with a probit or logit on the matching variables. Increasingly with machine learning too — it's a prediction problem. Because it collapses everything into one dimension , there's no curse of dimensionality. But the regression brings the linearity assumption back. Don't pick matches on it — that has known statistical problems. Use inverse probability weighting . Weight treated observations by 1/p and controls by 1/(1−p): the least likely treated who were , and the most likely treated who weren't . Watch for extreme scores (trim them), common support , and balance — then go back and re estimate. Distance matching works, but it collapses under many matching variables. And step back to why you're doing any of this: you want the causal effect of X on Y, back door variables are driving the correlation you observe, and you want to control for that. But here's the insight. Closing all those back doors doesn't require closing off all the variation in those variables. That was never the goal — the goal is to close the back doors. Those confounders do all sorts of things, and only part of what they do is affect treatment. If you can close off the part of those variables that determines whether somebody is treated, and control for just that, you've done your job. The propensity score Plain English. One number per person: how likely they were to get treated. Precisely. The propensity score is the probability that somebody is going to be treated. Propensity score matching predicts your propensity for being treated from the matching variables, then uses that probability to find who is most like you in terms of how likely you were to be treated — because controlling for that is the real thing you were trying to do with all these back door closures. You don't have to control for all the individual variables, so long as you can control for the propensity score. Anchor image. You don't need to know everything about a lock. Only which key opens it. How it's estimated. Typically with a probit or logit model: take whether or not you were treated, regress it on the set of matching variables, and use that to predict the probability you were treated. That's your estimated propensity score. Probit and logit aren't the only options — increasingly you'll see machine learning applications predicting the propensity score, which makes sense: this is a prediction problem, and prediction is what machine learning is good at. But probit and logit remain the most common. What using a regression costs and buys Plain English. The linearity you escaped comes back — but the dimensionality problem goes away. Precisely. Cost: one of the good things about matching was that it doesn't lean on linearity assumptions the way regression does. Now you're running a regression, so that assumption comes back in. Benefit: you can take a large number of matching variables and combine them in a way that handles high dimensionality far better than distance matching. You don't get the curse of dimensionality in the same way , because you're collapsing all the matching variables together — you end up back in a single dimension , and one dimension can't suffer a curse of dimensionality. Don't pick matches — use inverse probability weighting Plain English. Matching people with similar propensity scores is the obvious move, and it's the wrong one. Precisely. The obvious approach: you have an 80% chance of being treated and you were treated, so find someone untreated with a 79% or 81% chance and call them your best match. But it's been shown that using a propensity score to pick a set of matches has statistical issues — it doesn't work all that well. So of the two matching approaches from Ep 35, use weighting . Inverse probability weighting: Group Weight Consequence Treated inverse of the probability of being treated — 1/p Those most likely to be treated get the lowest weight Control inverse of the probability of not being treated — 1/(1−p) Those least likely to be treated get the lowest weight Why that direction. The control group didn't get treated. So if you want treated observations that resemble the control group, you want the people who were least likely to get treated — and they get the heaviest weights. Symmetrically, the treated group did get treated, so among the untreated you want people who were most likely to be treated and yet weren't — and they get the heaviest weights. You end up comparing the people least likely to be treated who were against the people most likely to be treated who weren't — and those are likely to be pretty decent comparisons for each other. Anchor image. Two crowds you want to make resemble each other. Turn up the volume on the unusual people in each and turn it down on the typical ones. Difficulty 1: extreme propensity scores Plain English. Dividing by a probability near zero blows up. Precisely. Inverse probability weighting runs into problems when the probability of being treated is near one or near zero: Near zero probability of treatment but you were treated → weight is something like 1 ÷ 0, which you can't do. Near one probability but you weren't treated → weight is 1 ÷ almost zero. Same problem. The fix: trim the propensity score — drop people with very high or very low probabilities of being treated. Difficulty 2: common support Plain English. Is there actually anybody on the other side who resembles these people? Precisely. You're trying to find the control observations most like the treated g

title: Coarsened Exact Matching and Entropy Balancing
one: Distance and propensity score matching can leave you with groups that still don't look alike, sending you back to try again — so two **non-iterative** methods guarantee the result up front: **coarsened exact matching** allows zero distance
text: Distance and propensity score matching can leave you with groups that still don't look alike, sending you back to try again — so two non iterative methods guarantee the result up front: coarsened exact matching allows zero distance after binning continuous variables, and entropy balancing finds weights that force the moments you asked for to be equal. Distance and propensity score matching tend to fail — you run them and the matched groups still don't resemble each other, so you iterate. Non iterative methods avoid the guess and check loop entirely. Coarsened exact matching: distance matching where the worst acceptable match has zero distance . Exactly the same on every matching variable. Coarsened because continuous variables are first cut into bins , so exact matching is possible at all.
