It works without checking — but it drops people ruthlessly , and the curse of dimensionality hits extra hard. Entropy balancing: you name variables and moments (means, variances…) you want equal across groups, and it finds weights that satisfy them. Uncommon and relatively new, but Nick thinks it has a promising future. Both methods so far share a flaw: they have a tendency to fail. You perform the matching and end up with a set of matched observations that doesn't actually look all that much alike. Then you go back, change something about how you matched, or change your propensity score estimation model, and do it again — over and over until you get a decent set of matches. The goal, remember, is a set of treated and control observations that look pretty similar, whether after matching or after applying weights. Thankfully some methods don't require the guess and check loop. Coarsened exact matching Plain English. Distance matching where the only acceptable distance is zero. Precisely. Exact matching allows no differences at all between observations on a matching variable. Coarsened exact matching is a form of distance matching where the worst acceptable match has zero distance — exactly the same on all matching variables. Anchor image. A key that either turns or doesn't. There's no "nearly fits." Worked example. You're studying a job training program, matching on state, sex, and education level. Nick is 35, lives in Washington, and has a PhD. He'll only accept as comparable someone who is also 35, also lives in Washington, and also has a PhD . Anyone else isn't a match at all. And if nobody on the other side of treatment is like that, he gets dropped — he simply doesn't get to be in the data. Where exact matching also shows up. You can incorporate it into other methods. In distance matching you might say: you can be a bit further apart on the size of your bill, I want the shortest distance there — but you have to match exactly on which state you live in. Coarsened exact matching just says you must match exactly on everything. The "coarsened" part Plain English. Continuous variables get chopped into bins first, or nobody would ever match. Precisely. Exact matching on a continuous variable is impossible — if your bill is $9,132, nobody else has exactly that. So before exact matching, continuous variables are coarsened into bins . Instead of a bill of 9,132 you have a bill in the range 9,000 to 10,000 , and you can match with anyone else in that bin even though the exact numbers differ. Why it needs no checking. You know before you run it that you'll get treated and control sets that are exactly the same on average, because you aren't allowing any differences. Any difference at all and you're chucked from the sample. The cost: it drops people ruthlessly Plain English. Being slightly unusual on any single variable gets you deleted. Precisely. The curse of dimensionality works extra hard here. How many people in your data are 35, in Washington, with a PhD, and on the other side of treatment from you? There might be nobody. And it might be very common for there to be nobody — not just for 35 year olds, but even once you bin to 30 to 40, and even once you coarsen education to "bachelor's degree or higher." It gets worse with every matching variable you add. Imagine matching on nine things — finding someone exactly the same as you on nine different aspects of your life would be pretty amazing. Consequences: you drop a lot of people, giving very imprecise results — and possibly biased ones, because you're dropping people who are merely slightly unusual on even one aspect. Practice. When using coarsened exact matching, check how many observations got dropped . If it's a lot, either use a different method or do some iterating after all — make bigger bins, or remove some matching variables. Where it works well. Coarsened exact matching simply doesn't work when you have to match on many variables; there's no way to match someone on nine, ten, fifteen, twenty elements, so if you need to close a lot of back doors it may not do it for you. But if the variables you're matching on are broad enough that you'll find plenty of matches, it can do a very good job — or if you simply have a dataset with millions and millions of observations, you may find matches on all nine elements anyway. Entropy balancing Plain English. State what you want to be equal, and it solves for weights that make it so. Precisely. The least common of the four approaches — distance matching with Mahalanobis, propensity score matching, coarsened exact matching, and this. It's relatively new and relatively underused. You pick a set of variables and a set of moments — means, variances, skewness, kurtosis, and so on down the list — and declare that you want those moments to be the same across your two groups. Worked example. Your list of requirements might be: The mean age should be the same between treatment and control. The variance of age should be the same between treatment and control. The proportion of people living in Washington should be the same. The proportion with a PhD should be the same. You can enforce that averages match across matching

title: When Matching Goes Wrong
one: Matching rests on three things — the **conditional independence assumption** (you matched on enough to close every back door), **common support** (there is actually somebody on the other side to match to), and **balance** (the matching did
text: Matching rests on three things — the conditional independence assumption (you matched on enough to close every back door), common support (there is actually somebody on the other side to match to), and balance (the matching did what you asked) — and the last two you can and must check. Conditional independence assumption: treatment is essentially random once you condition on your matching variables. This is "close all the back doors" wearing a matching shaped hat. Common support: you need substantial overlap in the matching variables' distributions between groups. If nobody untreated looks like the treated, there's nothing to match to. The usual fix — dropping areas of non common support — works, but can delete the most typical members of your treated group and leave an unrepresentative estimate. Balance: after matching, did the groups actually end up similar? Check with a balance table of means. Means aren't enough. Two distributions can share a mean and look nothing alike. Check distributions too. If balance is bad, go back and redo the matching. That's legitimate. Several episodes of how to match, and almost nothing on the assumptions that make matching appropriate — or what you can check to see whether things went well. Three things: one assumption you have to make, and two you can actually inspect. 1 · The conditional independence assumption Plain English. You matched on enough variables to close every back door. Precisely. You pick matching variables and match the treated and control groups on them, because you want the differences between those groups to be driven by the effect of treatment rather than by other variables. The conditional independence assumption says you've achieved that: you matched on all the variables needed to close all the back doors, so treatment is basically random conditional on the matching variables you selected . Where it breaks. It's another name for something you already know. Identify an effect → close all the back doors. In the matching context that's the conditional independence assumption; in the regression context it's getting rid of omitted variable bias. Lots of different words for the same idea. 2 · Common support Plain English. There has to be somebody on the other side who looks like you. Precisely. For matching to work you need substantial overlap in the distributions of the variables you're matching on. Look at the distribution of a matching variable in the treated group and in the control group: there must be at least some ranges where you see plenty of people in both , or you can't match on that variable. Anchor image. A dating app where everyone in your city is already in a relationship. The app works fine. There's just nobody to match you with. Worked example — the job training program. Suppose the program deliberately targets one neighbourhood — Capitol Hill in Seattle, where Nick's college is — and recruits everyone there. Neighbourhood is on a back door, so you'd like to match on it. But if everybody in Capitol Hill got recruited , there's nobody in Capitol Hill who wasn't treated to match to. And symmetrically there's nobody treated outside Capitol Hill to match to the untreated group. You can't match on neighbourhood at all. Worked example — the Broockman study. Broockman sent fictionalised requests for help to legislators, using an email name that sounded like it belonged to a Black person — a fictional Tyrone Washington . The question: if you're a legislator and the email comes from outside your district , do you respond? There's no inherent benefit — they can't vote for you. And does the response rate differ between Black and non Black legislators? Treatment: whether the legislator is Black. The back door: Black legislators are more likely to be elected in areas with many Black residents, so the percentage of Black people in the district sits on a back door between the legislator's race and, for other reasons, whether they respond. Now look at the distributions of percent Black in district: Group Where they're concentrated Non Black legislators The vast majority in districts without many Black residents Black legislators Very likely in districts 50–75% Black Very different distributions. There may be common support at relatively low percentages — but in the 50–75% range, where most Black legislators are , there simply aren't enough non Black legislators to compare against. That's a failure of common support exactly where it hurts. Common support for the propensity score. The same requirement applies to propensity scores. To compare treated to untreated while adjusting for the propensity of being treated, you need some treated people who had a decent probability of not being treated, and some untreated people who had a decent probability of being treated. In Broockman's data the propensity score distributions for Black and non Black legislators are very disjoint — there isn't much overlap where you could say "here's a Black legislator, and here's a non Black legislator with a similar probability." The fix, and its cost. Drop the observations for which there's no common support — dropping areas of non common support . You might drop all the Black legislators in 50–75% Black districts, because there's no good match for them; or drop everybody above a certain propensity score, because only the low range is comparable. This fixes the statistical problem: better matches for whoever remains. But you're dropping a lot of observations — and here, specifically, the most common kind of Black legislator . So you've solved one problem and introduced another: how representative is your new estimate of the actual distribution? Propensity score trimming is the same logic. Drop very low and very high scores, because you're unlikely to find matches: someone with a 90% chance of being treated probably was treated, so there won't be many controls up there; someone with a 0.05% chance won't have many treated observations to compare to. 3 · Balance P

title: Treatment Effect Estimation with Matching
one: After all that matching, estimation is almost trivial — take the weighted average of the treated group, the weighted average of the control group, and compare — with the real subtleties sitting in **standard errors** (let the software do th
text: After all that matching, estimation is almost trivial — take the weighted average of the treated group, the weighted average of the control group, and compare — with the real subtleties sitting in standard errors (let the software do them) and in doubly robust estimators that survive getting either your matching model or your regression model wrong. Estimation: weighted average of the treated, weighted average of the control, compare. That's it. Which average you get depends on which direction you matched: control to treated gives the ATT , treated to control gives the ATUT , and combining them by the proportion treated gives the ATE . Standard errors are hard. The matching procedure itself injects uncertainty. A plain weighted regression gives standard errors that are too small . Let your software handle them — or use bootstrap standard errors, redoing the whole matching each resample. You can combine matching with regression : apply your matching weights inside a regression to get interactions and everything else regression is good at. Doubly robust estimators are right if either the matching model or the outcome model is correct. Both wrong and you're still in trouble. Odd to leave estimation to the very end of a chapter — except that once you've done the matching, it's relatively simple and straightforward. All the difficult questions were the matching questions. The actual estimation is quite a lot easier. The estimate itself Plain English. Average the treated, average the control, subtract. Precisely. Take your set of matched observations, or the weights you generated for your weighted matched sample, and calculate the weighted average of the outcome for the treated group and the weighted average for the control group. Compare those two. Done. Since you've already done the matching, the resulting comparison gives you the effect of treatment on the outcome. Anchor image. All the work was in setting the table. Serving dinner takes a minute. Which treatment effect average you get Plain English. It depends which group you made resemble which. Precisely. What you matched What you get Matched the control group to look like the treatment group Average treatment on the treated (ATT) Matched the treated group to look like the control group Average treatment on the untreated (ATUT) Both, averaged together by the proportion of people treated Average treatment effect (ATE) There are also ways to choose your inverse probability weights specifically to obtain the kind of treatment effect average you're after. Standard errors: hand them to the software Plain English. Matching adds uncertainty of its own, and naive standard errors miss it. Precisely. Standard errors with matching are tougher than in regression modelling. Nick's advice is essentially: don't listen to him on this — pick software that does matching estimation and let it calculate the standard errors for you , because a lot of difficult things go on in those calculations. The reason: the matching procedure itself injects uncertainty that you need to account for. If you simply do the comparison — regress the outcome on treatment using your matching weights — it will not properly account for all the uncertainty that went into the matching process, and your standard errors will be too small . Anchor image. Timing a race with a stopwatch you also had to build. The stopwatch's own error belongs in the result. The exception: bootstrap standard errors Plain English. Resample your data over and over, redoing the whole procedure each time. Precisely. Bootstrapping means randomly drawing a new sample from your data many times over. Every single time, you redo the entire matching process and the entire estimation process , and get an estimate. Do that repeatedly and you're mimicking the sampling distribution yourself. Why it works here: because you redo the matching every time, it incorporates the uncertainty of the matching process into your estimate. So you get the sampling distribution as a whole and can calculate your own standard errors. Where it breaks. This works as long as you're using a weighted approach. A procedure where you're picking samples to be in or out of the control group doesn't work so well with bootstrap standard errors. Technical details are in the chapter. Combining matching with regression Plain English. Matching weights slot straight into a regression, so you get regression's conveniences too. Precisely. You don't have to use matching by itself. Combine it with regression to do the things regression is good at. Worked example. Suppose you want an interaction term — how the effect varies across groups. That's awkward with a plain weighted average, and very easy in a regression. So just add your matching weights into the regression model and you're set. Broockman did exactly this in the legislator study: he didn't just take a weighted average with his matched group. He took his matching weights and applied them to a regression model that had an interaction term in it . Doubly robust estimators Plain English. Two chances to be right. Get either model correct and you're fine. Precisely. You could misspecify two different things: Your matching procedure — picking the wrong variables, or the wrong functional form. This is a model of how treatment gets assigned . Your regression model — the wrong functional form, matching variables in the wrong place, a missing interaction term. This is a model of the outcome . A doubly robust estimator gives you the right answer as long as at least one of those is correct . If one is wrong, you're okay — and you won't necessarily know which one saved you. If both are wrong, you're still in trouble. Anchor image. A backup system. If one fails, the other catches you. Both fail and you fall. Where it breaks. Double robustness isn't a method — it's a property that certain estimators have. Several estimators are doubly robust; some are harder to implement, some easier. Inverse probability weightin

title: Simulation
one: Generate your own data with a random number generator, run your method on it, and check whether you get back the answer you built in — because you **chose the truth**, which almost never happens with real data.
text: Generate your own data with a random number generator, run your method on it, and check whether you get back the answer you built in — because you chose the truth , which almost never happens with real data. Simulation = generate data yourself, apply your method, look at the result. Reason 1: find out whether a method works , without writing a proof. Reason 2: find out when it breaks , and how badly. Reason 3 — the big one: you pick the truth. You know what the right answer is, so you know whether the method found it. Method: start with data satisfying every assumption, confirm it works, then break things one at a time . Some broken assumptions barely matter. Some ruin everything. Simulation is how you tell which. The book has code in three languages; the videos stay generic. Simulation is an important tool to have in the applied statistician's work belt — and it answers a question that's been hanging over this whole series. You've been told a lot about what certain methods do and don't do, when they perform well and when they don't. How would you check any of that for yourself? You could write a statistical proof, working out the estimator's properties in mathematical terms. That sounds very difficult, and it isn't especially intuitive — you can reach the answer and still not understand it on a gut level unless you genuinely think in maths. Simulation is something you can do yourself. What simulation is Plain English. Make up data on purpose, run your method on it, see what comes out. Precisely. Generate your own data, typically using some kind of random number generator, then apply your methods to that data and look at the result. Reason 1 and 2: does it work, and when does it break? Plain English. Not just "is this method good" but "how wrong does it go when I violate its assumptions?" Worked example. You've been told regression gives you the causal estimate if you've controlled for everything needed to remove omitted variable bias. But what if you haven't? How bad is the bias going to be? Is it worse or better if the omitted variable is strongly related? Weakly ? Negatively ? Positively ? What if it's more related to the treatment than the outcome? More related to the outcome than the treatment? Every one of those is a question simulation can answer. Reason 3: you pick the truth Plain English. You built the data, so you know the right answer. Real data never tells you that. Precisely. Because you're generating the data yourself, you know what the right answer is — which is rarely the case with actual data. So when you apply your method, you know what it should return if it's working properly. If you get something else, you know either the method is broken or the data you gave it isn't a good setting to apply that method in. Anchor image. Calibrating a thermometer in ice water. You already know it's 0°C — that's the entire point. The method: start clean, then break things Plain English. Confirm it works when everything is perfect, then sabotage one thing at a time. Precisely. Start with a dataset that satisfies all the assumptions the estimator has — the model is linear, you've controlled for everything — and check that it works there. If it does, start breaking things one at a time. Make one of the true model features non linear, and see whether a linear model is really wrong, or whether it seems to work okay anyway. Where it breaks — in both directions. There are a lot of cases in statistics where you can break an assumption and it makes things wrong, but only by a little bit . Simulation is a good way to ferret those out. There are also cases where breaking something messes things up a really great deal , and simulation finds those too. Nick's own practice. He runs a simulation whenever he faces a new method he isn't sure about, or just wants to try something out. Sometimes he has an idea for an estimator and doesn't want to do all the work of writing a proof — so a simulation is a first attempt to see whether it seems to work at all. He does this quite regularly. A note on code Simulation is inherently something that involves a lot of coding, and these videos have deliberately avoided code. The book contains all the coding examples — in three different languages , which is why the videos don't reproduce them. For the actual implementation, go to Chapter 15 and find your preferred language. Simulation is for people who can't do the maths → Actually, it's a legitimate alternative to a proof, and it's what Nick reaches for before committing to writing one. The point of simulation is to test a method on realistic data → Actually, the point is that you chose the truth, so you can tell whether the method recovered it. If a method fails on simulated data, the method is bad → Actually, it may instead mean that data isn't a good setting for that method. Both are worth knowing. Breaking an assumption invalidates a method → Actually, plenty of violations make results wrong only slightly. Finding out which is exactly what simulation is for. Start by simulating the messy case you actually care about → Actually, start where every assumption holds and confirm the method works, then break things one at a time. Here's a question that should have been bothering you this whole time: I keep telling you what methods do and when they work — how would you ever check any of that yourself? One option is to write a proof. Difficult, and honestly not that enlightening unless you genuinely think in maths. The other option is simulation , and it's something you can just do. The idea is simple: you generate the data yourself , with a random number generator, then run your method on it and see what comes out. And the reason that's powerful is the bit people miss. You pick the truth. You built the data, so you know the real answer — which basically never happens with real data. So when your method spits out a number, you can just check it. If it doesn't match what you put in, either the method is broken or that'

title: Running a Simulation
one: Five steps, in a loop: **choose a data generating process** whose truth you know, **generate a sample**, **apply your estimator**, **store the estimate**, and **repeat many times** — then check whether the mean of that sampling distribution
text: Five steps, in a loop: choose a data generating process whose truth you know, generate a sample , apply your estimator , store the estimate , and repeat many times — then check whether the mean of that sampling distribution comes back to the truth you put in. Step 1 — pick the data generating process. You choose it, so you know the truth. Step 2 — generate a sample from it. Step 3 — apply your estimator to that sample. Step 4 — store the estimate. One iteration done. Step 5 — repeat many, many times , building a whole distribution of estimates. Then compare the mean of that sampling distribution to the truth. Match → the estimator works in this context. Mismatch → it doesn't. For causal work, build a causal effect in by using one variable to generate another . Demonstrated entirely with a coin, pencil, and paper — no code. You have an estimator in mind — a regression done a particular way, matching done a particular way, whatever — and you want to know when and how well it works. Here's how to structure a simulation that tells you. No code here, because the book covers several languages. Instead: a coin as the random data generator, and the results written down. It works exactly the same way — the sample just won't be very big, because nobody's here all day. Step 1: choose the data generating process Plain English. You decide what's true. That's the whole trick. Precisely. Use a random number generator to produce data with whatever properties you want. You decide the data generating process, so that you know the truth . Worked example. The coin: choose one with a 50/50 chance of heads. So the true average is 50% heads — that's the truth. Whatever estimation method you use later should give back 50% on average. If it doesn't, the estimation method is bad. How to build in a causal effect. For causal inference you generally want a causal effect in there that you'll then try to recover. Do it by using one variable to generate another : 1. Generate a random variable — call it x — say from a random normal distribution. 2. Generate y from a random normal distribution whose mean is x . Now x causes y. A one unit increase in x should be associated with a one unit increase in y — and when you estimate later, you hope the relationship you get back out is also one . Where it breaks. The coin demo deliberately has no causal effect in it; it's a plain statistical estimation. But the structure is identical. Step 2: generate a sample Worked example. Flip the coin six times. The result: tails, heads, heads, tails, heads, heads — four heads and two tails. Step 3: apply your estimator Plain English. Run whatever method you're testing on that sample. Worked example. Normally you'd estimate the proportion of heads by, well, taking the proportion of heads. But the point is to test an estimator, so test a strange one: take the proportion of heads and multiply it by two thirds. No particular reason to think it works — that's what you're checking. Proportion of heads = 4/6. Multiply by 2/3 → 8/18 , a bit under half. That's the estimate. Step 4: store it Precisely. Write the estimate down. That's one complete iteration of the simulation procedure. Step 5: repeat, many times Plain English. One run tells you nothing. Do it over and over. Worked example. Three more rounds of six flips each, applying the same estimator: Round Heads Proportion Estimate (× 2/3) 1 4 of 6 4/6 8/18 ≈ 0.444 2 4 of 6 4/6 8/18 ≈ 0.444 3 3 of 6 1/2 1/3 ≈ 0.333 4 3 of 6 1/2 1/3 ≈ 0.333 Now look at the distribution of estimates — here, just take their average: Average ≈ 38.9% The comparison Plain English. Line the simulation's answer up against the truth you built in. Worked example. The truth is 50% — you chose a fair coin. The estimator says you get heads about 39% of the time. Those are different, so the estimation method does not work . Where it breaks. Four simulations isn't nearly enough — you'd want a thousand. But you'd still get something around 38%, so the conclusion holds. The general rule: run the whole procedure many, many times to get a distribution of estimates from all your random samples. Look at that sampling distribution . If its mean matches the truth you built in, the estimator seems to be working in this context. If it doesn't, it doesn't. Why deliberately test a broken estimator Plain English. He picked one he knew would fail, to show you what failure looks like. Precisely. Obviously if you want to know how often a coin comes up heads, you take the proportion of times it's heads — the ×2/3 estimator was chosen because it's wrong. The demonstration is that it returns a wrong answer, and that's what you're looking for : a result that doesn't line up with the truth tells you the method is wrong and you need a different one. Where it breaks — the other outcome is interesting too. Sometimes the simulation shows the method does work. It might have turned out that multiplying by two thirds doesn't mess up the answer, and that would have been an interesting thing to find out. (It did.) And sometimes you break an assumption, do something that seems wrong, and it's only a little bit wrong — and maybe that's fine. That's also a valuable result from a simulation. Run the simulation once and check the answer → Actually, a single iteration tells you nothing. You need many, to build a sampling distribution. The estimator is wrong if any individual estimate misses the truth → Actually, you compare the mean of the sampling distribution to the truth. Individual samples vary by construction. To simulate a causal effect you need special tools → Actually, you just use one variable to generate another — draw x, then draw y with mean x, and x causes y. You need real looking data for the simulation to be informative → Actually, a coin and a piece of paper demonstrate the entire procedure. Finding that your estimator works means it always works → Actually, it means it works in this context — in the data generating process you built. A simulation

title: Power Analysis
one: Power analysis asks whether you have enough firepower to detect what you're looking for — you fix four of **true effect size, variation in treatment, other variation in the outcome, desired precision, and sample size**, and solve for the fi
text: Power analysis asks whether you have enough firepower to detect what you're looking for — you fix four of true effect size, variation in treatment, other variation in the outcome, desired precision, and sample size , and solve for the fifth — and if the answer says you need 100,000 observations and you have 5,000, don't run the study . Statistics is not magical. Tiny effects , little variation in treatment , lots of noise , and high evidence standards all make its job harder. An underpowered study gives imprecise estimates, probably misses a real effect — and if it does find one, that's likely a fluke . Power analysis takes five quantities, holds four fixed, and solves for the fifth. Most common: solve for minimum necessary sample size , or for minimum detectable effect size . Calculators handle simple cases like a two group randomized trial. Simulation handles everything else. Simulation method: the usual loop, wrapped in another loop over different sample sizes or effect sizes. Statistics is not magical, and it's bad at some things. Four situations make its job harder: Tiny effects. Want to know whether a parachute saves your life if you jump out of a plane? One or two observations will do it. Want to know whether a pill makes your hair grow a fraction of a centimetre longer? You'll need an enormous number. Tiny effects are simply very difficult to spot. Not much variation. Statistics is all about studying variation — if there isn't any, there's nothing to figure out. Does going to the moon make you smarter? Only a couple of people have been. There's just not enough variation in that treatment to study, at least not statistically. Noise relative to signal. Does the kind of pencil you use affect your exam score? Suppose it genuinely does — a nicer pencil helps a little. But an awful lot of other things determine exam performance, and all that other stuff makes the real effect hard to see. High evidence standards. If you just want a general idea of how much the pencil helps, a certain sample might do. If you want to be really precise, you'll need a much bigger one. The question: is it too hard? Plain English. Do you have enough data to answer this at all — or should you go home? Precisely. You don't want to perform a study if you simply don't have the ability to estimate the thing you want to estimate. Trying with too small a sample is a bad idea: You'll get very imprecise estimates . There's a decent chance you won't find an effect even if one truly exists . And here's the nasty one: if you do find an effect, there's a decent chance it's a fluke . With a very tiny sample, managing to reject the null pretty much only happens when you happen to get weird data. Anchor image. Fishing with a net whose holes are bigger than the fish. Catching one tells you less than you'd think. Where it breaks. So it's a good idea to know you have enough sample size before you start. The five quantities Plain English. Fix four, solve for the fifth. Precisely. Power analysis looks at five things: Quantity The question True effect size How big is the actual effect you're trying to study? Variation in treatment Do you have plenty of treated and untreated people, or only a couple treated? The more treatment moves around, the easier it is to see whether it does anything. Other variation in the outcome How much else is going on — all the determinants of the test score other than the pencil? Statistical precision If there truly is an effect, do you want to reject the null 80% of the time? 90%? Do you want standard errors below a certain size? Sample size How much data do you have to work with? Set four constant, and it tells you how big the fifth needs to be. The two common uses: Minimum necessary sample size. How many observations do you need to hit your precision goal, given a certain true effect size and the variation involved? Minimum detectable effect size. You know your sample size — how big does the true effect have to be for you to detect it? With 30 observations you can only detect genuinely huge effects; with a million, you can pick up much smaller ones with decent precision. You could solve for the other three, but these two are far and away the most common. Why do it with simulation Plain English. Calculators handle the textbook case. Simulation handles yours. Precisely. Power analysis calculators exist and will do the work for you — but only in the settings whose designers thought about them ahead of time. A simple randomized controlled experiment with two groups and random assignment to one or the other? The calculator will handle that. But in observational data, where you want to incorporate complex features — the hierarchical structure of your data, or the complex ways treatment gets assigned — you'll want a simulation. Simulation lets you incorporate all the features of your actual data generating process. The procedure Plain English. The normal simulation loop, with a second loop wrapped around it. Precisely. Steps 1–4 are the same as any simulation: 1. Create data with your setting in place — how you think the data is generated, the true effect size you picked, the variation in treatment, the other variation in the outcome, how much noise you have to deal with. 2. Apply the estimation method you're planning to use. 3. Store the estimate — and if you're looking at statistical power , meaning how often you reject a false null, store whether you rejected the null . 4. Repeat many times to get the distribution of estimates, or the rejection rate. Step 5 is what makes it a power analysis: take that entire iteration procedure and wrap another iteration around it , running the whole thing again with different sample sizes , or different true effect sizes , and see what changes. Worked example. Suppose the true effect is that changing your pencil raises your test score by 5 points . Sample size True effect Result 100 students +5 points Reject the null 70% of the time 1,000 students +5 points ? 10,000 stu

title: Fixed Effects
one: If you can't measure a confounder but it sits inside a broader category you *can* identify — like your birthplace sitting inside **you** — then controlling for that category controls for everything inside it, which is why controlling for th
text: If you can't measure a confounder but it sits inside a broader category you can identify — like your birthplace sitting inside you — then controlling for that category controls for everything inside it, which is why controlling for the individual strips out all the between variation and leaves only the within . New problem: the diagram tells you what to control for, and you don't have the data . The insight: if an unmeasured variable is constant within a broader category you do observe, control for the category and you've controlled for the variable. Requires panel data — the same individuals measured repeatedly over time. People, companies, countries. Controlling for the individual controls for everything unique to them that doesn't change over time . This splits variation in two: between (differences across individuals) and within (changes for one individual over time). Fixed effects removes all the between variation , leaving only within. So back doors made of time constant things close — even ones you could never measure. Time varying back doors stay open. This starts a whole section of the book on research designs that solve one particular problem. So far it's been ways to control for variables — regression, matching, drawing causal diagrams that tell you what needs controlling. And there's a very, very common problem with all of it, especially in social science: you draw the diagram, it tells you what to control for, you go to set up your regression or your matching estimator — and you realise you don't actually have data on it . Thankfully there are methods that let you estimate a causal effect even when you can't measure everything you need to control for. They only work in particular settings, so you have to get a little lucky and be clever about applying them. But if you can apply them, they work — and depending on who you ask, some people think they're the only good way to get causal effects from observational data when you can't run an experiment. The key insight Plain English. You can't measure the thing — but you can measure the box it lives in. Precisely. If you need to control for a variable, and you can't measure it, but you do have data on a broader category that the variable sits inside — and that category explains all the variation in that variable — then you can control for the broader category and it controls for everything inside it. Anchor image. You can't weigh the individual ingredients after they're baked in. You can weigh the cake. Worked example. You have panel data : the same measurement on the same people over time — you this year, last year, the year before. It works for individual people, companies, countries: New Zealand this year, New Zealand last year, and so on. Say the variable you need to control for is your birthplace . Your birthplace both affects whether you were treated and affects your outcome — a back door. And you don't have data on it. But you do have data on you . And your birthplace is fixed within the category of you . So if you could somehow control for your identity, you'd be controlling for everything unique to you that doesn't change over time — including your birthplace, even though you can't measure it. That's fixed effects: add a control for the individual, and in doing so control for all the things unique to that individual that are constant over time. Worked example: chancellor visits and trade Plain English. Bundle everything unchanging about a country into one variable called "country." Worked example. Does a recent visit from the Chancellor of Germany make your country trade more with Germany? Plenty of other things determine how much you trade: Variable Why it's a back door Can you measure it? Geography Closer to Germany, more trade Sort of History with Germany Prior trading history affects both whether the chancellor visits and future trade How do you put a number on it? Culture of the population A German speaking country, or shared heritage, trades more How do you measure a population's culture? Current politics Is your country angry at Germany or friendly with it? Maybe Several of those are genuinely hard to measure. But notice something: some of them are constant within country over time . Geography isn't changing much — Austria has had the same geography for decades. History with Germany is an ever evolving thing, but go back far enough and the history you had a hundred years ago isn't changing from year to year. So take all of those and bundle them into a single variable: country . dag node C 0 0 "Country" unobs node V 1.6 0.9 "Chancellor visit" treat node T 3.2 0.9 "Trade with Germany" out node P 1.6 0.3 "Current politics" edge C V edge C T edge C P edge P V edge P T edge V T note 1.6 1.7 "geography + history + culture, all constant within country" Move between countries and these things change — different countries have different geographies, different histories with Germany. But stick with a single country and there's no variation left in them . Austria's geography doesn't vary from year to year, at least not on the time scale of your data. That diagram is much easier to work with. Control for which country you are and you close all those back doors at once. Where it breaks. It doesn't close everything. Current politics does change from year to year — Austria's relationship with Germany shifts — so that back door stays open and you'd need to control for it separately. Fixed effects gets a lot of your work done; it doesn't finish the job. Between and within variation Plain English. Differences across people versus changes within one person over time. Precisely. Controlling for the individual separates your variation into two forms: Between variation — variation between individuals. Austria has different geography than England does. Within variation — variation within an individual over time. Austria's political relationship with Germany changes from year to year. A variable can have both . Politics has a between compo

title: Estimating Fixed Effects
one: A fixed effect is just a **categorical control variable for the individual** — mathematically identical to any other categorical control, and different only in *why* you added it — so you can estimate it by literally adding that control, or
text: A fixed effect is just a categorical control variable for the individual — mathematically identical to any other categorical control, and different only in why you added it — so you can estimate it by literally adding that control, or, far more commonly, by demeaning : subtract each individual's own average from everything and regress the leftovers. Fixed effects and adding a categorical control are the same thing mathematically . Only the research design reasoning differs. The model gets i,t subscripts (panel data) and an individual specific intercept , which is exactly what binary category indicators do. Estimation route 1: add the categorical control. Works — but you may have hundreds of thousands of individuals and therefore coefficients. Estimation route 2: demeaning. Subtract each individual's own mean from y and every x, then regress the leftovers. This is what fixed effects software actually does. Cluster your standard errors at the level of the fixed effect. Very common, and it makes sense. Interpretation is a normal regression interpretation — just say "within individual." Two way fixed effects adds a time fixed effect too. Easy to run, harder to interpret. The last episode was about why you'd use fixed effects. This one is about actually estimating one — and the crucial thing to hold onto is that you're just controlling for a variable . You've added control variables before, and there is nothing different about adding a control for individual. Individual is just a categorical control variable, and you already know how to add those. Fixed effects and a categorical control are mathematically the same thing. The only difference is why : you're adding this control not because you want to control for that thing specifically, but because you think it represents a bunch of other individual stuff you can't measure. Keeping that in mind clears up a lot of the apparent difficulty. The model and its notation Plain English. Extra subscripts to show panel data, and an intercept that varies by person. Precisely. A fixed effects model looks like a standard regression with a couple of differences in the subscripts: y with i,t subscripts — y varies over both individual i and time t . That's what tells you it's panel data: the same individuals measured in multiple periods. x and the error term also carry i,t subscripts. The intercept has its own i subscript — each individual gets their own intercept. Why an intercept and not another x? Because that's what a categorical control is . Add binary indicators for countries — Austria is 1 if you're Austria and 0 otherwise. That coefficient just says your intercept changes: if you're Austria, add Austria's coefficient to the intercept; if not, don't. So a set of category indicators is exactly the same as giving each individual their own intercept . Estimation route 1: add the categorical control Plain English. Just put it in the regression. Precisely. Literally no different from adding any categorical control. Add it to the model and you're done. Where it breaks. Normally a categorical control has four categories, or ten. With fixed effects you're controlling for individual , and with a lot of data that might be thousands or hundreds of thousands of individuals. You don't want to estimate a regression with hundreds of thousands of coefficients — at the very least it will be extremely slow. So while you can do it this way, it generally isn't the best approach. Estimation route 2: demeaning Plain English. Subtract each person's own average out of everything, then run a normal regression on what's left. Precisely. Take the average of your y and of all your x variables for each individual, and subtract that average out. And you've seen this before. It's exactly one of the methods of controlling for variables from Chapter 4: take what you can predict with your control variables and subtract the prediction out. When your control variable is individual , what's the best prediction of your outcome knowing only that it's you? Your own average. Anchor image. Measuring how much everyone grew this year rather than how tall they are. Subtract each person's baseline and the remaining numbers are comparable. Worked example. If you average four hours of exercise a week across all the years observed, then knowing only that it's you, the best prediction is four hours. Subtract that out and you get your demeaned values — the fact that this year you exercised one hour more than average, and that year one hour less. That's your within variation. Then simply regress your demeaned outcome on your demeaned predictors . That's it — and this is what most software specifically designed to estimate fixed effects is actually doing. Clustered standard errors Plain English. Cluster at the level of the fixed effect. Almost everyone does. Precisely. It's very common to estimate a fixed effects model with clustered standard errors , clustered at the level of the fixed effect. You'd expect the error terms to be more related within individual than between individuals, and clustering by individual is how you allow for that. Where it breaks. Not that you always have to — but it's very, very common, and it makes sense why. Interpretation Plain English. Same as any regression. Just add "within individual" to the sentence. Worked example. Regress life expectancy on a country's log GDP per capita , with country fixed effects. Coefficient: 9.769 . The standard reading: a one unit increase in log GDP per capita is associated with a 9.769 unit increase in life expectancy. Exactly as you'd interpret any regression — except that it's within country . Spelled out: comparing a given country across time, a year in which that country has a one unit higher log GDP per capita would be expected to have a 9.769 longer life expectancy than that same country in a different year with lower log GDP per capita. That's all. A regular regression interpretation, keeping in mind you're controlling for individual an

title: Random Effects
one: Fixed effects throws away **all** the between variation, which costs you statistical power and any hope of studying time-constant variables — so random effects instead **assumes the individual effects follow a distribution**, buying precisi
text: Fixed effects throws away all the between variation, which costs you statistical power and any hope of studying time constant variables — so random effects instead assumes the individual effects follow a distribution , buying precision back with an assumption, and its better variants let you keep the between variation and the within variation separately. Fixed effects works, but it discards a lot of interesting information . Cost 1: you can't study anything that's purely between — birthplace has no within variation, so it's gone. Cost 2: poor statistical power , because you threw out so much information. Random effects doesn't remove all the between variation. It constrains the individual effects to follow a distribution . Any assumption buys precision and risks being wrong. Plain random effects needs the individual effect to be unrelated to the controls and the treatment — usually false. Hierarchical linear modeling predicts where you sit in that distribution using measurable characteristics. Common — more common than fixed effects in many fields. Correlated random effects (the Mundlak estimator ) includes both the mean and the demeaned version of each variable, separating between from within. This chapter has been about the difference between between variation — Austria has different geography than England — and within variation — Austria's politics differ from one year to the next. Fixed effects controls away all the between variation and leaves you with the within, and it's effective at that. There are downsides. The two costs of fixed effects Plain English. You binned a lot of real information, and the bin had useful things in it. Precisely. Cost 1 — you can't study anything that's purely between. Want to know the effect of your birthplace? You can't use fixed effects to study that, because birthplace has no within variation. You threw out all the between variation, and anything interesting in there went with it. Cost 2 — poor statistical power. You're discarding a lot of information you actually have, and discarding information makes your estimates less precise. Anchor image. Throwing out the packaging to get at the contents — and finding the receipt was in the packaging. What random effects does Plain English. Don't delete the individual differences. Assume they follow a pattern. Precisely. Random effects tries to deal with the between variation without getting rid of all of it — improving statistical performance and letting you study some of what lives in the between variation, while still closing some of the back doors you care about. The mechanism: individuals really are different — Austria differs from England differs from France; you differ from me differs from our neighbour down the street. But instead of letting the individual effects be anything at all , random effects forces them to follow a distribution . You're willing to say something like: these effects follow a normal distribution . The trade. Any time in statistics that you make an assumption, you give yourself back some statistical power — and run the risk that if the assumption is wrong, your results are bad. By constraining the individual effects rather than letting them run free, you may not remove all the between variation, but you get precision back. Where it breaks. Doing just that doesn't always work well. For plain random effects to really close those between variation back doors, you have to assume the individual effect is unrelated to all the control variables and the treatment — which is usually not the case. Hierarchical linear modeling Plain English. Don't just assume a distribution — predict where each individual sits in it. Precisely. Hierarchical linear modeling is very common — probably more common than fixed effects in a lot of fields , though economics tends to prefer fixed effects. It says: we have these individual effects, we'll allow them to follow distributions — and we'll use measurable characteristics to predict where you are in that distribution. So there's an individual effect for you, and things like your geography , which can be measured, are used to predict where your individual effect sits. Why that helps. It gives more information about where you are on the distribution, so you understand your individual effect better — and crucially, it's now okay if your individual effect is related to geography , because you've incorporated that relationship into the estimation. That was exactly the assumption plain random effects couldn't survive. Anchor image. Not just "people vary in height" but "and taller parents predict taller children." Explaining the variation instead of merely allowing for it. Bonus. You don't have to stop at the individual effect. You can also model how the effect of one variable on another varies with individual characteristics — making it an alternative to interaction terms in a standard regression. Correlated random effects and the Mundlak estimator Plain English. Put both the individual's average and their deviations from it into the model. Precisely. Correlated random effects is the other route: again allow the individual random effect to be related to characteristics you can measure . The typical implementation is the Mundlak estimator . One variant is beautifully simple. Recall demeaning — take each individual's average and subtract it out. Here you do that, and use the demeaned variable in the model — but you also include the mean itself . So you have two versions of each variable : Version What it carries The mean The between variation The demeaned variable The within variation Put both in the model and you've separated the between and within variation, so you can use whatever mix of the two you want. Here's the between, here's the within — do what you like. Where this leaves you Plain English. Plain random effects blends between and within. The advanced versions hand you both, labelled. Precisely. Standard random effects doesn't get rid of all the between varia

title: Event Studies
one: Something happened at a moment in time; compare after to before — but you can't just **control for time**, because time perfectly determines treatment and controlling for it leaves no variation at all, so instead you use the pre-treatment p
text: Something happened at a moment in time; compare after to before — but you can't just control for time , because time perfectly determines treatment and controlling for it leaves no variation at all, so instead you use the pre treatment period to predict what the after would have looked like and measure the deviation. Event study: an event happens at a time, there's a before and an after, compare them. That's the whole design. These are canned designs — structures where, if your setting fits, you can expect to get a causal effect. One back door: time . Time determines whether you're before or after, and time also affects the outcome. You cannot control for time. Doing so removes all the variation in treatment — 0% treated before, 100% after — leaving nothing to study. Instead: predict what the post treatment period would have been , and compare the actual to that prediction. Three ways to predict: ignore the problem , extend a time trend , or use another observable variable the outcome used to track. Terminology is a mess — the same design has many names, and "event study" means different things in economics and finance. If you picked up a causal inference book based on what you'd heard about the field, you might be surprised this isn't Chapter 1. This is where the canned designs start — structures that, if your setting applies, let you expect to get a causal effect of interest. Fixed effects got partway there, but it's pretty rare that anyone thinks fixed effects alone identifies a causal effect. Controlling for variables works but is difficult — you have to figure out the whole causal diagram. Event studies are the very simplest of the canned designs. So simple you've probably done several without knowing. Dogs do event studies: whine, get fed, and learn that whining causes food. Simple doesn't mean it always works. The setting has to apply, and you have to do it correctly — which is what the chapter is about. A note on names Plain English. This design has many names, and its name means several things. Precisely. Because it's so simple, the same design goes by: Statistical process control Interrupted time series Before and after comparison Sometimes case control , though that usually means something else entirely And in the other direction, "event study" means different things in different fields: Field What "event study" usually means Economics Many events occurring to different people at different times, possibly with a control group Finance A single event at a single time, comparing before to after This chapter uses the finance sense. The diagram, and why you can't control for time Plain English. There's exactly one back door and it's time — and it's the one confounder you're forbidden from controlling for. Precisely. The treatment goes into effect at a particular time. Before that time you're untreated; after it you're treated. Whether you're treated is entirely determined by whether you're before or after the event. Time changes, so time affects whether you're currently before or after — and things also just change over time in general, so time affects the outcome too. dag node T 1 0 "Time" bad node X 0 1 "Treatment" treat node Y 2 1 "Outcome" out edge T X edge T Y edge X Y note 1 1.8 "one back door and you can't close it by controlling" You want the arrow from treatment to outcome. There's a single back door, through time. Time is measurable. So why not just control for it? Because time is far too good at determining treatment. Control for time and you predict the level of treatment before the event — which is zero , nobody was treated — and the level after, which is 100% , everyone was. Subtract those predictions out and there is no difference in treatment left to compare . You've removed all the variation in treatment, and there's nothing left to study. Anchor image. Adjusting for the light switch to study the lamp. The switch explains the lamp perfectly, so once you've adjusted for it there is no lamp left. The actual approach: predict the counterfactual Plain English. Use the before to guess what the after would have looked like, then see how far off reality was. Precisely. Use something about the before treatment period to make a prediction about what the post treatment period would have been like . Then look at how things actually turned out and compare: Actual vs prediction Conclusion Higher than predicted Positive treatment effect Lower than predicted Negative treatment effect Same as predicted Zero treatment effect This is different from controlling for time, because you are not removing all the variation in treatment — you're using pre treatment data to build a counterfactual for the post treatment period, and making your comparison against that. Worked example — two graphs. Graph one: a time series that jumps around but has no real trend — flat as you go along. The treatment goes into effect. What would you have expected without it? No trend before, so probably flat continuing. What you actually see: at the point of treatment the series jumps up , then fades back down toward what you'd have expected anyway. So: a strongly positive treatment effect right at the moment of treatment, fading out over time. Graph two: an upward trend — jumps day to day, but clearly climbing. You'd have expected the climb to continue. What you actually see: the outcome drops , against the trend. So: a negative treatment effect. In both cases you're comparing what you see to a counterfactual prediction . The three ways to build that prediction Plain English. Assume nothing changed, extend the trend, or borrow another variable's path. Approach 1 — ignore the problem. Simply compare after outcomes to before outcomes. Better now → positive effect. Worse now → negative effect. You didn't close the back door; you assumed there wasn't one. Sometimes that works. If you're in a situation where you're confident nothing would have changed from before to after — you look at the data and there's no trend

title: Interrupted Time Series
one: Fit a time trend before the event, fit another after, and look at where they meet — and if you **centre time at the event date**, the whole thing becomes a regression with an interaction term where the jump you care about is just **β₂**.
text: Fit a time trend before the event, fit another after, and look at where they meet — and if you centre time at the event date , the whole thing becomes a regression with an interaction term where the jump you care about is just β₂ . Fit a trend in the pre treatment period, fit a trend in the post treatment period, see where they line up at the cutoff. Mathematically quite similar to regression discontinuity , a few chapters ahead. Implement it with a regression on time , an after treatment indicator , and their interaction . Centre time so it equals zero at the event. Then the intercepts are the predictions at the cutoff, and the jump is β₂ . Pre treatment line: intercept β₀ , slope β₁ . Post treatment: intercept β₀ + β₂ , slope β₁ + β₃ . You can add polynomials — just interact them with the after indicator too. Two serious cautions: autocorrelation makes you over reject, and the result is very sensitive to functional form . Event studies compare what you saw to what you predicted you'd see. The big question is how do you build that prediction? Interrupted time series is probably the simplest way, and it's a good method when you think there's likely to be a consistent trend over time, then another consistent trend afterwards, and you're interested in the difference between the two trends . The method Plain English. Draw a line through the before, draw a line through the after, look at the gap where they meet. Precisely. First fit a time trend — perhaps by regression — in the pre treatment period. Then fit a time trend in the post treatment period. Then look at where those two trends meet up at the cutoff. Worked example of the shape. Suppose you have a declining trend beforehand, heading downward. Then you fit the post treatment trend and it starts up higher. You predicted the trend would have continued down to here , and instead you ended up at there — that's a jump . That's the name: a time trend going along, then the event interrupts it, and it moves on to something else. Anchor image. A pencil line drawn along a ruler, the ruler nudged, and the line continuing from a different place. The offset at the join is what you're measuring. Where it fits. This is, at least mathematically, quite similar to regression discontinuity , which arrives a few chapters later. Implementing it with a regression Plain English. Outcome on time, an after indicator, and their interaction — with one crucial recentring. Precisely. You could use advanced time series methodology to make a forecast, and honestly it's probably better if you do. But a simpler route is linear regression with an interaction term. Regress the outcome on: time — a continuous measure, maybe the week or year the observation was made after — a binary indicator for being in the post treatment period time × after — the interaction The crucial adjustment: take the time variable and subtract the cutoff date from it , so that time equals zero at the moment the event goes into effect . Now the interpretation is very straightforward: Period after Intercept Slope Pre treatment 0 β₀ β₁ Post treatment 1 β₀ + β₂ β₁ + β₃ In the untreated period, after is zero so the β₂ and β₃ terms fall away, leaving β₀ + β₁·t. Why centring matters so much. What you usually want is how things jumped at the cutoff — the immediate change at the moment treatment went into effect. And the intercept is the prediction at time zero . Since you just centred time so that zero is the event date, the prediction at the event is simply the intercept. So compare the intercept in the untreated period, β₀ , against the intercept in the treated period, β₀ + β₂ . The difference — the jump at the intercept, which is the jump at the event — is β₂ . β₂ is your treatment effect. Worked example: ambulance quality improvement Worked example. A study of a quality improvement programme for ambulances — a way of making ambulances coordinate information better. Does it improve their performance? The treatment went into place at a particular time, and the question is what changed about the effectiveness rating. Graphically, fit a straight line either side of the cutoff: Left of the cutoff (untreated): a straight line sloping upwards slightly — but only a little. Right of the cutoff (treated): an entirely separate line. What you see at the cutoff: no real jump in the prediction. Which suggests there wasn't an immediate effect of the policy going into place. But the slope gets steeper afterwards. So the reading is: no immediate effect, but the quality improvement programme seems to have made things better slowly, over time . Two cautions Caution 1 — you don't need a straight line, but functional form matters enormously. You can add polynomials quite easily — just add an interaction between your after indicator and all the polynomial terms, which lets you fit non straight shapes that your data may require. But this is an obvious downside of the approach: it's very sensitive to the choice of functional form. It isn't a willy nilly decision whether to use a linear form or a polynomial — it has big implications for what effect you find. Why: a curve makes its weirdest predictions near the edges . A straight line might predict one thing at the end; add a curve and the middle barely changes while the edge changes a lot. And the edge is exactly where you're looking — right at the point the event goes into effect. Anchor image. Extrapolating a curve is like pointing a laser. A tiny change in angle barely moves the beam nearby and moves it enormously at the far end. Caution 2 — autocorrelation, which you almost certainly have. Standard errors here are difficult, because you're likely to have autocorrelation in the error term — see the regression chapter. An observation in one period is likely correlated with the observation in the next. If you run an interrupted time series with a plain regression, no bells and whistles, no adjustment for autocorrelation, you tend to over reject the null . That is: you're

title: Event Studies in Finance
one: An event study only requires comparing what you observed against a predicted counterfactual — no straight line necessary — which is why finance, where effects are **immediate and then gone**, builds the prediction from the estimation-period
text: An event study only requires comparing what you observed against a predicted counterfactual — no straight line necessary — which is why finance, where effects are immediate and then gone , builds the prediction from the estimation period mean , from the market as a whole , or from an estimated relationship to the market, and calls the gap an abnormal return . An event study needs a counterfactual prediction. It does not need a straight line regression. Finance is full of events, and finance responds to the expectation of change — so effects arrive immediately . Two consequences: short horizon predictions are far more reliable , and effects fade at once , so fitting a post period trend line makes no sense. The process: pick an estimation period and an observation period , predict, compare, analyse. The gap between actual and predicted is the abnormal return . Three prediction methods: mean adjusted , market adjusted , and risk adjusted returns. None of it is actually specific to finance. The last episode estimated an event study by fitting a linear regression on the pre treatment side and comparing it to one from the post treatment side. You don't have to do that. The key component of an event study is that you're comparing what you observe in the post treatment period against some predicted counterfactual — a prediction of what you'd have seen without the treatment. That doesn't require any sort of straight line regression. And in finance, it's very common not to use one. Since finance is one of the most common places you'll see event studies applied, it's worth knowing how they do it. Why finance suits event studies so well Plain English. Lots of events, and the effects land instantly. Precisely. Finance has a lot of events : Google merged with something — what did that do to Google's stock? Apple unveiled the new iPhone — what did that do to Apple's? Something happens at a particular time and you expect an effect on an easily observable outcome. That applies in lots of fields. What's different about finance is that stock prices respond not to literal physical changes but to the expectation of change — so effects go into play immediately . Anchor image. A thermometer versus a barometer. One waits for the weather; the other reacts to the forecast. Worked contrast. Suppose you want the effect of an education programme that gives students better teachers, randomly assigned, on whether they graduate from college. Even if everyone's confident it works, you will not see any change in the number of degrees awarded for about ten years . Apple unveils the new iPhone and ten seconds later everyone can trade and bid the price up. The two consequences of immediacy Consequence 1 — short horizon predictions are much better. All these methods rest on predicting what would have happened absent the event, and those predictions are far stronger over a very short window. Forecasting how many college degrees there'll be in ten years without your policy is very hard. Forecasting Apple's stock price one minute from now , knowing what it is right now, is pretty easy. So the immediacy of the effect makes the assumptions much more plausible. If you predict Apple's price one minute out and then look one minute later, you can be fairly confident the change came from the announcement and not from some long term trend you failed to notice — there simply isn't time for other effects to sink in. The time back door gets closed convincingly. Consequence 2 — the effects fade right away. A corollary of immediacy: everything happens at once and then stops. Almost all of Apple's price growth happens on the first day after the announcement. Everyone thinks that's going to be good for Apple, the price should be up there — so why wait? They bid it up within minutes . You might see a huge effect today and none tomorrow. Where it breaks — and why this chapter exists. When the effect appears briefly and then goes away, fitting a whole time series line to the post treatment period doesn't make sense. You don't want a straight line. You want an effect that's there once and then gone. So you need a method that allows for that. The four step process Plain English. Choose your windows, predict, compare, analyse. Precisely. Throughout, assume you're looking at the effect of an event on the price of a stock — though it applies more broadly. Step 1 — pick an estimation period and an observation period. Roughly the pre treatment and post treatment periods, though not necessarily all your data. You might not use everything before; maybe you give yourself a little lag, or use only a narrow window right next to the treatment. And you might not follow things forever afterwards — maybe just a couple of days. The estimation period is what you use to estimate the general patterns; the observation period is where you make predictions from those patterns. Step 2 — make the predictions. Take the model estimated from the estimation period and predict what would occur in the observation period. Step 3 — compare, giving the abnormal return. You have your prediction, and you can see what actually happened. Worked example. You predict Apple's stock would go up by 3% . It actually went up by 6% . That extra 3 percentage points — beyond what you'd expect from the prior pattern — is the abnormal return . Above and beyond what you'd normally expect, hence abnormal . Step 4 — analyse the abnormal returns. You may have a different abnormal return for each day of the observation period, or one for each treated group. Calculate standard errors for them and see what the effect of treatment actually was. Where it breaks. None of this is structurally different from the last episode: an estimation period to fit a model, predictions in the post treatment period, and a comparison. The only real difference is that you're not using a straight line to do it. Prediction method 1: mean adjusted returns Plain English. Take the average from before and use it as your predic

title: Difference-in-Differences
one: Add a group that never got treated, watch how *they* changed over the same period, and treat that change as what would have happened to the treated group anyway — subtract it out, and what's left is the effect.
text: Add a group that never got treated, watch how they changed over the same period, and treat that change as what would have happened to the treated group anyway — subtract it out, and what's left is the effect. One of the most commonly applied designs, because the conditions are so easy to find. All you need: a treatment that goes into effect at a particular time and affects some people and not others . The problem it solves: you have a before/after comparison but don't trust any event study way of closing the time back door — and you can't control for time, because that removes the treatment entirely. The fix: bring in a control group that was never treated. This makes the diagram worse — a second back door through group — but it lets you close the time one. Logic: the control group's change over time is what would have happened to the treated group anyway. Subtract it. John Snow's 1850s cholera study is a difference in differences before the name existed. Difference in differences is commonly applied because it's very easy to find conditions in which it seems to apply. You don't need much: a treatment that goes into effect at a particular time, and a treatment that affects some people and not others. That's it. There are further assumptions required for it to work properly — but with that basic structure in place, you can probably get a difference in differences going, which is a large part of why it's so popular. A policy enacted in one country but not another? Difference in differences. A school makes a reform and a different school doesn't? Difference in differences. The problem being solved Plain English. You have a before and an after, and no way to trust the before and after comparison. Precisely. You have a treatment that went into effect at a particular time — an event, a change, a new policy implemented, an old one cancelled — with a before period and an after period. Event studies handle exactly this, comparing before to after with some adjustment, perhaps for a trend. But what if you don't believe any of those ways of closing the time back door? Worked example. A school introduces a teaching reform and you want to know whether it improves students' maths scores. You can look at how they were doing before and how they were doing after — but how do you tell it's the effect of the new policy and not that things would have changed anyway? Maybe there's no real way to use a time trend to account for that. And you can't just control for time. Control for the differences in time that determine treatment and there is literally no treatment variation left. Time and treatment go hand in hand — you can't control for one without controlling away the entire treatment. The fix: make the problem more complex Plain English. Bring in a second group that never got treated. Precisely. So far there's only the group that gets treated, observed before and after. Now add a group that did not get the treatment in either period — your control group , or comparison group. And this makes the causal diagram look worse: dag node T 0.9 0 "Time" bad node G 2.7 0 "Which group" bad node X 0 1.2 "Treatment" treat node Y 3.6 1.2 "Outcome" out edge T X edge T Y edge G X edge G Y edge X Y note 1.8 2 "two back doors now but the second one buys you the first" You still have the back door through time , and now there's an additional back door because which group you're in clearly determines whether you're treated — one group got treated, the other didn't — and there are likely baseline differences between those groups anyway. Things seem to have gotten worse. Except that the addition of this group is what lets you close the time back door. How the control group closes the time back door Plain English. Whatever happened to the untreated group is what would have happened to the treated group anyway. Precisely. The before/after comparison for the treated group contains two things at once : the effect of treatment, and whatever would have changed over time anyway. In fixed effects language, you're looking at that group's within variation — which solves some problems, but does not control for anything that changes over time . And that's exactly what you're worried about. Now look at the control group's within variation — how they changed from before to after. The placebo test intuition. If there were no time variation going on — if the time back door didn't matter, and only the treatment changed things — you'd expect the control group not to change at all . So if you look at the control group and see no change, that's a decent indication you can simply compare before and after for the treated group. And if the control group did change? Say maths scores at the school with no reform went up . That tells you something would have changed over time regardless. And it tells you roughly how much — that's probably the amount of change you'd have expected in the treated group too. Worked example. Group Change from before to after Control school (no reform) +5 points Treated school (reform) +8 points Difference of the differences +3 points ← the treatment effect Five of those eight points are probably what would have happened anyway, because that's what you saw in the untreated school. The extra three points is your effect. Anchor image. Two boats rising with the tide. One also has an engine. To find out what the engine did, measure how much more it rose than the other boat.
