That's the design. Take the difference in the treated group from before to after. Take the difference in the control group from before to after. Treat the second as what would have happened anyway, subtract it from the first — a difference between the differences — and whatever remains is the treatment effect. Worked example: John Snow and the London cholera epidemic Plain English. A water company moved its intake upstream, and Snow compared its customers' death rates against everyone else's — before the name "difference in differences" existed. Precisely. In

title: Parallel Trends
one: Difference-in-differences rests entirely on **parallel trends** — that the gap between treated and control would have stayed the same without treatment — and the maddening thing is that **you can never check it**, because it's a claim about
text: Difference in differences rests entirely on parallel trends — that the gap between treated and control would have stayed the same without treatment — and the maddening thing is that you can never check it , because it's a claim about something you don't observe. Parallel trends: the change over time you see in the control group is the change you'd have seen in the treated group had it not been treated. Stated precisely: the gap between the two groups would have stayed the same from before to after. Baseline differences are fine — the gap must be stable. You cannot check it , because it's about a counterfactual you never observe. Failure mode 1: the groups were naturally trending together or apart — the US and China. Failure mode 2: something else happened to the control group — a free lunch programme. Make it plausible three ways: know your context , pick similar groups , and check prior trends . Prior trends can look parallel (reassuring) or converging (a warning). This is the big assumption you have to make for difference in differences to work — and it's really annoying, because you can't actually check it . You always have to wonder whether the whole study rests on something untrue. What parallel trends is Plain English. Whatever happened to the control group over time is exactly what would have happened to the treated group. Precisely. Look at the treated group's before to after difference. What does it represent? The effect of treatment, plus whatever change would have occurred anyway from the mere passage of time — things change naturally even when no treatment goes into effect, and you don't want to confuse that with the effect. So you look at the control group, see how they changed over time, and assume that change is the change you'd have seen in the treated group had they not been treated . That's what lets you subtract it out and be left with the treatment effect. That assumption is parallel trends. It's hidden inside the subtraction, and if it's false — if the change the treated group would have experienced differs from the control group's — the whole thing falls apart. The more specific statement: you're assuming that the gap between the treated and control groups would have stayed the same from before to after , had the treatment not occurred. Anchor image. Two escalators side by side. It's fine that one starts higher. What you need is that they'd have risen at the same rate. Where it breaks — and this is the freeing part. It's okay that the treated and control groups are different in some way. Baseline differences are permitted. What must hold is that the amount by which they differ would have stayed the same. Failure mode 1: naturally converging or diverging Plain English. Two things already moving apart will keep moving apart, treatment or not. Worked example. You want the effect of a policy the United States implemented at a particular time — say a new tax policy — on economic growth. What can you use as a control? Let's use China . But China has already been growing much faster than the United States. You'd expect China to grow more than the US even if the policy hadn't changed , simply because that's what's been happening for decades. So you'd expect parallel trends to fail: the gap between them changes regardless of any policy. Failure mode 2: something else happened to the control group Plain English. Your control group had its own treatment, and now you're measuring two things. Worked example. A school introduces a teacher training reform, and you take a different school as control. But what if that other school introduced free school lunches at the same time? Now something changed over time for them too, and you can't tell the difference between the policy that happened here and the policy that happened there. The treated students didn't get a free lunch, so what you're seeing in the control group isn't just changes over time — it's changes over time plus the effect of a free lunch . You can't use it as a comparison group. Making parallel trends plausible Plain English. Three things you can do, none of which is proof. 1 — Know your context really well. You should be pretty certain, before running the analysis, that there's nothing else changing for the control group at the same time the treatment goes into effect. Did that comparison school district start a free lunch programme the same year the treated school started its teacher training reform? You want to be able to say no . 2 — Pick groups that are comparable. Choose groups similar enough overall that it isn't unrealistic to say they'd have evolved the same way. Note the subtlety: this is not actually a requirement of difference in differences — baseline differences are fine, as long as the gap stays constant. But it's far more plausible and believable that two very similar groups would have evolved the same way absent treatment. If you want to be advanced about it, you can use matching procedures to pick untreated comparison groups that look very similar to the treated group. 3 — Check prior trends. You can't observe what would have happened to the treated group without treatment — that's the whole difficulty — so you can't check parallel trends directly. But you can look at other pre treatment periods . Worked example. The teacher reform goes into effect in 2012 , and you have data on both schools from 2005 to 2011 . Look at their maths scores over that window. If both schools appear to be heading in the same direction — scores improving at both, at roughly the same rate — that's a plausible indication they'd have continued to improve at the same rate after treatment. Where it breaks. It's not a guarantee. You could still have something like the free lunch programme starting in 2012. But if you don't think that's what's going on, prior trends give you a decent sense that the two trends would have continued in the same way. Reading prior trends Plain English. Look only at the data left of the dashed line, an

title: Estimating Difference-in-Differences
one: Compute **four means** and subtract twice, or run a regression with a **treated × after interaction** where **β₃ is the answer**, or — for many groups and many periods — use a **two-way fixed effects** model; and all of these break the mome
text: Compute four means and subtract twice, or run a regression with a treated × after interaction where β₃ is the answer , or — for many groups and many periods — use a two way fixed effects model; and all of these break the moment different groups get treated at different times . Four means: treated after minus treated before, minus (control after minus control before). Done. Limitations: no standard errors, and it doesn't extend past two groups and two periods. Regression version: regress the outcome on treated group , after treatment , and their interaction . The four coefficients recover the four means, and β₃ is the effect . Two way fixed effects: a fixed effect for group , a fixed effect for time , and a variable for "currently being treated." Handles many groups and periods. Cluster standard errors by group. Critical: all of these require every treatment to begin at the same time . Staggered treatment breaks them. The concept is settled. Now you have to actually estimate it — which means comparing the treated group's before to after change against the control group's, and subtracting. The four means approach Plain English. Just literally do it. Four averages, two subtractions. Precisely. Calculate the average outcome after treatment for the treated group and the average before, and subtract. Do the same for the control group. Now you have a before to after difference for each, and you subtract one from the other. Worked example: organ donation and forced choice Plain English. California made you answer the organ donor question instead of letting you skip it, and donation rates fell. Precisely. In the United States, organ donation is an opt in system: when you get your driver's licence they ask whether you want to be an organ donor, and you check a box for yes or leave it. That produces much lower donation rates than countries with opt out systems, where you're assumed to be a donor unless you say otherwise. Forced choice is a variant in between. You still have to opt in, but you're required to respond — yes for yes, no for no . You can't simply ignore it. The question: does forced choice increase organ donation rates? Kessler and Roth looked at California , which adopted forced choice in July 2011 , and compared it against a set of other, comparable states that didn't. The graph. Four lines: a dashed line before and after the July 2011 cutoff for the control states, and a solid line before and after for California. There are three quarters of California donation data before treatment and three quarters after; average each to get the two solid segments. The estimation is: see how much the dashed line jumped at the cutoff, declare that's how much California would have jumped anyway, and push the lines down so the dashed segments meet — moving California's line down by the same amount. The four means , in organ donation rates: Group Before After Change Control (other states) 44.5 45.9 +1.4 California (treated) 27.1 26.3 −0.8 Difference in differences −2.2 The reasoning. Control group donation rates were going up over time — so absent any policy, California's probably would have gone up too, by around 1.4 percentage points. Instead they went down by 0.8. So the policy appears to have made things worse by 2.2 percentage points . The arithmetic is just the four means in the right order: (26.3 − 27.1) − (45.9 − 44.5) = (−0.8) − (1.4) = −2.2 . Where it breaks. Two obvious limitations. It's hard to get a standard error this way. And it's very difficult to go beyond two groups and two time periods — what if you have data from more periods, or more groups? The regression version Plain English. Outcome on treated, on after, and on their interaction. The interaction is the answer. Precisely. Set up a regression that estimates those four means for you. Regress the outcome on: an indicator for being in the treated group an indicator for being after treatment the interaction between the two — which is also just asking, are you currently being treated? The four coefficients map onto the four means: treated group after treatment Combination Which mean 0 0 β₀ Untreated group, before 1 0 β₀ + β₁ Treated group, before — so β₁ is the baseline difference 0 1 β₀ + β₂ Untreated group, after 1 1 β₀ + β₁ + β₂ + β₃ Treated group, after β₃ is your difference in differences effect — the regression does the calculation for you. Why. Ask what happens when after goes from 0 to 1: Untreated group: from β₀ to β₀ + β₂ — an increase of β₂ . That's the time effect. Treated group: from β₀ + β₁ to all four terms — an increase of β₂ + β₃ . That's the time effect and the treatment effect. Subtract the time effect β₂ and you're left with β₃ . Anchor image. Two receipts with a shared surcharge. Subtract the common line item and only the thing you actually bought remains. Where it breaks. That's a lot to hold at once — it's a fairly standard application of an interaction term, so reviewing Ep 32 helps as much as re reading this. Beyond two groups and two periods: two way fixed effects Plain English. Fixed effects for group and time, plus one variable saying "treated right now." Precisely. The organ donation study actually had six time periods — three quarters before and three after — and a number of different states. You might not want to lump them all into a single control and treatment, before and after; you might want their individual variation to show. And you already know the tool. You're looking at within variation — the before to after change for the treated group, and the before to after change for the control group. Within variation means fixed effects . So regress the outcome on: Term What it does α_g — fixed effect for group Ensures you're working within within variation α_t — fixed effect for time Subtracts out the shared time effects, estimated using the control group treated — 1 if you're in a treated group in a post treatment period Its coefficient β₁ is the effect of treatment That leaves β₁ as the treatment effe

title: Supporting Parallel Trends
one: You can't prove parallel trends, but you can make it credible three ways — **reason about the context**, **test whether prior trends were equal**, and run **placebo tests** on periods where nothing happened — while remembering that **prior
text: You can't prove parallel trends, but you can make it credible three ways — reason about the context , test whether prior trends were equal , and run placebo tests on periods where nothing happened — while remembering that prior trends are not parallel trends , however much the names suggest otherwise. Parallel trends can't be checked directly. These are gut checks that make it plausible. 1 · Think through the context. Is there any reason it should be violated? Anything else happening at the same time? 2 · Check prior trends. Regress the outcome on group fixed effects and a time trend allowed to differ by group . The difference in slopes is β₂ ; you want it indistinguishable from zero. Caveats: the trend needn't be linear ; with few pre periods you may lack the power to detect a real difference, so look at the graph too . Prior trends ≠ parallel trends. Prior trends can hold perfectly while parallel trends fails. 3 · Placebo tests. Drop the post treatment data, pretend an earlier date was the treatment, and estimate again. You should get zero . None of it is proof. Failing these makes parallel trends unlikely; passing them puts you on a good footing. Parallel trends is the assumption difference in differences lives on: that the before to after change in the control group is exactly the change the treated group would have had without treatment. You can't check it directly. But there are things you can check in the data that bear on whether it's likely to hold in your particular sample. 1 · Think through the context Plain English. Ask whether you have any reason to believe it's violated. Precisely. Do you think there's a reason parallel trends should fail here — something else happening at the same time? Do you have no reason to think the treatment and control groups would have evolved in different directions? This is reasoning rather than testing, and it comes first. 2 · Check prior trends Plain English. Look at the pre treatment periods and see whether the two groups were already moving at the same rate. Precisely. Look at the data from before treatment went into effect for anybody , and check whether the treated and control groups appear to be trending in the same direction at the same rate. Worked example. Looking at maths scores in a school that implemented a teacher training reform and a school that didn't, examine the years before the programme began and see whether scores were changing at the same rate in both. If they were, they'd plausibly have continued to — meaning any deviation from those trends afterwards would be the treatment itself, which is exactly how you pick out the effect. Requirement: you need multiple periods of pre treatment data , or there's no trend to measure. How to test it. A linear regression of the outcome on: a set of group indicators — fixed effects for group a time trend , allowed to differ by whether you're treated or control Note that time enters here as a linear variable , not as a set of fixed effects the way it did when estimating difference in differences. Group Time trend Control (group = 0) a one unit increase in time raises the outcome by β₁ Treated (group = 1) a one unit increase in time raises the outcome by β₁ + β₂ So the difference between the two slopes is β₂ . β₂ different from zero → the groups are trending differently over time. That's evidence against parallel trends. β₂ indistinguishable from zero → good news. Not proof, but suggestive evidence in your favour. Anchor image. Two runners in the laps before the starting gun. If they were already pulling apart before the race began, you can't credit the race for the gap. Three caveats on that test Caveat 1 — linear is a choice. The test above uses a linear time trend, with time entering by itself and no squared term. But estimate the trend in whatever way makes most sense: if your trend doesn't look linear, don't use a linear one. Add a polynomial , and interact that polynomial with group as well. Caveat 2 — statistical power. Failing to reject β₂ = 0 is suggestive evidence of similar trends, but check it properly. With only a few periods of pre treatment data you may simply lack the power to reject the null, so even if the time trends really do differ, you might not be able to show it with a significant test. Any time you have a test where you want a non significant result , you have to worry about whether you actually had the power to reject zero if it isn't zero. So also do a visual inspection — look at the graph and ask whether these two things appear to be trending together or apart. If you see a visual difference in trend, that's a reason to worry even without significance. Caveat 3 — prior trends are not parallel trends. These are terribly named things that sound like they're the same and are not. Parallel trends can fail even when prior trends are exactly equal. Worked example. A school introduces a teacher training reform; the comparison school introduces a free lunch programme at the same time. Look at the period before treatment and the two schools are trending in exactly the same way — prior trends hold . But parallel trends does not , because even if the teacher training programme had never happened, the free lunch programme would have made the two deviate from each other. 3 · Placebo tests Plain English. Pretend the treatment happened at a time when it didn't, and check you find nothing. Precisely. A placebo test is any test that, if your assumptions are correct, should give you an effect of zero — so a non zero result tells you something is missing from your model. Here you look for a difference in differences effect in a period where the treatment wasn't happening . Worked example. The teacher training reform went into effect in 2012 . 1. Drop all the 2012 and later data. 2. Use only 2008 through 2011 . 3. Pretend the treatment occurred in 2009. 4. Estimate difference in differences again. You should find zero . If you don't, that suggests prior trends — and possibly parallel

title: Dynamic Difference-in-Differences
one: Instead of one effect for "after", estimate a **separate difference-in-differences effect for every period** — by interacting the treated indicator with time fixed effects — which lets you watch the effect evolve and, crucially, gives you *
text: Instead of one effect for "after", estimate a separate difference in differences effect for every period — by interacting the treated indicator with time fixed effects — which lets you watch the effect evolve and, crucially, gives you pre treatment estimates that should all be zero . Don't lump all the afters together. Compare 2011 to 2013, 2011 to 2014, 2011 to 2015 — separately. Why: treatments often do nothing at first, or do a lot then fade. Forcing one effect across all periods is arbitrary. How: take the two way fixed effects model and interact the treated indicator with time fixed effects . You must omit one period as the reference — the baseline everything else is compared to. Graph the coefficients to see the effect's trajectory. The pre treatment coefficients are placebo tests. They should be zero. If they aren't, prior trends is violated. Economists call this an event study — nothing to do with Chapter 17's use of the term. Still requires everyone to be treated at the same time. So far, if you had multiple periods you added time fixed effects to account for different levels in different periods. You can go further. A terminology warning first. If you're an economist you may have heard this called an event study — but Chapter 17 used that term for something entirely different, an entire chapter that has nothing to do with this method. Here it's dynamic difference in differences . The idea Plain English. Why treat every post treatment period identically? Estimate one effect per period. Precisely. You have a control group, a treated group, and a before/after comparison. But rather than comparing all the befores to all the afters, let each after period have its own effect. Worked example. The teacher training reform goes into effect in 2012 . Basic difference in differences compares everything from 2008–2011 against everything from 2012–2020. Instead: Compare 2011 to 2013 — that's one difference in differences effect. Compare 2011 to 2014 — another. 2011 to 2015 , 2011 to 2016 , and so on. And do it with pre periods too: what's the "effect" in 2010 ? In 2009 , before the policy ever went into place? What that gives you: the effect of the policy one year after implementation, two years after , three years after, and so on. Why bother. There's no reason to force a single treatment effect. A lot of treatments — especially those going into effect at a particular time — take a while to do anything, or do a great deal at first and then fade out. Insisting the same effect applies in every period is a choice with nothing behind it. Anchor image. A photograph versus a film. One tells you it happened; the other tells you how. Estimating it Plain English. Take the two way fixed effects model and interact the treatment indicator with every year. Precisely. Start with the two way fixed effects model: fixed effects for group, fixed effects for time, and a treated indicator equal to 1 if you're in a treated group in a post treatment period, and 0 otherwise — either before treatment, or in the untreated group, or both. To make it dynamic, one change: take that treated indicator and interact it with a set of fixed effects for time periods. So you build a variable that's 1 if it's 2008 and you're in the treated group . Note the treatment hasn't happened yet — you still want that indicator. Then one for 2009. Then 2010. Skip 2011. Then 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015. Why you skip one. You need a reference group to compare everything against — a baseline year. That's why there's no β₀; the coefficients run from β₋₁ to β₁ . This model is really doing a two period difference in differences many times over, so it needs a baseline before period — and that's also what the other pre treatment periods get compared to. Reading the output. Each β is just like a coefficient from a regular two way fixed effects difference in differences. β₋₁ is the effect of treatment in the last period before treatment occurred; β₁ is the effect in the first period after. Graph them and you can see how the effect changes over time. Worked example: organ donation, dynamically Worked example. Back to Kessler and Roth — California changing how it asks people to become organ donors in 2011. The static model found a negative effect. But maybe that averaged together several different effects and came out negative when one of them was positive. Estimate the dynamic model and look. The reference period. The point at quarter 2 of 2011 has no confidence interval at all . That's the reference group — the baseline last pre treatment period, the rock everything else is compared to. The post treatment periods: Period Effect Q3 2011 Large negative, −0.02 , statistically significant — the confidence interval doesn't touch zero Q4 2011 About the same, perhaps slightly smaller Q1 2012 Back down again, and much less precise So an immediate negative drop in organ donation rates, and not much evolution afterwards — the effect went into place and stayed there. Where it breaks — the case for doing this at all. That isn't always what happens. Some treatments do nothing at first and slowly start to matter. Worked example. When gas prices jump, people start buying more fuel efficient cars. Compare a place where the gas price jumped a lot against one where it stayed relatively constant, and you'd expect the fleet to get more efficient — but it takes a while for people to buy new cars . So the effect right after treatment would be very small and would grow over time. If you forced a single post treatment effect, you would probably lose the effect that's actually there and miss it entirely. The pre treatment coefficients are placebo tests Plain English. You estimated an "effect of treatment" in years before any treatment. It had better be zero. Precisely. Look at quarter 4 of 2010 and quarter 1 of 2011 — both pre treatment periods, and yet you're estimating an effect of treatment for them. Why? Placebo tests. There shouldn't be any effect there. If there is, it i

title: Staggered Treatment Difference-in-Differences
one: When different groups get treated at different times, **two-way fixed effects quietly uses already-treated groups as controls** — and if treatment effects evolve at all, those "controls" deviate from trend and break parallel trends by thems
text: When different groups get treated at different times, two way fixed effects quietly uses already treated groups as controls — and if treatment effects evolve at all, those "controls" deviate from trend and break parallel trends by themselves, which is why you must estimate separate effects per cohort per period and aggregate afterwards. Staggered treatment / rollout design: groups treated at different times. School A in 2012, B in 2013, C never. Researchers long assumed two way fixed effects handled this. It doesn't. Why: there are two ways to show no change in treatment — never treated, or already treated . So two way fixed effects uses already treated units as controls . If effects evolve over time , those already treated units are deviating from trend — violating parallel trends by construction. This is a two way fixed effects problem, not a difference in differences problem. The fix, shared by all the new estimators: allow effects to differ by cohort and by period , then aggregate . Two approaches: Callaway and Sant'Anna (matching to pick eligible controls each period) and Wooldridge–Mundlak (a great many interaction terms). You cannot just use two way fixed effects. It can bias you terribly. This is the secret shame of econometrics — or at least the most recent one. A staggered treatment , or rollout design , is a difference in differences setup where multiple groups become treated at different times. Say three school districts: one gets the new teacher training programme in 2012 , another gets it in 2013 , and a third never gets it. The treatment was applied in a staggered fashion — rolled out to different places at different times. For a long time researchers thought you could just treat this as normal and use two way fixed effects. Which makes sense on the face of it: you still have group fixed effects for baseline differences, time fixed effects for shared changes over time, so the coefficient on treated should give you the effect. It does not. Why two way fixed effects fails Plain English. "No change in treatment status" includes people who were already treated — so they get used as controls. Precisely. Two way fixed effects strips out within variation via the group fixed effects, then compares within variation between places where the policy went into effect and places where no change was seen. But there are two different ways to see no change in your treatment level : 1. You weren't treated before, and you continue to not be treated. (A real control.) 2. You were already treated before, and you continue to be treated. (Not a control at all.) So two way fixed effects isn't just using the control group as a control group — it's also using anybody who has already been treated as a control group. Anchor image. Measuring how fast a car accelerates by comparing it to a parked car and to a car already doing 60. The second isn't stationary — it's just not changing gear. Where this might be survivable. Not necessarily fatal. Parallel trends could still hold: if the already treated group took its jump in outcomes and, after that jump, trends stayed the same, you'd be fine. But if treatment effects evolve over time, that's never true. Worked example. School A gets the teacher training programme in 2012 , school B in 2013 . Suppose the programme doesn't do anything right away — it takes a while to start working. You're the 2013 school. Two way fixed effects compares you against a "control group" that includes both the genuine never treated control and the 2012 school that's already treated . Right away, you see nothing. But in the year afterwards, as the 2012 school's effect starts kicking in, that already treated group starts deviating from trend. So whatever parallel trends you thought held, doesn't — the treatment effects becoming more or less effective over time is itself driving the violation. And everything falls apart. The important framing. This is really just a problem with two way fixed effects . It is not a conceptual problem with difference in differences. Difference in differences doesn't mind that you have several control groups, some becoming treated later and some earlier. It's that two way fixed effects forces already treated groups to act as though they're control groups when in fact they aren't . The fix, in general Plain English. Let every cohort have its own effect in every period, then average afterwards. Precisely. You can still use difference in differences with a staggered rollout — you just need a different estimation method. As of writing, a number of estimators have been proposed and it's a fertile area of research; there may or may not be a settled standard in future. But they share the same basic idea. The problem is that you're using the wrong control group and not allowing the effect to change over time . So fix both: 1. Allow the effect to differ by cohort. One effect for those who started being treated in 2012, a different one for those who started in 2013 — when you started being treated gets you your own treatment effect. 2. Allow the effect to differ by time period. The 2012 cohort gets one effect in 2013, another in 2014, another in 2015. The 2013 cohort gets its own set. 3. Then aggregate these individual effects back into a single number if you want one — average everything for an overall effect, or average across years since treatment to get "the effect one year after being treated, whenever that happened to be," two years after, and so on. Estimate a bunch of different effects, then aggregate them afterwards. Approach 1: Callaway and Sant'Anna Plain English. Pick a fresh, legitimate control group for each period using matching. Precisely. First it uses matching in each period . For 2012 it asks: who's the best control group for you? And obviously it will only use people who were not actually treated at the time — already treated people don't get to be picked as controls. Then it may use matching variables or some other process to pick a control g

title: Instrumental Variables
one: An instrumental variable is a randomized experiment you didn't run — you find something in the world that randomly pushes people into treatment, and then do **the exact opposite of controlling for a variable**: instead of subtracting out wh
text: An instrumental variable is a randomized experiment you didn't run — you find something in the world that randomly pushes people into treatment, and then do the exact opposite of controlling for a variable : instead of subtracting out what it predicts, you keep only what it predicts. Everything so far closed back doors. This chapter isolates the front door instead. The statue analogy: carve away marble until the shape emerges (closing back doors), or pour metal into a mould so only the right shape can come out (finding a front door). IV is a randomized controlled trial where you don't get to do the randomizing — you find randomization already in the world. Exogenous means as good as random in the system you're studying — no back doors to the outcome. Mechanically: a control variable is predict and subtract . An instrument is predict and keep . Two assumptions: relevance (the instrument really predicts treatment) and validity (the instrument really is exogenous). IV doesn't remove the back door problem. It moves it from a hard variable to an easier one. Every method so far — controlling for variables in regression, matching, fixed effects, event studies, difference in differences — is about finding things on the back door, measuring them, and cutting that variation out. That isn't the only approach. Back in the finding front doors chapter, the alternative was to skip closing back doors and simply isolate the front door path you want . That's what a randomized controlled experiment does: take some data, randomize within it, and in that data the only path that exists is the front door. You don't need to control anything away — you selected a part of the data in which there is only a front door. Anchor image — two ways to make a statue. You can take a big block of marble and carve away at it until you find the statue inside — whittling away back doors until only the variation with a causal interpretation is left. Or you can have a mould and pour molten iron into it, and the exact shape you want comes straight out — because you only allowed variation of that shape in the first place. That's finding a front door. What an instrumental variable is Plain English. A randomized experiment you didn't run. Precisely. Instrumental variables is basically the same idea as running a randomized controlled experiment, except you don't get to randomize or control it . You look for a form of randomization that exists in the broader world and then isolate it statistically — and that gives you your causal effect. Worked example: a trial where people don't comply Plain English. You randomized who should take the drug. People did what they liked. Use the assignment, not the taking. Worked example. You run a randomized controlled experiment assigning people to get a drug or not. But people don't always do what you tell them. Some assigned to take it don't — they forgot, or didn't feel like it. And some assigned not to take it find their own way to get it: you're running a trial about a drug that might cure my disease, I'll go get it myself. So now there are two different variables: Variable Random? Random assignment to get the drug or not Yes — you did it Whether you actually took the drug No — people chose whether to follow instructions The random assignment is a source of exogenous variation . Exogenous, in causal inference, means it's as good as random in the system you're looking at — no back doors from it to your outcome, so it might as well be random. Here it genuinely is random, but it doesn't have to be; there's just some outside randomizing pressure that leads you toward treatment or away from it. And the random assignment affects whether you took the drug. So random assignment is an instrumental variable for treatment . Treatment isn't completely randomly assigned — but a form of random assignment takes part in determining it. Isolate just that part , and you've found the random portion of assignment. Focus on that alone and you get the causal effect without worrying about back doors, because within the part of the data you've isolated, there's random assignment to look at. The mechanism: the opposite of controlling Plain English. Control variables subtract what they predict. Instruments keep it. Precisely. With a control variable , you have a back door — an alternative explanation for why treatment and outcome go together — so you see what you can predict with the control and subtract that out . With an instrumental variable , you have a source of random assignment to treatment, and that's the only variation you want. So you do the exact same operation backwards : see what you can predict with the instrument, and keep only that . Anchor image. A sieve used two ways. One time you keep what falls through; the other time you keep what stays behind. Worked example: the graphs Precisely. A continuous treatment x , a continuous outcome y , and an instrument z that for simplicity takes only two values, 0 and 1. Assume z is effectively randomized — a source of exogenous variation, completely unrelated to y except that it helps determine how much treatment x you get. The raw data. There's a relationship between x and y, but it's largely a random cloud. The z = 0 and z = 1 clusters don't really overlap: the hollow circles sit toward the bottom right, the solid ones toward the top left. So z is clearly related to x, and it appears related to y — but the assumption is that it's only related to y because it changes how much x you get . Step 1 — predict x using z. Get the average x for z = 1 and the average x for z = 0. Two predictions, one for each part of the data. Step 2 — keep only the prediction. Here's the departure from controlling. If you were controlling for z you'd subtract out everything you just predicted. Instead, subtract out everything you did not predict. All the variation in the data collapses down to just what z predicts — and since z has only two values, you get exactly two points: mean x when z =

title: Instrumental Variable Validity
one: Validity demands more than "no back doors" — the instrument must be **unrelated to the outcome except through the treatment**, meaning it can't have other front doors either — and it's so hard to defend that instruments once accepted, like
text: Validity demands more than "no back doors" — the instrument must be unrelated to the outcome except through the treatment , meaning it can't have other front doors either — and it's so hard to defend that instruments once accepted, like rainfall , have since fallen apart. Validity: the instrument is a source of random variation, at least once you've controlled for a few things. It's stronger than no back doors : the instrument must also cause nothing else that reaches the outcome. At any IV seminar, essentially every question is about whether you believe the instrument is valid. Decades ago people barely worried. Now they're far more sceptical — and previously accepted instruments have collapsed. Rainfall was the standard instrument for agricultural productivity. It's correlated across neighbours, and Sarsons found it predicts productivity even where dams make water access irrelevant. Validity is inherently a theoretical question about your diagram. Canonical designs — judge assignment, draft lotteries, twins, wind direction, Mendelian randomization — are the ones that have survived scrutiny. Validity is the assumption that the instrument has no back doors between it and the outcome — or that you can close them all — so you can treat it as a source of random variation in your treatment. That's what you're after: treatment might not always be randomly assigned, but if it's randomly assigned some of the time, you can isolate that portion and use just it, exactly as in a randomized trial where you don't use all the data, only the part you actually randomized in. Validity is stronger than "no back doors" Plain English. The instrument must reach the outcome only through treatment. Not around it, and not by any other route forward either. Precisely. Validity requires the instrument to be unrelated to the outcome except through the treatment . That is a stronger condition than merely having no back doors. The diagram you're claiming: dag node U 1.8 0 "The annoying thing" unobs node Z 0 1.2 "Randomization" treat node X 1.8 1.2 "Treatment" node Y 3.6 1.2 "Outcome" out edge U X edge U Y edge Z X edge X Y note 1.8 2 "Z reaches Y only through X no back doors, no other front doors" There's a treatment whose effect on an outcome you want. There's some back door for the treatment you can't control for — call it the annoying thing. And there's a source of random variation affecting whether, or how much, treatment gets assigned. You isolate the path randomization → treatment → outcome. Two requirements: 1. Nothing else between randomization and outcome. No back doors from the instrument to the outcome — or if there are, you must be able to control and close them so you get back to this diagram. 2. Randomization cannot cause anything else. If it does, then when you isolate the part of treatment determined by randomization, you'll also pick up a bit of that other thing. So it's stronger than "no back doors": there also can't be any other front doors from the instrument to the outcome except through the treatment. Anchor image. A pipe from a reservoir to a tank. Nothing may feed the pipe from elsewhere, and the pipe must not branch off to anywhere else along the way. Why this is genuinely hard Plain English. In the social sciences, nothing is unrelated to anything. Precisely. Go to any seminar where someone presents a paper using instrumental variables and pretty much all the questions will be about whether the instrument is actually valid . Can you really believe your chosen instrument is exogenous? It's a tough call, especially in the social sciences or anything involving people or complex systems. If everything is related to everything else, how can one variable happen to be sitting out there minding its own business, completely unrelated to everything important? Seems unlikely. Where standards have moved. Go back a couple of decades and you'll find people writing IV papers without worrying about this much at all — here's a variable, I'm going to pretend it's random — and not examining it further. These days people are a great deal more sceptical, and there are a number of instruments that were once considered fine and exogenous which people have come to doubt. Worked example: rainfall falls apart Plain English. Rain looks perfectly random. It isn't clean. Worked example. People used to use rainfall as an instrument for agricultural productivity all the time. Controlling for the part of the world you're in and perhaps the season, it seems kind of random whether it rains more or less in a given year — and that makes farms more or less productive. So you could study the effect of agricultural productivity on war and conflict, on economic growth, on urbanization, on whatever, and just use rainfall as the instrument. If rainfall really is exogenous — and surely it is? — you're set. Problem 1 — spatial correlation. Rainfall is correlated between people who live next to each other . If it's raining on your farm, it's probably raining on your neighbour's. That affects their outcomes, which affects the outcome you're looking at, in a way that isn't just through your own productivity. That's a back door through your neighbour's productivity. Problem 2 — the dams result. A paper by Heather Sarsons looks at the effect of rainfall on agricultural productivity and finds it is pretty much the same in regions that have dams for water irrigation and in ones that don't . That doesn't make sense given how you'd think rain affects agricultural productivity. If you have a dam, access to water isn't really the main constraint — and yet rainfall still heavily predicts agricultural productivity. Which suggests something else is going on , something you can't confidently call a source of exogenous variation. Validity is a theoretical question Plain English. No test settles it. You have to argue for the diagram. Precisely. Thinking about whether an instrument is valid is inherently theoretical . Can you make the claim that the c

title: Estimating Instrumental Variables
one: **Two-stage least squares** does exactly what the concept says — predict treatment from the instrument, then use **only that prediction** to predict the outcome — and whatever number comes out is a **local average treatment effect**, weight
text: Two stage least squares does exactly what the concept says — predict treatment from the instrument, then use only that prediction to predict the outcome — and whatever number comes out is a local average treatment effect , weighted toward whoever responds most strongly to your particular instrument. Two stage least squares: first stage predicts treatment from instrument + controls; second stage regresses the outcome on the predicted treatment. Using only the exogenous part of treatment means no omitted variable bias — random variation can't relate to the error term. Standard errors need adjusting, because the predicted treatment was estimated rather than observed. 2SLS is not the same thing as instrumental variables. It's one estimator among several. Generalized method of moments builds moment conditions from "the instrument is uncorrelated with the error term." Better under heteroskedasticity and with multiple instruments . The estimate is a local average treatment effect : never takers and always takers don't count , and stronger responders count more. Different instruments give different effects, because different people respond to them. You have the concept — a source of exogenous variation, used to narrow your view to the part of the data that's randomly assigned. Now you need to actually estimate it. Two stage least squares Plain English. Two regressions. Predict treatment from the instrument, then use only the prediction. Precisely. Probably the most common estimator, and conceptually the most direct. Two equations: First stage. Use your instrument z and whatever control variables w you have to predict your treatment x . Run that regression and get the predicted values, x̂ — which are only the part of x predicted by the instrument and the controls. That is your exogenous variation . Second stage. Where you'd normally regress y on x and w , instead regress y on x̂ and w. Because you've used only the part of x predicted by exogenous variation — the randomized part — there can be no omitted variable bias : in random variation, there's nothing that can be related to the error term. The coefficient β₁ on x̂ is your two stage least squares estimate of the effect of x on y. Anchor image. Filtering water and drinking only the filtrate. Whatever was dissolved in the rest never reaches you. Where it breaks — a detail. You need to adjust your standard errors to account for the fact that x̂ was estimated rather than being an actual value in the data. And a naming warning. Two stage least squares is one way of estimating an instrumental variables model. It is not the same thing as instrumental variables — an easy confusion, and you'll see people talk about the two as though they're identical when they aren't. Generalized method of moments Plain English. State what should be true in your data, then pick coefficients that make it true. Precisely. Generalized method of moments takes some calculations from your data, says what they should be equal to, and chooses coefficients such that those things are equal. The assumption it's built on. In the second stage equation — y on x and your controls, with an error term ε — you're assuming that your instrument is unrelated to that error term . (With plain ordinary least squares you'd assume your x is unrelated to the error term; in instrumental variables you assume your z is.) Take that assumption and build a set of moment conditions from it: your instrument should be uncorrelated with your residuals. Then choose your coefficients so that holds. Where it fits. You can use generalized method of moments even for ordinary linear regression with no instruments at all. The estimates work out almost exactly the same as two stage least squares. Two things make it better: 1. It performs considerably better under heteroskedasticity — smaller and more accurate standard errors. 2. It handles multiple instruments better. Nothing stops you using several sources of exogenous variation to predict your treatment, and generalized method of moments does a little better there. There are further options too — limited information maximum likelihood , among others. The point is that several different estimators get at the same idea. What treatment effect are you actually getting? Plain English. Only people the instrument moves are in your estimate — and the more it moves them, the more they count. Precisely. You're using only the variation in x predicted by the instrument. So you're only using people for whom the instrument does a good job of predicting their treatment . Worked example — the drug trial. Suppose you're deeply suspicious of medicine and won't take the drug regardless of assignment. You're a never taker , and whatever effect the drug would have had on you doesn't count — you drop out of the estimate. Similarly, if you're the kind of person who'd find a way to take the drug no matter what, you don't count either. The instrumental variables estimate reflects only people who don't take it when not assigned, and do take it when assigned. And beyond that — the stronger your response, the more you count. Worked example — exercise. Randomize telling some people "you should exercise more" and telling others nothing at all. How effective is that finger wagging? Maybe one person exercises one more hour a week because of it, and another is really responsive and exercises five more hours a week . However effective exercise is for each of them, you'll get far more of the five hour person's effect than the one hour person's . That's the local average treatment effect — the average effect, weighted more heavily toward people who respond more strongly to the instrument. If you're not among those who respond to the instrument, your treatment isn't randomly assigned, so you aren't counted. Which means different instruments give different answers. Suppose one instrument is finger wagging — "you should exercise more" — and another is showing you a picture of a really physically fi

title: Testing Instrumental Variables Assumptions
one: **Relevance** you can genuinely check — via the first stage F statistic, though the "F above 10" rule is wrong and the honest reading is Stock and Yogo's tables — while **validity** is inherently theoretical, and the two tests that claim to
text: Relevance you can genuinely check — via the first stage F statistic, though the "F above 10" rule is wrong and the honest reading is Stock and Yogo's tables — while validity is inherently theoretical, and the two tests that claim to check it both amount to treating a failure to reject a null as proof, which Nick doesn't buy. Conditions: relevance , validity , and also monotonicity — the instrument must push everyone's treatment probability in the same direction. Weak instruments are disastrous: little variation in predicted treatment means wildly noisy estimates. Worse, instrumental variables is biased in finite samples , and weak instruments let that bias dominate. First stage F statistic tests joint significance of the instruments. The bigger, the smaller the IV bias relative to OLS bias — but it never vanishes until F is enormous. F 10 is not how it works. Use the Stock and Yogo tables . Abandoning studies on a failed F test creates publication bias . Better: proceed and use Anderson–Rubin confidence intervals . Validity is theoretical . The Durbin–Wu–Hausman and over identification tests both rest on failing to reject a null. For instrumental variables to work you need relevance — the instrument genuinely predicts the treatment, because if it doesn't, isolating the random part of treatment leaves nothing to study — and validity — no open back door between instrument and outcome, and no front door from instrument to outcome except through the treatment you care about. Your instrument can't be causing two different treatments; that gets confusing. There are other assumptions too, such as monotonicity : the instrument must affect the probability of being treated in the same direction for everybody . That one isn't covered much here; the chapter has more. So: what can you actually check in the data? Relevance, and why weak instruments are so bad Plain English. If your instrument barely moves treatment, your estimate is noise. Precisely. Relevance is the easy one — just look at how closely related the instrument and treatment are. Strongly related, good. Weakly related, and you have a weak instrument problem , which can be very bad because it's very noisy. Why. Recall the components of an OLS standard error: the more variation there is in the predictor, the more precisely you can estimate. If x moves around a lot, it's easy to see whether y moves with it; if x barely moves, it's hard to tell. And in instrumental variables you're using only the part of x predicted by z . So if z predicts only a little variation in x, there's very little variation in your predicted values — very little movement down in the first stage — which makes it very hard to see what's happening up in the second. The result is extremely noisy estimates — estimates that can change by thousands of percentage points from sample to sample, which makes the whole approach worthless. Anchor image. Steering by a compass needle that barely moves. Every tiny wobble sends you somewhere completely different. Small sample bias Plain English. In any finite sample, "unrelated to the error term" isn't exactly true — and that introduces bias. Precisely. Instrumental variables has small sample bias : as an estimator it is genuinely biased in a finite sample . The reason is the assumption that your instrument is unrelated to the second stage error term. In the population that might literally be true — you may be able to say theoretically, with real confidence, that the true model has no back door from your instrument to the outcome. But in a small sample , by random chance, that relationship won't be exactly zero. And because it isn't exactly zero, a little bias creeps into the estimation. Where it breaks. Not a huge problem unless you have a really small sample or a really weak instrument. But the weaker the instrument, the more that bias takes up of what's going on in your estimate , and the less of it is the actual effect. The first stage F statistic Plain English. Test whether your instruments jointly predict treatment. Bigger is better. Precisely. The most common check. Look at the first stage and perform an F test of the joint significance of your instruments — whether the coefficient on your instrument is zero, or, with multiple instruments, whether all their coefficients are zero simultaneously. The bigger the F statistic, the better a job your instruments do of predicting treatment. Where the rule of thumb goes wrong. You may have heard that an F statistic of 10 or above means you're fine. That's not really how it works. What's actually true is that the bigger your F statistic, the smaller the instrumental variables bias is relative to the bias you'd get from plain ordinary least squares if you didn't correct for endogeneity at all. So a bigger F means instrumental variables looks better relative to that comparison — but the bias is still there . It doesn't simply go away, at least not until your F statistic is positively enormous, something like 105 . What to use instead. The Stock and Yogo tables , which show how big your F statistic needs to be to keep the small sample bias small enough relative to the OLS bias. At least a slightly more detailed version of the number you're looking for. The problem with the F statistic approach itself Plain English. Abandoning studies when the F test fails quietly selects for flukes. Precisely. What happens when you fail? You might conclude your instrument is too weak, drop the study, and go away. That introduces publication bias. Worked example. Suppose you have an instrument that's genuinely somewhat weak. Not the strongest — but you're fairly confident it's valid . Because it's weak, its measured strength varies a lot from sample to sample. Now two different researchers both use this instrument, and both apply an F statistic cutoff. One happens to draw a sample where the instrument looks stronger ; the other draws one where it looks weaker . The first proceeds with their study; the secon

title: Regression Discontinuity
one: Where a **smooth change** is followed by a **sudden one at a cutoff**, the people just on either side of that cutoff are essentially identical except that one got treated — so comparing within a narrow **bandwidth** closes every back door,
text: Where a smooth change is followed by a sudden one at a cutoff , the people just on either side of that cutoff are essentially identical except that one got treated — so comparing within a narrow bandwidth closes every back door, including ones you could never measure. The setup: a running variable changes smoothly, a cutoff produces a sudden jump, and a bandwidth narrows you to the comparable cases. San Diego to Tijuana: a few miles takes income from $85,000 to $50,000. A few feet takes it from $50,000 to $25,000. Why it works: $12,001 and $11,999 of income are basically the same people , but only one qualifies. This is a front door design, like instrumental variables — not a close the back doors design. You can't control for the running variable — it determines treatment entirely. You control for it everywhere except around the cutoff. Applies to geographic borders, close elections , and any policy with a threshold. Probably the most trusted design after randomized experiments — though Nick suspects future statistical power problems. Regression discontinuity is all about smooth changes followed by big changes at cutoffs . And the cutoff is where the attention goes. Worked example — the border. San Diego sits at the very southern end of California, and it's fairly wealthy: median income $85,000 , quite a bit above the national average. Downtown is roughly at the northern end, and as you move south toward Mexico, average incomes fall. By the time you reach San Ysidro , the neighbourhood just above the Mexican border, average incomes are more like $50,000 . Then you get out of your car, and there's the border, with all the stands of people guarding it. You walk a few feet — from San Ysidro into Tijuana — and incomes drop precipitously, to something like $25,000 . It took a few miles to fall from 85 to 50. It takes a few feet to fall from 50 to 25. A continuous change as you go further south, and then a big change all at once when you cross. That is a discontinuity in the relationship between how far south you are and how much income there is. The three pieces Plain English. Something that changes gradually, a line it crosses, and how close you stay to that line. Term What it is In the border example Running variable The continuous variable that changes slowly and determines when you pass the cutoff How far south you are Cutoff The point at which something changes abruptly The US–Mexico border Bandwidth A narrow range around the cutoff, giving a cleaner comparison Only people living very near the border Why the bandwidth matters. Going further south predicts lower income all by itself. So if you simply compare everyone in Mexico against everyone in the United States, one of those groups is further south than the other , and that influence contaminates the comparison. But focus on people living just on one side of the border against people just on the other, and they're about as south as each other — while being on different sides. Comparing within a narrow range around the border strips out the influence of "going south" and isolates the influence of "crossing the border." Anchor image. Two houses on opposite sides of a street that happens to be a county line. Same weather, same soil, same shops — different laws. Where it applies Plain English. Anywhere a threshold flips something on. Geographic borders — a literal national border, or one determining police jurisdiction, and therefore which kind of policing you're exposed to. Close elections — vote share moves smoothly. Party A is winning at 50 something percent, then 50.1%, then 50.01% — and a tiny change from 50.01% to 49.9% dramatically changes who won the election . So you can see the effect of having one politician rather than another. Threshold policies — say a poverty alleviation policy you qualify for by earning less than a set amount. To find its effect on your future chances of a job: as your income falls, that's related to your future prospects anyway. But right at the cutoff — at $12,001 you don't get it, at $11,999 you do. Why it identifies anything Plain English. At the cutoff, which side you land on is essentially random. Precisely. The causal diagram: dag node B 1.6 0 "Back door stuff" unobs node R 0 1.2 "Running variable" node C 1.6 1.2 "Above cutoff?" treat node X 3.2 1.2 "Treatment" node Y 4.8 1.2 "Outcome" out edge B R edge B Y edge R C edge C X edge X Y note 2.4 2 "narrow bandwidth: no variation in the running variable left" Whether you're treated is entirely determined by whether you're above or below the cutoff, which is determined by the running variable. And there are plenty of back door variables related to how far south you are — or, in the policy case, how much income you have. Almost certainly you couldn't control for them all. But this isn't a design where you control for things. Like instrumental variables, it's a design where you isolate a front door . By looking only at a bandwidth around the cutoff, you focus on the part of the data where there isn't really any variation in the running variable , and it becomes rather like being randomly assigned to one side or the other. Worked example. Yes, lots of back door stuff relates to income. But compare people earning $12,001 against people earning $11,999 . Those are basically the same people — the same on pretty much every characteristic you can imagine — except one got treated and the other didn't. It's almost random which of those you happen to earn in a given year: maybe one of them showed up to work fifteen minutes early one day, got an extra fifteen minutes on their paycheck, and that pushed them over the edge. So they're comparable, one was treated and one wasn't, and you close all the back doors — even for things you cannot measure. Why you can't just control for the running variable Plain English. It determines treatment perfectly, so controlling for it leaves nothing. Precisely. If you tried to control for the running variable directly, that wouldn't wo

title: Estimating Regression Discontinuity
one: Fit one line on each side of the cutoff, **centre the running variable so it equals zero there**, and the jump between the two intercepts — **β₂** — is your effect; then wrestle with bandwidth, functional form, and the fact that a **fuzzy**
text: Fit one line on each side of the cutoff, centre the running variable so it equals zero there , and the jump between the two intercepts — β₂ — is your effect; then wrestle with bandwidth, functional form, and the fact that a fuzzy cutoff turns the whole thing into instrumental variables. Regress the outcome on (running variable − cutoff) , a treated indicator, and their interaction . Two lines, one per side. Subtracting the cutoff makes the running variable zero there, so the intercepts become the predictions at the cutoff. Untreated side: intercept β₀ , slope β₁ . Treated side: intercept β₀ + β₂ , slope β₁ + β₃ . The effect is β₂ . Mathematically identical to interrupted time series — but a very different design, because "randomly on one side" is far more plausible than "randomly before or after." Bandwidth is a bias–variance trade off: narrow means credible but noisy, wide means precise but less comparable. Functional form matters enormously , because you're extrapolating to where each line hits the cutoff. A quadratic is usually as far as you go. Fuzzy regression discontinuity — where the cutoff only partly determines treatment — is solved with instrumental variables . You have the design. Now estimate it. And as ever, the research design and the estimation method are not the same thing — there are often many ways to estimate a given design, and the same estimation method can apply to different designs depending on context. The basic regression Plain English. One line each side, and the gap where they meet is the answer. Precisely. Regress your outcome y on: (running variable − cutoff) an indicator for being treated — being on the correct side of the cutoff the interaction between the two You're effectively estimating two lines : one linear regression to the left of the cutoff, a different one to the right. Side treated Intercept Slope Untreated 0 β₀ β₁ Treated 1 β₀ + β₂ β₁ + β₃ Why you subtract the cutoff Plain English. So that "zero" means "at the cutoff" — which makes the intercepts do the work for you. Precisely. Take your running variable — say the income that qualifies you for some relief programme — and subtract the cutoff. If the cutoff is $12,000 and you earn $12,005 , your centred running variable is 5 , because you're five above the cutoff. Now think back to the interpretation of an intercept: it's your prediction of the outcome when all other predictors are zero . So set the running variable to zero and the intercept is your prediction at zero — and by centring, zero is the cutoff . The intercept becomes your prediction at the cutoff , which is exactly the point where the two lines meet. Reading the graph. Fit one regression line on the left and a different one on the right. Where the left hand line hits the dashed cutoff line, that's the left intercept — β₀ . Where the right hand line hits it, that's the right intercept — β₀ + β₂ . So the jump at the cutoff, where the treatment goes into effect, is the change from one intercept to the other: β₂ is your regression discontinuity effect. Anchor image. Two roads approaching a river from opposite banks. What you're measuring is the height difference where each one reaches the water. It's mathematically the same as interrupted time series Plain English. Same maths, completely different assumption. Precisely. If you watched the event study videos, this is mathematically the exact same thing as the interrupted time series approach estimated with linear regression. And there's a reason: event studies and regression discontinuity are basically the same thing mathematically. In event studies the running variable is time ; in regression discontinuity it's something else. So why treat them as entirely separate designs? Because the assumptions you need are very different. It is a much different assumption to say that people just before and just after an event, within a narrow window, are basically comparable — versus people on one side of a cutoff or the other. The idea that you're randomly falling on one side or the other makes a lot more sense for running variables that aren't time. You can't really randomly be before or after. In general people consider regression discontinuity a lot more plausible than event studies for exactly that reason. Bandwidth and the power trade off Plain English. The whole design says only the cases near the cutoff are comparable — and then you went and used all the data. Precisely. The basic regression above used the entire range of the data to estimate its lines, which may not be what you want. So a common step is to narrow your range , dropping data far from the cutoff. Worked example. With the income cutoff at $12,000, you might decide people earning between $11,900 and $12,100 have incomes close enough to be comparable, limit the data to that, and fit your regression lines within it. The trade off — and this is the statistical power issue. Bandwidth Bias Precision Narrow Low — you genuinely believe in the identification Poor — you drop a lot of data, the sample gets very small, the estimate gets very noisy Wide Higher — you're comparing someone on $13,000 to someone on $10,000, and back door variables may return Better — more observations Widening asks whether $13,000 and $10,000 of income are really comparable. Maybe, maybe not — and at some point you start to lose belief in the identification. Choosing one. There are procedures called optimal bandwidth selection . Alternatively, just try a bunch of different bandwidths and see whether your results are stable as they widen and your estimates get more precise. If the estimate itself doesn't change much as you bring in more data, you might as well bring it in — it doesn't seem to be biasing you much. Functional form matters more than usual Plain English. You're extrapolating each line to the cutoff, so getting the shape wrong moves your answer directly. Precisely. You don't have to use a linear regression on either side. In the example graph the fit is

title: Adjustments to Regression Discontinuity
one: Two refinements soften the bandwidth and functional-form problems — **kernel weights**, which let distant observations count a little rather than not at all, and **local regression**, which fits a fresh line inside each small window and so
text: Two refinements soften the bandwidth and functional form problems — kernel weights , which let distant observations count a little rather than not at all, and local regression , which fits a fresh line inside each small window and so builds the bandwidth in automatically while letting the curve go anywhere. Both problems matter because you need the estimate at the cutoff to be as correct as possible. Weights: points closest to the cutoff count most, declining to zero further out — via a kernel function . Triangular and Epanechnikov kernels are the standard choices. Weights split the difference : more observations for precision, at reduced influence so they add less bias. Local regression: slide a window along the running variable, fitting a separate regression in each one. That builds the bandwidth in automatically and allows any shape of curve. Cost: less precision — and regression discontinuity is already precision poor. Bonus: within a small window you barely need polynomials, because zoom in far enough and everything looks linear . The basic estimator left two problems open. Bandwidth. How far outside the cutoff are you willing to look? Include a lot of data far from the cutoff and you defeat the purpose of the design, which was that people just around the cutoff are comparable apart from treatment. But limit the sample to just around the cutoff and you lose a great many observations, with real problems for statistical precision. Functional form. If the relationship between running variable and outcome is curvy, your line should be curvy too. Both are important, because you need to get your estimate at the cutoff as correct as possible. Weights Plain English. Don't let every observation count equally. The nearer the cutoff, the more it counts. Precisely. You don't have to let each point in your data count the same in estimation. Use weights so some observations have more influence on the fitted line and others less. The natural structure: points closest to the cutoff count most. If you're right next to the cutoff, the comparison is essentially unbiased — you can put someone right on the other side against you with no problem. As you move further away, you're less certain, then less certain still, and eventually you don't think those people are comparable at all. The kernel function puts exactly that idea into the analysis. A kernel is a function centred around zero , typically tallest at zero , descending as you move away until it finally reaches an actual weight of zero . Sit right at the cutoff and you get the biggest weight; move away and your weight declines until you're far enough out that you don't count at all. Kernel Shape Triangular Looks exactly like a triangle — straight line decline Epanechnikov A bit more curved The basic idea is the same for all of them: closer to the cutoff means a bigger weight, declining to zero as you move away. Anchor image. A dimmer switch instead of an on/off switch. The far away observations aren't excluded — they're just turned down. What it buys you. It lets you include a few more observations while reducing how much they influence your bias. Limit the data to a narrow strip right around the cutoff and there aren't many observations. Let everybody in and you may introduce bias you're unsure about. Weighting says: these people are okay, but only at a very reduced weight , and puts most of the weight on the ones you're really certain about. That's the point — splitting the difference between the additional precision of allowing more observations and the additional bias of allowing more observations. Best of both worlds, at least a little. Local regression Plain English. Don't fit one line per side. Slide a window along and fit a fresh line inside each one. Precisely. Instead of a single line on the left and a single line on the right, local regression moves along the running variable taking a little window of data at a time . In each window it fits a different regression — sometimes linear, sometimes polynomial — and produces a set of predictions just for the data in that narrow range. So the model can be curvy: here the slope looks flat, so put a flat line; move over a bit, it looks steeper, so put a steeper line; steeper again after that. The slope changes continuously . It builds the bandwidth in automatically. Think about what happens right next to the cutoff. You're using only observations in that little window to estimate the regression at that point — so you're automatically including a bandwidth, or a weight, depending on exactly how you construct the local regression. The trade off. This allows full curviness — the fitted line can take any shape, which fits nicely with what regression discontinuity is trying to do. But it is less precise than simply fitting a regression. Allowing more flexibility in statistics almost always costs precision. And that's a real problem, because with regression discontinuity you're already in a low precision zone . The bonus — and the best image in the chapter. By focusing on a small window at a time, you can use a more linear approach. You don't need a pile of polynomials to fit a very curvy line — and you probably don't want them, especially when using bandwidths. Because if you zoom in closely enough, pretty much everything looks linear. You could have the wildest curve imaginable, but look at just a tiny window and it's straight. Anchor image. That's why the horizon looks like an almost straight line. It's a very zoomed in picture of a round Earth — you've simply zoomed far enough into a tiny portion of it. Weighting is a way of excluding distant observations → Actually, it's the opposite: it lets you include them at reduced influence rather than dropping them entirely. The kernel choice determines whether the design works → Actually, triangular and Epanechnikov differ in shape, but all kernels do the same thing: most weight at the cutoff, declining to zero. Local regression is just a smoother looking l

title: Checking Regression Discontinuity Assumptions
one: Two assumptions can fail — that people either side of the cutoff are genuinely comparable, and that nobody is **manipulating** their running variable — and you check both by **running regression discontinuity again**: once on your control v
text: Two assumptions can fail — that people either side of the cutoff are genuinely comparable, and that nobody is manipulating their running variable — and you check both by running regression discontinuity again : once on your control variables, where you hope to find nothing, and once on the density of the running variable itself, where you hope you can't spot the cutoff at all. Regression discontinuity is not a free lunch. If the assumptions fail, so does the estimate. Assumption 1: people just either side of the cutoff are comparable. Check it by re running the design with your control variables as the outcome . A jump means either something else changes at the cutoff, or you got a bad draw. If something else changes, the design is broken. If it's bad luck, add a control variable . Assumption 2: nobody manipulates the running variable to get treated. Check it by binning the running variable and looking at the counts . You want to be unable to find the cutoff. Manipulation is very hard to fix. Two more running variable problems: granularity and heaping . You knew it was coming. Regression discontinuity is not a free lunch — there are assumptions required for it to work, and if they're wrong, your estimate is wrong too. It looks nice and clean, but there are assumptions hiding under the hood. Assumption 1: the two sides are comparable Plain English. People at $11,900 and $12,100 should be alike in every way except treatment. Precisely. Focus narrowly on the bandwidth and you believe you're picking out groups that are comparable except that some got treated. Pairing incomes of $11,900 against $12,100 isn't a huge difference, one group got the treatment and the other didn't, so you feel you can attribute the differences to the treatment. But what if the two groups actually look quite different? Worked example. Among people who just barely qualify for the income poverty alleviation programme — incomes from $12,000 down to $11,900 — 20% have a college degree . Among people just above the cutoff — $12,000 to $12,100 — 40% do. That's strange. You've claimed these groups are almost exactly the same except for treatment, and yet their college completion rates are very different. Two things it could mean: 1. Something else changes at the cutoff. Perhaps another policy helps you get a college degree if you earn slightly more than $12,000. Then you'd be confusing the effect of the income policy you care about with the effect of a college policy you don't. Your research design doesn't make sense. 2. Bad luck. By random sampling chance alone, you happened to draw a sample in which the two groups differ, even though on average they wouldn't. Neither is good. Checking it: run regression discontinuity again Plain English. Use your control variables as the outcome and hope you find nothing. Precisely. This is easy — and a lot of regression discontinuity tests work this way, which is rather neat: you just run regression discontinuity again. Take everything you'd consider a back door control variable and use it as the dependent variable . For the income programme: do the running variable and treatment predict your college completion rate ? College completion may change smoothly along the income distribution — but does it jump at the $12,000 cutoff? If it does, then it isn't just the treatment jumping at that point. Worked example: government transfers and political support Precisely. From a paper called Government Transfers and Political Support , on a poverty alleviation programme: earn just below a certain amount and the government sends you a check. The main question: does getting that check make you more supportive of the government? The main result: support for the government jumps quite a bit right at the cutoff . Just on the untreated side, support is considerably lower and changes smoothly along the income axis — and as soon as you dip below the dashed line, government support jumps immediately. Exactly as you'd expect if the treatment is responsible. The placebo test: produce the very same graph, but with covariates as the dependent variable instead of the outcome. Two were used, neither of which a government check should affect: Covariate What the graph shows Mean age in the household Changes smoothly over the income distribution, with slightly higher ages at slightly higher incomes — which makes sense, since you earn more as you get older. No jump at the cutoff. Mean education in the household Barely related to income in this narrow range at all, and no real jump at the cutoff . Things look pretty much the same either side of the dashed line in both cases — exactly what you want to see. A jump would indicate a difference across the cutoff you'd be at risk of confusing with the treatment. What to do if you do find a difference Plain English. There's nothing wrong with adding controls to a regression discontinuity specification, and it can even out sampling differences across the cutoff. The general principle. This kind of placebo test is a common and excellent way to check plausibility: think of parts of your data where the regression discontinuity effect should not be there, and see whether it is.
